title: Blueprint_Extraction
text:
1 Preface
Welcome to Volume 5: Blueprint Extraction — a comprehensive guide to reverse-engineering system blueprints from software repositories.

title: Blueprint_Extraction
text:
1.1 About This Volume
This volume presents an evidence-driven engineering methodology for extracting and constructing system blueprints from codebases. We provide a complete pipeline from signal classification and evidence modeling to blueprint generation, covering all aspects of architecture reverse engineering, including component discovery, interface extraction, data model analysis, and workflow identification.

title: Blueprint_Extraction
text:
1.2 How to Read This Book
This volume is organized into six main parts:

Part 0 establishes the context, contracts, and boundaries of the extraction framework
Part I introduces the evidence-driven engineering approach and hypothesis workflow
Part II develops the signal taxonomy and evidence object model (v0.1)
Part III presents the ontology mapping methodology from repositories to system graphs
Part IV describes blueprint draft generation through kernel slot filling and view generation
Part V covers validation, review protocols, and quality gates
Part VI addresses operating blueprint mining at scale

The volume concludes with a formal specification and practical appendices containing templates and case studies.

title: Blueprint_Extraction
text:
1.3 Prerequisites
Readers should have: - Basic understanding of software architecture concepts - Experience working with code repositories - Familiarity with system design principles

title: Blueprint_Extraction
text:
1.4 Conventions Used in This Book

Code examples use monospace font
Key terms are highlighted when first introduced
Confidence levels are explicitly marked (observed, inferred, unknown)
Evidence traces follow a standardized format

title: Blueprint_Extraction
text:
1.5 Feedback and Contributions
This is v0.1 of the Blueprint Extraction specification. We welcome feedback and contributions from the community.

Volume 5 — Blueprint Extraction
Last Updated: March 8, 2026

title: 2  Introduction
text:
2.1 The Extraction Challenge
Every software repository contains a system blueprint — but extracting it is notoriously difficult. A repository is a collection of facts (code, configuration, documentation), while a blueprint is an interpretation of how those facts constitute a coherent system. The gap between repository reality and system understanding is the core challenge this volume addresses.

title: 2  Introduction
text:
2.2 Why Evidence-Driven Extraction?
Traditional approaches to architecture understanding rely heavily on manual documentation or heuristic-based analysis. These methods suffer from several limitations:

Documentation decay: Architectural documentation quickly becomes outdated
Heuristic brittleness: Pattern-matching approaches fail at scale
Opacity violations: It’s unclear why specific conclusions were drawn
Auditability gaps: There’s no way to trace inferences back to source evidence

An evidence-driven approach addresses these challenges by making every inference explicit, traceable, and auditable.

title: 2  Introduction
text:
2.3 The Extraction Pipeline
This volume presents a complete extraction pipeline:
Repository → Signal Detection → Evidence Collection → 
Hypothesis Generation → Ontology Mapping → Blueprint Draft → 
Validation → Quality Gate → Usable Blueprint
Each stage produces auditable artifacts and confidence scores, enabling human reviewers to understand, verify, and correct the extraction process.

title: 2  Introduction
text:
2.4 Key Principles

2.4.1 1. Evidence Traceability
Every field in the extracted blueprint must be traceable to specific evidence in the repository. This ensures auditability and enables reviewers to validate inferences.


2.4.2 2. Confidence Semantics
Not all inferences are equally reliable. We explicitly model confidence levels (observed, inferred, unknown) to guide review prioritization and risk assessment.


2.4.3 3. Human-in-the-Loop
Automated extraction is powerful but imperfect. We design the pipeline with built-in review gates where human experts can correct, refine, and validate extraction results.


2.4.4 4. Scalability
The methodology scales from single repositories to large-scale mining operations, supporting incremental updates and cost control through cheap-pass/deep-pass strategies.

title: 2  Introduction
text:
2.5 What You’ll Learn
After reading this volume, you will understand:

How to classify signals that leak system structure from repositories
How to build an evidence object model that supports aggregation and conflict resolution
How to map repository artifacts to system ontology entities
How to generate blueprint drafts through kernel slot filling
How to validate, review, and quality-gate extraction results
How to operate extraction pipelines at scale

title: 2  Introduction
text:
2.6 Who Should Read This Book
This volume is for:

System architects who need to understand existing codebases
Platform engineers building developer tooling for architecture analysis
Researchers studying software architecture at scale
Technical leaders managing large software portfolios

title: 2  Introduction
text:
2.7 Scope and Boundaries
This volume focuses on:

Evidence-driven methodology (not specific tools or implementations)
Architecture-level extraction (not detailed code analysis)
General principles (not language-specific patterns)

We explicitly exclude:

Specific model implementations (ML or symbolic)
Language-binding analysis rules
Detailed code comprehension beyond architectural level
Security vulnerability detection

title: 2  Introduction
text:
2.8 The Evidence Mindset
The core shift this volume advocates is from “what is the architecture?” to “what evidence supports this architectural understanding?” This seemingly simple question transformation changes everything:

It makes architectural claims testable and refutable
It enables systematic improvement of extraction accuracy
It supports confidence-based risk assessment
It facilitates human-AI collaboration in architectural understanding

title: 2  Introduction
text:
2.9 Structure of This Volume
The remaining chapters are organized as follows:

Part 0 establishes contracts and boundaries
Part I introduces the evidence-driven approach
Part II develops signal taxonomy and evidence model
Part III presents ontology mapping techniques
Part IV describes blueprint generation
Part V covers validation and quality gates
Part VI addresses scale operations

Each part builds upon the previous ones, culminating in a complete specification (Chapter 21) and practical appendices.

title: 2  Introduction
text:
2.10 Getting Started
If you’re new to blueprint extraction, start with Part 0 to understand the scope and contracts, then proceed through Parts I-III to build foundational knowledge. Parts IV-VI can be read independently based on your specific interests.
For practitioners who want to dive straight into implementation, jump to Chapter 21 (the formal specification) and the appendices, then circle back to foundational chapters as needed.

title: 2  Introduction
text:
2.11 Acknowledgments
This methodology draws from decades of research in software architecture, reverse engineering, and program analysis. We stand on the shoulders of giants who have explored these challenging problems before us.

Let the evidence guide us to understanding.

title: 3  Why Extraction Is Hard: Repositories Are Facts, Blueprints Are Interpretations
text:
3.1 The Ontological Gap Between Code and System
A software repository contains facts.
A blueprint describes a system interpretation.
This fundamental distinction represents the central challenge of blueprint extraction.
Repositories record the artifacts that developers produce during implementation: source code, configuration files, dependency declarations, scripts, and documentation. These artifacts are concrete, observable, and verifiable. They represent the implementation state of a system at a specific point in time.
Blueprints, however, describe the structural understanding of that system. They capture architectural intent, component boundaries, responsibilities, capabilities, and interactions. Blueprints represent not what the code does, but what the system is—its essential structure independent of implementation details.
The two are related, but they are not equivalent. They operate at different levels of abstraction and serve different purposes.
Extraction therefore requires interpreting implementation artifacts as system structure, a process that demands methodological rigor, explicit traceability, and disciplined uncertainty management.
The difficulty of blueprint extraction stems from a fundamental ontological gap between how repositories organize information and how blueprints represent architecture.
A repository organizes information according to development practices and technical constraints. Its structure reflects decisions about file organization, build systems, version control, and developer workflows. These structures are pragmatic rather than architectural.
A blueprint organizes information according to system architecture semantics. Its structure reflects decisions about component decomposition, interface boundaries, data ownership, and workflow coordination. These structures are intentional rather than incidental.
This gap manifests in the types of questions each representation can answer:
Repositories answer implementation questions:
Blueprints answer architectural questions:
The repository contains fragments of evidence that hint at these architectural structures, but the structures themselves are not explicitly represented. They exist implicitly, distributed across multiple artifacts, encoded through conventions, and inferred from patterns.
Extraction therefore becomes a process of reconstructing architectural meaning from implementation traces—a reverse-engineering problem that requires sophisticated interpretation rather than simple translation.
crumbs: 3  Why Extraction Is Hard: Repositories Are Facts, Blueprints Are Interpretations

title: 3  Why Extraction Is Hard: Repositories Are Facts, Blueprints Are Interpretations
text:
What files exist in the codebase?
What dependencies are declared?
What functions call other functions?
What services are started at runtime?
What configuration values are set?



What is the goal of the system—what problem does it solve?
What capabilities does it provide to users or other systems?
What are the components and their respective responsibilities?
What interfaces expose the system to external actors?
What data is owned, maintained, and transformed by the system?
What workflows coordinate behavior across components?
crumbs: 3  Why Extraction Is Hard: Repositories Are Facts, Blueprints Are Interpretations

title: 3  Why Extraction Is Hard: Repositories Are Facts, Blueprints Are Interpretations
text:
3.2 Repositories Leak Structure Indirectly
Although repositories do not explicitly encode architecture, structural information inevitably “leaks” through conventions, patterns, and artifacts. These leaks provide the raw material for extraction.
Structural leaks appear in several forms:
Directory structures that suggest component boundaries:
services/
├── api/          # API service components
├── workers/      # Background processing components
├── pipelines/    # Workflow orchestration components
└── plugins/      # Extensibility components
Dependency graphs that reveal subsystem relationships and technology choices.
Configuration files that expose deployment topology, service dependencies, and integration endpoints.
Route definitions that reveal interface surfaces and capability exposure.
Database migrations that reveal data models, schema evolution, and data ownership.
Workflow definitions that reveal execution coordination and control flow.
Testing structures that reveal component interfaces, expected behaviors, and system boundaries.
Each of these artifacts provides signals—observable indicators that architectural structure may exist in a particular form. However, no single artifact is sufficient to reconstruct the complete blueprint. Each signal is weak on its own, ambiguous without context, and potentially misleading if interpreted in isolation.
Extraction must therefore combine multiple weak signals into coherent architectural hypotheses, then validate those hypotheses through evidence aggregation and systematic review.
crumbs: 3  Why Extraction Is Hard: Repositories Are Facts, Blueprints Are Interpretations

title: 3  Why Extraction Is Hard: Repositories Are Facts, Blueprints Are Interpretations
text:
3.3 Extraction as Interpretation Under Uncertainty
Because repositories do not directly encode architectural semantics, extraction inevitably involves interpretation under uncertainty. The extractor must bridge the gap between observable artifacts and architectural meaning while acknowledging the limitations of available information.
Three classes of uncertainty commonly appear in extraction:
First, missing information.
Some architectural elements exist only outside the repository or are only partially represented:

Operational constraints: Runtime policies, scaling requirements, performance SLAs
Runtime environments: Container orchestration, service discovery, infrastructure topology
Organizational ownership: Team responsibilities, governance structures, compliance requirements
External integrations: Third-party services, API dependencies, network boundaries

These elements are critical to understanding the complete system but may be documented in external systems, encoded in infrastructure-as-code repositories, or maintained through operational practices rather than explicit artifacts.
Second, implicit conventions.
Architectural intent may be encoded indirectly through naming patterns, framework conventions, or organizational standards:

Directory naming suggesting component boundaries (e.g., services/, workers/, plugins/)
Module naming suggesting functionality (e.g., auth/, payment/, notification/)
Framework conventions implying interfaces (e.g., controller patterns, middleware hooks)
Configuration patterns suggesting topology (e.g., service naming, environment variable conventions)

These conventions are powerful signals but fragile—they may vary across teams, evolve over time, or be inconsistently applied.
Third, ambiguous evidence.
A single artifact may admit multiple architectural interpretations:

A directory named services/ might represent independent deployable microservices or merely logical modules within a monolithic application
A message queue dependency might indicate asynchronous processing or merely message transport
A scheduler configuration might indicate time-based workflows or merely periodic maintenance tasks
Configuration references to other services might indicate runtime dependencies or merely potential integrations

The extractor must therefore treat architectural reconstruction as hypothesis formation rather than direct observation, explicitly representing uncertainty and maintaining multiple plausible interpretations when evidence is insufficient to resolve ambiguity.
crumbs: 3  Why Extraction Is Hard: Repositories Are Facts, Blueprints Are Interpretations

title: 3  Why Extraction Is Hard: Repositories Are Facts, Blueprints Are Interpretations
text:
3.4 The Evidence Requirement
To manage interpretive uncertainty systematically, blueprint extraction adopts a strict disciplinary principle:
Every extracted architectural element must be traceable to evidence.
This principle is not optional—it is the foundation upon which reliable extraction rests. An extracted component, interface, workflow, or data entity must reference concrete repository artifacts that justify its existence, structure, and relationships.
Evidence can take many forms:

Source files: Code implementing component behavior or interface handlers
Configuration definitions: Files defining service dependencies, routing, or infrastructure
Dependency declarations: Manifests declaring required libraries or frameworks
Schema migrations: Files defining data structures and their evolution
Deployment scripts: Code describing how components are packaged and executed
Runtime specifications: Manifests defining execution environments and resource requirements
Documentation fragments: Text describing system capabilities or architectural decisions

This traceability serves multiple purposes:

Auditability: Reviewers can verify that architectural claims are grounded in actual artifacts
Reproducibility: The same extraction process applied to the same repository yields consistent results
Debugging: When extraction produces incorrect results, the evidence trail helps identify which signals led to the error
Confidence assessment: The strength and quantity of evidence support confidence calculations
Evolution tracking: As repositories change, evidence traces help identify which architectural elements have changed

Without evidence, architectural claims become speculation. With evidence, extraction becomes a verifiable engineering discipline.
crumbs: 3  Why Extraction Is Hard: Repositories Are Facts, Blueprints Are Interpretations

title: 3  Why Extraction Is Hard: Repositories Are Facts, Blueprints Are Interpretations
section: 3.5 Why Naïve Static Analysis Is Not Enough
text:
3.5 Why Naïve Static Analysis Is Not Enough
Traditional reverse-engineering tools often rely heavily on static analysis techniques such as parsing, control flow analysis, and dependency tracking. These tools can generate call graphs, class hierarchies, module maps, and other structural representations.
While useful for understanding code organization, such outputs rarely correspond to meaningful system architecture. The problem is not that static analysis produces incorrect information, but that it produces implementation-level information rather than architectural-level information.
Architecture involves semantic structure, not merely structural connectivity:

A call graph reveals which functions call which other functions, but not whether those functions represent capability boundaries or merely internal implementation details
A dependency graph reveals which modules depend on which other modules, but not which dependencies represent externally visible interfaces versus internal implementation coupling
A module graph reveals file organization, but not whether modules correspond to architectural components or merely logical code organization
A class hierarchy reveals inheritance relationships, but not whether those relationships represent architectural abstractions or implementation convenience

Blueprint extraction therefore requires combining static analysis with semantic interpretation and evidence aggregation. Static analysis provides the raw material—the structural skeleton of the codebase—but interpretation, context, and architectural knowledge are required to transform that skeleton into meaningful architectural understanding.
This is why extraction cannot be reduced to a purely algorithmic problem. It requires domain knowledge, pattern recognition, and judgment—capabilities that humans provide naturally but that machines must approximate through explicit rules, confidence modeling, and systematic review.
crumbs: 3  Why Extraction Is Hard: Repositories Are Facts, Blueprints Are Interpretations

title: 3  Why Extraction Is Hard: Repositories Are Facts, Blueprints Are Interpretations
text:
3.6 Extraction as an Engineering Pipeline
Because extraction requires interpretation, uncertainty handling, and evidence management, it cannot be treated as a single algorithm or a one-time transformation. Instead, blueprint extraction is designed as an engineering pipeline—a structured, multi-stage process that progressively converts raw repository artifacts into structured architectural knowledge.
The pipeline operates through the following conceptual stages:
Repository Artifacts
        ↓
Signal Detection
        ↓
Hypothesis Generation
        ↓
Evidence Collection
        ↓
Ontology Mapping
        ↓
Blueprint Draft Generation
        ↓
Validation
        ↓
Human Review
        ↓
Approved Blueprint
Each stage serves a specific purpose:

Signal Detection: Identify observable artifacts that may indicate architectural structure
Hypothesis Generation: Formulate provisional interpretations of those signals in architectural terms
Evidence Collection: Gather concrete artifacts that support or refute each hypothesis
Ontology Mapping: Translate validated evidence into canonical architectural entities and relationships
Blueprint Draft Generation: Assemble mapped entities into the Blueprint Kernel structure
Validation: Check structural correctness, reference integrity, and consistency
Human Review: Validate interpretations, resolve ambiguities, and approve the blueprint

This pipeline discipline ensures that extraction remains:

Auditable: Every stage produces traceable outputs that can be inspected
Scalable: Stages can be automated, parallelized, and applied to many repositories
Improvable: Each stage can be enhanced independently without disrupting the overall process
Compatible with automation: Machine learning, rule systems, and pattern recognition can be integrated at appropriate stages
Human-in-the-loop: Critical validation and interpretation tasks remain under human control
crumbs: 3  Why Extraction Is Hard: Repositories Are Facts, Blueprints Are Interpretations

title: 3  Why Extraction Is Hard: Repositories Are Facts, Blueprints Are Interpretations
text:
3.7 The Core Principle
The central principle of blueprint extraction can therefore be stated as follows:

Repositories contain implementation facts. Blueprints represent architectural interpretations grounded in evidence.

Extraction is the systematic process that connects these two domains. It transforms implementation artifacts into computable architectural knowledge through disciplined interpretation, evidence aggregation, and systematic validation.
This transformation enables repositories to become inputs for:

Blueprint libraries: Curated collections of architectural representations
Architecture datasets: Training data for architecture analysis tools
Architecture intelligence systems: Automated systems that reason about system structure at scale
Pattern discovery: Mining repositories for reusable architectural patterns
Evolution tracking: Understanding how architectures change over time

The remainder of this volume details how this transformation is accomplished—methodologically, technically, and operationally—while maintaining the discipline of evidence-driven interpretation at every stage.
crumbs: 3  Why Extraction Is Hard: Repositories Are Facts, Blueprints Are Interpretations

title: 3  Why Extraction Is Hard: Repositories Are Facts, Blueprints Are Interpretations
text:
3.8 Chapter Summary

Repositories and blueprints operate at different levels of abstraction: implementation facts versus architectural interpretation
The ontological gap between code and system requires reconstruction rather than direct translation
Repositories leak structural information through signals, but each signal is weak and ambiguous
Extraction involves three classes of uncertainty: missing information, implicit conventions, and ambiguous evidence
Every architectural element must be traceable to concrete evidence to ensure auditability
Naïve static analysis provides implementation-level information but not architectural understanding
Extraction requires a structured engineering pipeline with explicit stages for signals, hypotheses, evidence, mapping, validation, and review
The core principle: repositories contain facts, blueprints represent evidence-backed interpretations

Next Chapter: Chapter 2 defines the deliverables of this volume—the complete extraction framework that makes architectural interpretation reproducible, auditable, and scalable.
crumbs: 3  Why Extraction Is Hard: Repositories Are Facts, Blueprints Are Interpretations

title: 4  Deliverables of This Volume
text:
4.1 Extraction Pipeline
Blueprint extraction is not defined by a single algorithm, tool, or technique.
It requires a structured engineering discipline that makes architectural interpretation reproducible, auditable, and scalable.
This volume therefore delivers a complete extraction framework rather than an isolated solution. The framework defines how extraction should be structured, not how a particular tool must be implemented. It provides the conceptual foundation, technical specifications, and operational discipline required to build and operate extraction systems.
The framework consists of six core elements:
Pipeline — The staged process for transforming repositories into blueprints
Signals — Observable indicators that architectural structure may exist
Evidence — Traceable justification for every architectural claim
Mapping — Rules for translating artifacts into ontology entities
Confidence — Explicit representation of uncertainty in extracted elements
Review Gate — Human validation ensuring architectural reliability
Together, these elements define the operational architecture of blueprint extraction—a systematic, evidence-driven approach to reconstructing system architecture from repositories.
Extraction is organized as a multi-stage pipeline that progressively transforms repository artifacts into structured blueprint representations. This pipeline design is essential for several reasons:
The pipeline transforms artifacts through several conceptual stages:
Each stage has clearly defined responsibilities:
Each stage has defined inputs and outputs:
The pipeline ensures that extraction can be executed repeatedly across many repositories while maintaining consistent methodology, traceable outputs, and auditable processes.

title: 4  Deliverables of This Volume
text:
4.2 Signals
Signals are the first layer of information used in blueprint extraction. They are observable structural indicators extracted from repositories that suggest architectural structure may exist in a particular form.
Signal characteristics:

Observable: Signals can be detected through automated scanning of repository artifacts
Indirect: Signals do not directly define architecture—they hint at structure
Weak: No single signal is sufficient to determine architectural structure
Ambiguous: The same signal may support multiple architectural interpretations
Diverse: Signals originate from many types of repository artifacts

Signal sources include:

Repository directory structures: Module organization, service boundaries, plugin directories
Dependency declarations: Package manifests, library requirements, framework references
Application entrypoints: Server initialization, CLI bootstrap, worker startup commands
Configuration files: Service configuration, routing tables, environment variables
Deployment scripts: Container definitions, infrastructure manifests, orchestration specs
API route definitions: HTTP routes, RPC services, endpoint handlers
Database migrations: Schema definitions, table structures, evolution history
Workflow definitions: DAG specifications, pipeline configurations, task orchestration
CI/CD pipelines: Build processes, deployment automation, quality checks
Observability hooks: Logging configuration, metrics instrumentation, tracing setup

Signal role in extraction:
A signal does not directly define architecture. Instead, it indicates that a particular architectural element may exist and triggers the formation of an architectural hypothesis. For example:

A directory named workers/ signals that background processing components may exist
Route definitions signal that API interfaces may exist
Migration files signal that data entities may exist
DAG specifications signal that workflows may exist

Signals therefore act as the starting points of architectural hypotheses. They provide the initial observations that drive the extraction process forward. Without signals, extraction would have no basis for inference; with signals alone, extraction would lack validation. The pipeline must therefore progress from signals to hypotheses to evidence to validated architecture.

title: 4  Deliverables of This Volume
text:
4.3 Evidence
Evidence is the traceable basis for every architectural claim made during extraction. Unlike signals, which are often weak indicators, evidence provides verifiable justification for blueprint elements.
Evidence object structure:
Each evidence object captures a precise reference to a repository artifact:

Source: The repository artifact (file, URL, or location)
Location: The exact position within the artifact (line range, JSON path, object identifier)
Excerpt: The relevant content fragment from the artifact
Type: The classification of the artifact (code, config, schema, deploy, documentation)
Strength: How directly the artifact supports the architectural interpretation (strong, moderate, weak)

Evidence characteristics:

Traceable: Every evidence object points to a specific, locatable repository artifact
Verifiable: Reviewers can inspect the original artifact to verify the claim
Fragmentary: Evidence objects capture only the relevant portion of artifacts
Multiple: Multiple evidence objects may support a single architectural element
Conflicting: Evidence may contradict itself, requiring resolution

Evidence aggregation:
A single architectural element is rarely supported by a single artifact. Instead, architectural interpretation typically emerges from multiple independent signals:

A worker component might be supported by: a worker directory, queue dependencies, scheduler configuration, and deployment manifests
An API interface might be supported by: route definitions, controller implementations, OpenAPI specs, and test suites
A data entity might be supported by: migration files, ORM models, schema definitions, and access patterns

Extraction aggregates these evidence objects into evidence sets associated with each architectural element. Aggregation serves two purposes:

Increased confidence: Multiple consistent signals strengthen confidence in the interpretation
Improved auditability: The complete evidence basis for each element is visible to reviewers

Evidence conflict resolution:
Repositories may contain conflicting signals:

Documentation may describe a component that no longer exists
Configuration files may reference deprecated services
Directory names may suggest structures that differ from deployment topology

The extraction framework preserves conflicting evidence rather than discarding it. Conflict resolution occurs at the mapping or review stage, not during evidence collection. This ensures that architectural interpretation remains transparent and reviewable.
Evidence traceability:
The fundamental requirement is that every blueprint element can be audited back to repository artifacts. This traceability ensures that:

Architectural claims are verifiable rather than speculative
Errors can be debugged by examining the evidence trail
Blueprints can be updated as repositories evolve
Reviewers can validate extraction decisions systematically

title: 4  Deliverables of This Volume
text:
4.4 Mapping
Mapping defines the rules that translate repository-derived evidence into system ontology elements. This translation is essential because:

Implementation differs from architecture: Repository artifacts reflect implementation choices, not architectural structure
Terminology varies: Different repositories use different names for the same architectural concepts
Abstraction levels differ: Evidence exists at the code or configuration level; blueprints operate at the architectural level
Consistency required: Blueprint datasets require canonical representations that enable comparison and analysis

Mapping targets:
Mapping translates repository artifacts into canonical ontology elements:

Components: Structural units of responsibility (services, workers, schedulers, plugins)
Interfaces: Interaction surfaces (APIs, events, CLI commands)
Data Entities: Persistent state structures (tables, collections, documents)
Workflows: Execution coordination structures (pipelines, DAGs, orchestrations)
Capabilities: Functional abilities provided by the system
Constraints: Limitations or guarantees affecting the architecture

Mapping examples:

HTTP route definitions map to Interface entities
Migration files map to Data entities
Scheduled tasks map to Workflow elements
Queue consumer implementations map to Component entities
CLI command definitions map to Interface entities

Mapping requirements:
Mapping rules must satisfy several requirements:

Interpretability: Rules must be explainable and reviewable, not opaque heuristics
Determinism: Where possible, the same input should produce the same output
Explicitness: Rules should be documented rather than implicit
Consistency: Rules should apply consistently across repositories and ecosystems
Traceability: Mappings should reference the evidence objects that triggered them

Mapping output:
The mapping process produces two core structures:

Entity graph: Nodes representing architectural entities (components, interfaces, data, workflows)
Relation graph: Edges representing relationships between entities (exposes, owns, triggers, depends-on)

These graphs form the structural foundation of the blueprint and enable further analysis, visualization, and pattern discovery.

title: 4  Deliverables of This Volume
text:
4.5 Confidence
Because architectural reconstruction involves interpretation, every extracted element carries an associated confidence level. Confidence represents the degree of certainty that the extracted element accurately reflects the system architecture.
Confidence states:
The framework defines three canonical confidence states:

Observed: Directly supported by explicit repository artifacts (e.g., route definitions, schema migrations, workflow DAGs)
Inferred: Derived from indirect signals and interpretation (e.g., component boundaries suggested by directory structure)
Unknown: Insufficient evidence exists to determine presence or structure

Confidence derivation:
Confidence is derived from observable properties of the evidence:

Evidence strength: Artifacts that explicitly encode architecture provide stronger evidence than indirect indicators
Evidence quantity: Multiple independent evidence objects increase confidence
Evidence consistency: Consistent evidence across artifacts increases confidence; conflicting evidence decreases it
Pattern alignment: Alignment with known architectural patterns can strengthen confidence

Confidence purposes:
Confidence values serve two critical purposes:

Uncertainty representation: Make uncertainty explicit rather than hidden, allowing blueprint consumers to understand reliability
Review prioritization: Guide human review by flagging low-confidence but high-impact elements that require attention

Confidence in practice:

High-confidence elements (observed) typically require minimal review
Medium-confidence elements (inferred) require moderate review, especially for critical architectural slots
Unknown elements may require review when they correspond to important structural roles or when strong signals suggest missing information

Confidence semantics ensure that extraction produces honest, auditable representations rather than overconfident speculations.

title: 4  Deliverables of This Volume
text:
4.6 Review Gate
Blueprint extraction is not fully automated. Human review is an explicit and required stage of the extraction process. This design choice is intentional:

Architectural interpretation requires judgment: Distinguishing correct from incorrect interpretations often requires domain knowledge
Ambiguity resolution: When evidence is conflicting or insufficient, human judgment is necessary
Quality assurance: Human validation provides the final quality gate before blueprints enter production use
Governance: Review establishes accountability and decision-making responsibility

Review gate responsibilities:
The review gate verifies that the extracted blueprint is:

Structurally complete: All required kernel slots are populated or explicitly marked as unknown
Semantically consistent: Relationships between entities form a coherent architecture
Supported by evidence: Every architectural element references traceable evidence
Aligned with system intent: The blueprint accurately reflects the system’s purpose and design

Review process:
Reviewers inspect the blueprint draft together with supporting materials:

Blueprint draft: The proposed architectural representation
Evidence bundle: All evidence objects supporting the draft
Extraction report: Diagnostics, gaps, conflicts, and confidence summaries

Review outcomes:
Reviewers may:

Confirm elements that accurately reflect the system
Adjust mappings that misinterpret artifacts
Add missing information not captured by automated extraction
Downgrade confidence for elements with insufficient support
Mark fields as unknown when evidence is absent

Maturity states:
After review, blueprints progress through defined maturity states:
draft → usable → reference

Draft: Direct output of the extraction pipeline, not yet reviewed
Usable: Passed initial review, considered sufficiently accurate for analysis and understanding
Reference: Fully reviewed and verified, may serve as canonical examples

This mechanism ensures that blueprint repositories maintain architectural reliability rather than becoming collections of speculative interpretations.

title: 4  Deliverables of This Volume
text:
4.7 The Extraction Discipline
These six elements—Pipeline, Signals, Evidence, Mapping, Confidence, and Review Gate—define the operational discipline of blueprint extraction.
The discipline in action:

Signals detect structural hints from repository artifacts
Evidence anchors interpretation to concrete, traceable artifacts
Mapping translates artifacts into canonical ontology structures
Confidence expresses uncertainty explicitly and guides review
Review gates ensure correctness and architectural reliability
The pipeline organizes the entire process into a reproducible workflow

Why this discipline matters:
Without this discipline, blueprint extraction would suffer from:

Speculation: Architectural claims without evidence
Opacity: Untraceable decisions and unreviewable interpretations
Inconsistency: Different results across different implementations
Unreliability: Incorrect architectures entering production datasets

With this discipline, blueprint extraction becomes:

Evidence-driven: Every claim is grounded in traceable artifacts
Auditable: Every decision can be inspected and validated
Reproducible: The same process yields consistent results
Scalable: The process can be automated and applied at scale
Improvable: Each component can be enhanced independently

The transformation:
Together, these elements transform repositories from raw implementation archives into sources of structured architectural knowledge—enabling blueprint libraries, architecture datasets, pattern discovery, and architecture intelligence systems.

title: 4  Deliverables of This Volume
text:
4.8 Chapter Summary

Blueprint extraction requires a structured engineering discipline, not a single algorithm
The framework delivers six core elements: Pipeline, Signals, Evidence, Mapping, Confidence, and Review Gate
The pipeline defines staged transformation from repository artifacts to approved blueprints
Signals are observable indicators that trigger hypothesis formation
Evidence provides traceable justification for every architectural claim
Mapping translates artifacts into canonical ontology entities and relationships
Confidence explicitly represents uncertainty in extracted elements
Human review gates ensure architectural reliability through validation
The discipline transforms repositories into sources of structured architectural knowledge

Next Chapter: Chapter 3 defines the scope and boundaries of this volume, establishing model independence, language independence, and requirements for auditability and scalability.

title: 5  Scope and Boundaries of This Volume
text:
5.1 Model Independence
Blueprint extraction addresses a fundamental problem: how to reconstruct system architecture from implementation artifacts. This problem is complex, multi-faceted, and situated at the intersection of software engineering, systems architecture, and program analysis.
However, the objective of this volume is not to prescribe a specific toolchain, algorithmic implementation, or technological solution. Doing so would limit the framework’s applicability, tie it to transient technologies, and constrain its evolution.
Instead, this volume defines the engineering framework within which extraction systems can be built, operated, and improved. The framework specifies what extraction should accomplish, how it should be structured, and what constraints it must satisfy—while leaving implementation choices open to innovation and adaptation.
To maintain generality, long-term applicability, and broad adoption, this volume establishes three explicit boundaries:
These constraints ensure that blueprint extraction functions as an architectural methodology—a durable, principled approach to architectural reconstruction—rather than a transient tool tied to specific technologies.
Extraction systems may employ a wide variety of computational techniques to interpret repository artifacts and infer architectural structure. The field is evolving rapidly, with new approaches emerging from research in static analysis, program understanding, machine learning, and artificial intelligence.
Possible computational approaches include:
The framework’s position:
The framework defined in this volume does not assume, endorse, or require any specific computational technique. It is deliberately model-agnostic. This agnosticism is essential for several reasons:
The pipeline’s relationship to models:
Regardless of the computational techniques employed, the extraction pipeline treats model outputs as intermediate hypotheses, not authoritative architectural facts. This design has important implications:
Models as assistive components:
This design prevents blueprint extraction from becoming dependent on a particular generation model or inference engine. Instead, models function as assistive components within a larger evidence-driven system:
This separation of concerns ensures that the framework remains robust even as model capabilities evolve or change.
crumbs: 5  Scope and Boundaries of This Volume

title: 5  Scope and Boundaries of This Volume
text:
Rule-based analysis: Explicit rules that map patterns to architectural interpretations
Static program analysis: Compiler techniques that analyze code structure without execution
Graph analysis: Network algorithms that analyze dependency and call graphs
Pattern matching: Template-based approaches that recognize known architectural patterns
Machine learning: Statistical models that learn to interpret artifacts from training data
Large language models: Neural systems that generate architectural descriptions from code and documentation
Hybrid approaches: Combinations of the above techniques




Technology evolution: Inference techniques are evolving rapidly; binding the framework to a specific approach would make it obsolete
Flexibility: Different repositories and use cases may benefit from different techniques
Competition and innovation: Leaving the model layer open encourages competition and innovation in extraction techniques
Risk mitigation: Dependence on a single model creates single points of failure and bias




Evidence requirement: Any inference produced by a model must still be supported by repository evidence
Ontology mapping: Model outputs must be mapped to the canonical system ontology
Confidence assignment: Inferences must be assigned explicit confidence levels based on evidence strength
Review gate: All inferences, regardless of source, must pass human review before acceptance




Models generate hypotheses and interpretations
Evidence validates or refutes those interpretations
Mapping translates interpretations into ontology structures
Confidence represents uncertainty in those interpretations
Review validates the overall architectural reconstruction
crumbs: 5  Scope and Boundaries of This Volume

title: 5  Scope and Boundaries of This Volume
text:
5.2 Language Independence
Repositories across software ecosystems vary enormously in programming languages, frameworks, tooling, and conventions. A modern software portfolio may include systems implemented in:

Python: Web services, data processing, scientific computing
Java: Enterprise applications, microservices, Android development
Go: Cloud-native services, distributed systems, CLI tools
Rust: Systems programming, performance-critical components
TypeScript/JavaScript: Frontend applications, Node.js services
C++: Performance-critical systems, embedded software
Ruby: Web applications, automation scripts
Mixed-language environments: Polyglot systems combining multiple languages

The challenge of language dependence:
A language-specific extraction approach would face several problems:

Limited applicability: Each language requires separate implementation and maintenance
Ecosystem fragmentation: Different frameworks within the same language require specialized handling
Rapid obsolescence: Language and framework evolution requires constant updates
High cost: Building and maintaining language-specific parsers is expensive

The framework’s language-agnostic approach:
Extraction must therefore operate at the level of structural signals, not language-specific syntax. This approach recognizes that while implementation details vary across languages, structural patterns often recur:

Repository layout: Most ecosystems organize code into directories and modules
Dependency manifests: Most ecosystems have files declaring dependencies (package.json, requirements.txt, go.mod, etc.)
Entrypoints: Most ecosystems define how execution begins (main functions, server initialization, CLI bootstrap)
Configuration: Most systems have configuration files (YAML, JSON, environment variables, etc.)
Interface definitions: Most systems define interfaces through routes, RPC definitions, or command specifications
Deployment specifications: Most systems have deployment artifacts (Dockerfiles, Kubernetes manifests, etc.)
Workflow definitions: Most systems have orchestration or scheduling mechanisms

Signal taxonomy as the foundation:
The signal taxonomy (detailed in Chapter 7 and Appendix A) is designed to identify artifacts that exist across most ecosystems. By focusing on cross-ecosystem signals rather than language-specific syntax, the framework achieves language independence.
Role of language-specific tools:
Language-specific parsers, analyzers, and tools may improve extraction quality for particular ecosystems. However, the extraction framework itself remains language-agnostic:

The pipeline defines stages and responsibilities that apply regardless of language
The ontology defines entities and relationships that are language-independent
The evidence model defines traceability that works across all artifact types
The confidence semantics apply uniformly regardless of inference source

Benefits of language independence:
This property allows blueprint extraction to:

Operate across heterogeneous repositories: Analyze polyglot systems and diverse technology stacks
Scale effectively: Apply the same extraction framework to millions of repositories regardless of language
Support new ecosystems: Adapt to new languages and frameworks without framework redesign
Enable cross-ecosystem comparison: Compare architectures across different technology stacks

Language independence ensures that blueprint extraction remains broadly applicable and future-proof.
crumbs: 5  Scope and Boundaries of This Volume

title: 5  Scope and Boundaries of This Volume
text:
5.3 Auditability
Because blueprint extraction reconstructs architectural meaning—that is, it makes claims about what a system is and how it works—the process must remain verifiable. Reviewers, auditors, and users must be able to validate that extracted blueprints accurately reflect the underlying repositories.
The auditability requirement:
Every extracted architectural element must be traceable to its origin in repository artifacts. This traceability is not optional; it is a fundamental requirement for reliable extraction.
Achieving auditability through three mechanisms:
First: Evidence traceability
Every blueprint element references one or more evidence objects that point to concrete repository artifacts. Evidence objects capture:

The source artifact (file path, URL, or repository location)
The precise location within the artifact (line range, JSON path, object identifier)
The relevant content fragment (excerpt)
The artifact type (code, configuration, schema, deployment)
The evidence strength (strong, moderate, weak)

This granular traceability allows reviewers to locate and inspect the exact artifacts that support each architectural claim.
Second: Explicit mapping rules
The transformation from evidence to ontology entities must follow documented, interpretable rules rather than opaque inference or hidden heuristics. Mapping rules should be:

Explicit: Documented in specification or code
Interpretable: Explainable in plain language
Deterministic: Producing consistent outputs from the same inputs
Reviewable: Subject to inspection and challenge

Explicit mapping rules ensure that architectural interpretations are not arbitrary but follow systematic, justifiable procedures.
Third: Extraction reports
Each extraction run produces a comprehensive report describing:

Signals detected: What structural indicators were found
Evidence collected: What artifacts support each architectural claim
Mappings applied: What rules transformed evidence into ontology entities
Uncertainties encountered: Where evidence was weak, conflicting, or absent
Confidence assignments: How confidence was calculated for each element
Validation results: What checks passed, failed, or produced warnings

Extraction reports serve as operational documentation of the extraction process, enabling reviewers to understand how and why specific architectural interpretations were produced.
The auditability payoff:
Together these mechanisms allow reviewers to:

Inspect the reasoning behind generated blueprints
Validate that architectural claims are supported by evidence
Debug extraction errors by examining the evidence trail
Challenge incorrect interpretations
Improve extraction systems by identifying weak mappings or missing signals

Auditability ensures that blueprint repositories maintain architectural integrity—blueprints are not arbitrary descriptions but verifiable reconstructions grounded in evidence.
crumbs: 5  Scope and Boundaries of This Volume

title: 5  Scope and Boundaries of This Volume
text:
5.4 Scalability
The long-term objective of blueprint extraction is not merely to analyze individual repositories, but to support large blueprint datasets containing thousands or millions of architectural representations. These datasets enable:

Architecture pattern mining: Discovering recurring architectural structures
Architecture intelligence: Automated systems that reason about system design
Evolution tracking: Understanding how architectures change over time
Comparative analysis: Comparing architectures across systems and organizations
Design guidance: Informing architectural decisions based on patterns and precedents

Achieving this scale requires the extraction framework to operate efficiently across massive numbers of repositories while maintaining quality and traceability.
Scalability requirements:
Scalability imposes several design requirements on the extraction framework:
1. Pipeline execution
Extraction must support pipeline-based execution, allowing repositories to be processed:

Incrementally: Processing new repositories without reprocessing old ones
In parallel: Distributing work across multiple workers or machines
Stagewise: Applying different stages at different times or with different resources
Resumably: Recovering from failures without losing progress

Pipeline execution enables efficient resource utilization and fault-tolerant operation at scale.
2. Diff-friendly outputs
Extraction artifacts must be diff-friendly, enabling:

Versioned updates: Tracking how blueprints change as repositories evolve
Git review compatibility: Using standard version control workflows for blueprint review
Incremental extraction: Updating only the changed portions of blueprints
Change detection: Identifying what changed between extraction runs

Diff-friendly outputs require deterministic serialization, stable field ordering, and minimal noise from regenerated metadata.
3. Machine-processable artifacts
Evidence bundles, reports, and blueprints must remain machine-processable, enabling:

Automated validation: Checking structural correctness without manual review
Dataset construction: Building large collections of blueprints programmatically
Pattern mining: Analyzing blueprints to discover architectural patterns
Metrics calculation: Computing quality metrics, statistics, and summaries

Machine-processable artifacts require structured formats (e.g., YAML, JSON), explicit schemas, and consistent conventions.
4. Cost-tiered analysis
Extraction must support cost-tiered analysis, where:

Cheap pass: Lightweight scans identify promising repositories and filter out unpromising ones
Deep pass: Full extraction applied only to repositories that pass the cheap pass
Resource allocation: Computational resources allocated based on repository value and complexity
Priority queues: High-value repositories processed before low-value ones

Cost-tiered analysis prevents resource waste on repositories with minimal architectural content while ensuring thorough analysis of valuable systems.
Scalability as infrastructure:
These requirements allow blueprint extraction to function not only as an analysis technique, but as an infrastructure for architectural knowledge mining—a robust, scalable system capable of transforming massive repositories into structured architectural datasets.
crumbs: 5  Scope and Boundaries of This Volume

title: 5  Scope and Boundaries of This Volume
text:
5.5 Boundary Summary
The scope of this volume can therefore be summarized as follows:
What the framework defines:

How blueprint extraction should be structured: The pipeline stages, their responsibilities, and their relationships
What elements are required: Signals, evidence, mapping, confidence, and review gates
What constraints must be satisfied: Model independence, language independence, auditability, and scalability
What outputs must be produced: Blueprint packs with kernels, views, evidence bundles, and reports

What the framework does not define:

How a particular tool must be implemented: Implementation choices are left to tool builders
Which computational techniques to use: Model selection is left to extraction system designers
Which programming languages to support: Language-specific implementations are left to ecosystem specialists
Which tools or frameworks to integrate: Integration choices are left to operational requirements

Compliance principles:
Extraction systems may vary widely in implementation, models, and language support. However, any compliant system must satisfy three core principles:

Model independence: The system must not be bound to a specific inference model or computational technique
Language independence: The system must operate through structural signals rather than language-specific syntax
Auditability and scalability: The system must be verifiable through evidence traceability and capable of operating at scale

The payoff:
These constraints ensure that blueprint extraction remains a durable architectural discipline capable of:

Supporting evolution in computational techniques without framework redesign
Operating across diverse technology stacks and ecosystems
Maintaining architectural integrity through evidence traceability
Scaling to millions of repositories for large-scale architecture intelligence

The framework is designed to be a lasting foundation for architectural understanding, not a transient solution tied to specific technologies.
crumbs: 5  Scope and Boundaries of This Volume

title: 5  Scope and Boundaries of This Volume
text:
5.6 Chapter Summary

The volume defines an engineering framework, not a specific toolchain or implementation
Three explicit boundaries establish the framework’s scope and constraints
Model independence prevents dependence on specific inference techniques and supports evolution
Language independence enables operation across diverse ecosystems and technology stacks
Auditability ensures verifiability through evidence traceability, explicit mapping rules, and extraction reports
Scalability enables operation at scale through pipeline execution, diff-friendly outputs, machine-processable artifacts, and cost-tiered analysis
The framework defines what extraction should accomplish, leaving implementation choices open
Compliance with model independence, language independence, and auditability/scalability is required
These constraints ensure a durable architectural discipline capable of supporting large-scale architecture intelligence

Next Chapter: Chapter 4 introduces the Blueprint Kernel—the canonical architectural representation that extraction aims to reconstruct. This establishes the target of the entire extraction process and defines what architectural structures the pipeline seeks to identify and populate.
crumbs: 5  Scope and Boundaries of This Volume

title: 6  What We Extract: Blueprint Kernel and Views
text:
6.1 The Blueprint Kernel as the Extraction Target
Blueprint extraction does not attempt to reconstruct every detail of a system implementation. Doing so would be computationally expensive, architecturally noisy, and analytically unnecessary. Extraction operates at a higher level of abstraction.
Instead, the objective is to recover the architectural structure of the system in a stable, canonical, and computable form. This structure captures what the system is—its essential organization and design—rather than how it is implemented.
The extracted structure is represented through two layers:
The Kernel defines the canonical, complete architectural model. Views provide specialized perspectives on that model. This separation ensures a single source of architectural truth while enabling diverse analytical applications.
Extraction therefore targets the Kernel as the primary artifact. Views are not extracted independently; they are generated from the kernel through deterministic projection rules.
The Blueprint Kernel defines the minimal set of structural elements required to represent a system architecture. It is designed to be:
The Kernel consists of six mandatory slots:
Each slot represents a distinct, non-overlapping dimension of system structure. Together, they form a structural skeleton that captures the essential architecture of any system.
The six kernel slots:
Goal describes the problem space the system addresses and the value it provides. It answers: What is this system for?
Capabilities define the functional abilities the system provides to users or other systems. They answer: What can this system do?
Components represent the structural units responsible for implementing capabilities. They answer: What are the building blocks of this system?
Interfaces describe how components or external actors interact with the system. They answer: How is this system accessed?
Data represents the persistent information owned or managed by the system. It answers: What information does this system maintain?
Workflows describe the control flow through which components coordinate behavior. They answer: How does execution flow through this system?
These six slots provide comprehensive coverage of system architecture while remaining abstract enough to apply across diverse systems—from simple CLI tools to complex distributed systems.
The kernel as a structural skeleton:
The six slots form a structural skeleton that supports multiple architectural analyses:
The kernel provides a stable representation that is independent of specific programming languages, frameworks, or deployment environments. This stability is essential for building blueprint datasets that remain valid as underlying technologies evolve.
crumbs: Part I — Extraction as Evidence-Driven Engineering
6  What We Extract: Blueprint Kernel and Views

title: 6  What We Extract: Blueprint Kernel and Views
text:
Minimal: Contains only essential architectural information
Canonical: Represents the authoritative architectural model
Stable: Changes only when actual architecture changes
Language-independent: Abstracts away implementation details
Computable: Can be processed by automated tools and algorithms


Goal
Capabilities
Components
Interfaces
Data
Workflows












Component analysis: Examining components and their relationships
Interface analysis: Understanding interaction surfaces and contracts
Data analysis: Mapping data ownership and flow
Workflow analysis: Understanding execution coordination
Capability analysis: Mapping functional abilities to structure
Evolution analysis: Tracking how architecture changes over time
crumbs: Part I — Extraction as Evidence-Driven Engineering
6  What We Extract: Blueprint Kernel and Views

title: 6  What We Extract: Blueprint Kernel and Views
text:
6.2 Kernel Slots in Detail
Each kernel slot has a specific definition, extraction target, and evidence requirements. Understanding these details is essential for designing extraction systems and interpreting blueprints.

6.2.1 Goal
Definition: The high-level problem the system addresses and the value proposition it delivers.
Extraction targets: Repository artifacts that describe system purpose, including: - Repository README files and descriptions - Project documentation and design documents - Issue tracker descriptions and mission statements - Marketing materials or product descriptions - Source code comments expressing architectural intent
Evidence characteristics: Goal is often the most abstract kernel slot. Evidence may be: - Explicit: Direct statements of purpose in documentation - Inferred: Derived from aggregated capabilities and interfaces - Partial: Goal may be reconstructed indirectly from behavior
Confidence implications: Goal often requires interpretation and synthesis, so confidence is typically moderate to low. Unknown goals are common when documentation is absent.



6.2.2 Capabilities
Definition: The functional abilities the system provides to users or other systems.
Extraction targets: Artifacts that reveal what the system can do: - API endpoints and route definitions - CLI commands and subcommands - Event handlers and message consumers - Workflow steps and pipeline tasks - Test cases describing expected behavior
Evidence characteristics: Capabilities are often strongly evidenced by: - Explicit route or command definitions - Interface specifications (OpenAPI, protobuf, etc.) - Workflow definitions describing operations - Documentation of system functions
Confidence implications: Capabilities directly visible through interfaces often have high confidence. Capabilities inferred from indirect behavior have moderate confidence.



6.2.3 Components
Definition: The structural units of responsibility that implement capabilities.
Extraction targets: Artifacts that reveal structural decomposition: - Service boundaries and module organization - Worker processes and background tasks - Scheduler or orchestrator components - Plugin or extension systems - Microservice or container boundaries
Evidence characteristics: Component evidence varies in strength: - Strong: Explicit service boundaries, deployment manifests - Moderate: Directory structure suggesting decomposition - Weak: Naming conventions or module organization
Confidence implications: Explicit component boundaries (services, containers) have high confidence. Inferred components from directory structure have moderate confidence.



6.2.4 Interfaces
Definition: The interaction surfaces through which capabilities are accessed.
Extraction targets: Artifacts defining interaction mechanisms: - HTTP route definitions and API specifications - Event topics and message schemas - CLI command definitions and argument parsers - RPC service definitions and protocol buffers - WebSocket or streaming endpoints
Evidence characteristics: Interfaces typically have strong evidence: - Explicit: Route definitions, API specifications - Documented: Interface documentation or contract files - Tested: Integration tests verifying interface behavior
Confidence implications: Interfaces with explicit definitions and documentation have high confidence. Inferred interfaces from code patterns have moderate confidence.



6.2.5 Data
Definition: The persistent information owned, maintained, or transformed by the system.
Extraction targets: Artifacts revealing data structures: - Database migration files and schema definitions - ORM model definitions and data classes - Document collection schemas and validation rules - Cache structures and key-value stores - Data lake or object storage schemas
Evidence characteristics: Data evidence is typically strong: - Explicit: Migration files, schema definitions - Structural: ORM models reflecting database structure - Operational: Storage configuration and deployment specs
Confidence implications: Explicit schema definitions have high confidence. Inferred data entities from access patterns have moderate confidence.



6.2.6 Workflows
Definition: The execution sequences through which components coordinate to realize capabilities.
Extraction targets: Artifacts describing execution coordination: - DAG definitions and workflow specifications - Pipeline configurations and orchestration scripts - Scheduler configurations and cron jobs - Event flow definitions and processing pipelines - State machine definitions
Evidence characteristics: Workflow evidence varies: - Strong: Explicit DAG specifications, workflow engines - Moderate: Scheduler configurations, cron jobs - Weak: Event patterns inferred from code structure
Confidence implications: Explicit workflow definitions have high confidence. Inferred workflows from code organization have moderate confidence.
crumbs: Part I — Extraction as Evidence-Driven Engineering
6  What We Extract: Blueprint Kernel and Views

title: 6  What We Extract: Blueprint Kernel and Views
text:
6.3 Kernel Slots as Extraction Objectives
During extraction, each kernel slot becomes an explicit target of inference. The extraction pipeline systematically attempts to populate each slot with evidence-backed elements.
The extraction process for each slot:

Signal detection: Identify repository artifacts that may correspond to the slot
Hypothesis formation: Formulate provisional interpretations in terms of the slot
Evidence collection: Gather concrete artifacts supporting or refuting each hypothesis
Ontology mapping: Map validated evidence to canonical entities for the slot
Slot population: Assemble mapped entities into the kernel slot

Slot-specific extraction patterns:









Kernel Slot
Primary Signal Sources
Typical Evidence
Common Ambiguities




Goal
Documentation, descriptions, README
Explicit statements, indirect inference
Abstract, multi-faceted, often undocumented


Capabilities
Routes, commands, workflows, tests
Explicit definitions, test cases
Implicit capabilities, undocumented features


Components
Services, workers, plugins, modules
Deployment manifests, directory structure
Logical vs. deployable boundaries


Interfaces
Routes, topics, commands, RPC
Route definitions, API specs
Internal vs. external interfaces


Data
Migrations, schemas, ORM models
Schema files, migration history
Transient vs. persistent data


Workflows
DAGs, pipelines, schedulers
Workflow specs, cron configs
Ad-hoc vs. formalized workflows



Handling incomplete extraction:
Not all slots can always be fully reconstructed from repository artifacts. The extraction process allows:

Partial completion: Some elements of a slot are populated, others remain unknown
Explicit unknowns: Slots or fields marked as “unknown” when evidence is insufficient
Confidence annotations: Low-confidence elements marked appropriately for review

This honest representation of uncertainty prevents blueprints from accumulating unsupported assumptions.
crumbs: Part I — Extraction as Evidence-Driven Engineering
6  What We Extract: Blueprint Kernel and Views

title: 6  What We Extract: Blueprint Kernel and Views
text:
6.4 The Evidence Traceability Principle
A central, non-negotiable rule governs kernel extraction:
Every extracted field must be traceable to repository evidence.
This principle is the foundation of reliable extraction. It prevents architectural claims from becoming speculative interpretations and ensures that blueprints remain auditable, reproducible, and debuggable.
What the principle requires:
Each element that fills a kernel slot must reference one or more evidence objects that originate from concrete repository artifacts:

Source files: Code implementing component behavior or interface handlers
Configuration files: Files defining services, routing, or dependencies
Dependency manifests: Files declaring required libraries or frameworks
Schema definitions: Files defining data structures and constraints
Workflow specifications: Files describing execution coordination
Documentation fragments: Text describing system intent or design

Evidence object structure:
Each evidence object captures: - Source: The repository artifact (file path, URL, or location) - Location: The exact position within the artifact - Excerpt: The relevant content fragment - Type: The artifact classification (code, config, schema, deploy, documentation) - Strength: How directly the artifact supports the interpretation
The traceability payoff:
Evidence traceability ensures that blueprint elements remain:

Auditable: Reviewers can verify claims by inspecting source artifacts
Reproducible: The same extraction process yields consistent results
Debuggable: Errors can be traced to specific evidence or mapping decisions
Evolvable: Blueprints can be updated as repositories change
Verifiable: Independent systems can validate extraction results

Handling insufficient evidence:
When insufficient evidence exists to populate a kernel slot, the extraction process must:

Mark the field as unknown rather than filling it with speculation
Document why evidence is insufficient in the extraction report
Flag the field for potential human review if it corresponds to a critical architectural element

This rule maintains the integrity of blueprint datasets and enables reliable architectural analysis. Blueprints with honest unknown representations are more valuable than blueprints filled with confident but incorrect assumptions.
crumbs: Part I — Extraction as Evidence-Driven Engineering
6  What We Extract: Blueprint Kernel and Views

title: 6  What We Extract: Blueprint Kernel and Views
text:
6.5 Blueprint Views as Projections
While the Kernel represents the canonical blueprint structure, different stakeholders require different perspectives on the system. A single architectural model cannot simultaneously optimize for all analytical and operational needs.
Blueprint views provide these specialized perspectives. A view is a projection of the kernel structure that emphasizes specific architectural aspects while omitting or de-emphasizing others.
Key characteristics of views:

Derived: Views are generated from the kernel, not extracted independently
Deterministic: The same kernel always produces the same view
Perspective-specific: Each view serves a particular analytical or operational purpose
Lossy: Views may omit or summarize kernel information for clarity
Reproducible: Views can be regenerated deterministically from the kernel

The extraction process and views:
The extraction pipeline does not attempt to extract views independently. Instead:

The pipeline extracts and populates the kernel slots
Once the kernel is validated, view projections are generated through defined rules
Views are stored alongside the kernel in the blueprint pack

This kernel-first strategy ensures that: - All views are consistent with a single architectural model - Changes to the kernel propagate correctly to all views - New views can be added without re-extraction - Views can be generated on-demand as needed
Common blueprint views:
The Spec View focuses on system behavior and externally visible contracts. It emphasizes: - System goal and purpose - Exposed capabilities and their descriptions - Interface contracts and specifications - Functional requirements and constraints
The Architecture View emphasizes structural relationships between architectural elements. It highlights: - Component composition and boundaries - Interface connections and dependencies - Data ownership and flow - Workflow coordination and control
The Ops View focuses on deployment topology and operational execution. It captures: - Deployment units and packaging - Runtime dependencies and services - Operational workflows (build, deploy, monitor) - Observability and monitoring instrumentation
The Security/Trust View focuses on security boundaries and trust relationships. It reveals: - Trust boundaries between components - Authentication and authorization mechanisms - Sensitive data access paths - Security-relevant interfaces and policies
Each view reorganizes the same underlying kernel information to serve different analytical contexts—design review, operational planning, security assessment, or architectural comparison.
crumbs: Part I — Extraction as Evidence-Driven Engineering
6  What We Extract: Blueprint Kernel and Views

title: 6  What We Extract: Blueprint Kernel and Views
text:
6.6 Kernel-First Extraction
Because views are projections, the extraction pipeline adopts a kernel-first strategy. This strategy has important implications for the extraction process and system design.
The kernel-first extraction sequence:

Reconstruct the kernel slots:

Goal
Capabilities
Components
Interfaces
Data
Workflows

Validate the kernel:

Check structural completeness
Verify reference integrity
Assess confidence levels
Detect anomalies

Generate view projections:

Apply projection rules to create each view
Validate view-specific constraints
Package views with the kernel

Package the blueprint pack:

Assemble kernel, views, evidence, and reports
Generate diff-friendly serialization
Prepare for review and distribution


Benefits of kernel-first extraction:
Structural consistency: Since all views derive from a single kernel, they remain mutually consistent. Changes to the kernel propagate correctly to all views.
Evidence efficiency: Evidence is collected once for the kernel and reused across multiple views, avoiding redundant extraction effort.
Adaptability: New analytical views can be added by defining new projection rules without re-extracting repositories.
Maintainability: When extraction methodology improves, only kernel extraction needs updating—view projections regenerate automatically.
Traceability: Evidence traceability is maintained through the kernel; views inherit this traceability through their derivation from kernel elements.
The kernel as canonical truth:
The Kernel functions as the canonical representation of system architecture. Views act as interpretive lenses derived from that structure. This separation of concerns ensures:

A single source of architectural truth
Multiple analytical perspectives without inconsistency
Efficient update and evolution of the architectural model
Clear boundaries between extraction and analysis

The kernel-first strategy is therefore not merely a convenience but a fundamental architectural principle of blueprint extraction.
crumbs: Part I — Extraction as Evidence-Driven Engineering
6  What We Extract: Blueprint Kernel and Views

title: 6  What We Extract: Blueprint Kernel and Views
text:
6.7 Chapter Summary

Blueprint extraction targets architectural structure, not implementation details
The extracted structure has two layers: the Blueprint Kernel (canonical) and Blueprint Views (projections)
The Kernel consists of six slots: Goal, Capabilities, Components, Interfaces, Data, and Workflows
Each slot represents a distinct dimension of system architecture with specific extraction targets
The evidence traceability principle requires every extracted field to reference concrete repository artifacts
Insufficient evidence results in explicit unknowns rather than speculative assumptions
Views are projections generated from the kernel, not extracted independently
Kernel-first extraction ensures structural consistency, evidence efficiency, and adaptability
The Kernel is the canonical source of architectural truth; views provide specialized analytical perspectives

Next Chapter: Chapter 5 explores the fundamental distinction between repositories and systems, examining the missing layers (runtime, operations, constraints) that exist outside repositories and how extraction handles these gaps.
crumbs: Part I — Extraction as Evidence-Driven Engineering
6  What We Extract: Blueprint Kernel and Views

title: 7  Repo ≠ System: The Missing Layers
text:
7.1 Missing Layers: Runtime, Operations, and Constraints
A repository is often treated as the canonical representation of a system. In practice, this assumption is fundamentally incorrect. Treating repositories as equivalent to systems leads to extraction errors, architectural misunderstandings, and unreliable blueprints.
A repository contains implementation artifacts—the source code, configuration files, build scripts, and documentation that developers produce during software development. These artifacts represent how the system is implemented.
A system includes architectural elements—the structural organization, runtime behavior, operational constraints, and integration patterns that define what the system is and how it operates.
Many aspects of system architecture exist outside the repository or are only partially represented within it. Blueprint extraction must therefore acknowledge a fundamental limitation:
A repository is evidence about a system, not the system itself.
Understanding this distinction is essential for designing a reliable extraction methodology. It explains why extraction is difficult, why uncertainty is inevitable, and why evidence-based interpretation is necessary.
Several critical layers of a system are often absent or only partially represented in repositories. These missing layers create gaps that extraction must acknowledge rather than attempt to fill through speculation.
crumbs: Part I — Extraction as Evidence-Driven Engineering
7  Repo ≠ System: The Missing Layers

title: 7  Repo ≠ System: The Missing Layers
text:
7.1.1 The Runtime Layer
The runtime environment defines how the system executes in production. This layer includes:

Execution topology: How services are deployed, containerized, and orchestrated
Service discovery: How components locate and communicate with each other
Scaling policies: How the system scales horizontally or vertically
Resource limits: CPU, memory, storage, and network constraints
Load balancing: How requests are distributed across instances
Failover mechanisms: How the system handles component failures
