The Evidence Object Model provides the structural foundation for blueprint extraction. Without it, extraction would lack:

Verifiability: Claims could not be validated
Auditability: Decisions could not be traced
Reproducibility: Results would vary between runs
Debuggability: Errors could not be located
Reliability: Blueprints would accumulate speculation

With the Evidence Object Model, extraction becomes:
Evidence-driven:

Signals initiate hypotheses
Evidence validates or refutes hypotheses
Mappings translate evidence into ontology structures
Blueprints assemble validated structures

Auditable:

Every claim references evidence
Every evidence references artifacts
Every artifact is locatable
Every decision is traceable

Improvable:

Evidence quality can be measured
Evidence collection can be optimized
Evidence interpretation can be refined
Evidence conflicts can be resolved

Scalable:

Evidence is structured for machine processing
Evidence bundles can be versioned and diffed
Evidence quality can be automated
Evidence review can be prioritized

The Evidence Object Model therefore transforms blueprint extraction from heuristic inference into a verifiable architecture reconstruction process.
crumbs: Part II — Signal Taxonomy & Evidence Model

title: 10  Evidence Object Model
text:
10.10 Chapter Summary

Evidence is the foundation of reliable extraction—every claim must be traceable
Evidence objects represent structured references to repository artifacts
The evidence schema includes: id, source, location, excerpt, type, category, strength, and metadata
Evidence aggregation combines multiple signals to strengthen interpretations
Evidence conflicts must be preserved and resolved systematically
Evidence bundles provide the audit trail for extraction processes
Evidence quality is assessed through strength, freshness, consistency, and specificity
Confidence is derived from evidence through calculation
Observed confidence comes from strong, consistent, specific evidence
Inferred confidence comes from moderate evidence
Unknown confidence results from weak or insufficient evidence
The Evidence Object Model enables verifiable, auditable, reproducible extraction
Evidence-driven extraction is the foundation of reliable architectural reconstruction

Next Chapter: Chapter 9 introduces Confidence Semantics—the formal framework for representing uncertainty in extracted architectures. Confidence semantics ensures that extraction produces honest, auditable representations rather than overconfident speculations.
crumbs: Part II — Signal Taxonomy & Evidence Model

title: 11  Confidence Semantics
text:
11.1 The Problem of Uncertainty
Blueprint extraction reconstructs architectural structure from indirect repository signals. Because repositories rarely encode architecture explicitly, extracted elements inevitably contain varying degrees of certainty.
Confidence semantics provide a formal mechanism for representing this uncertainty. Rather than presenting extracted architecture as definitive truth, the extraction process attaches confidence annotations to every inferred element. These annotations communicate how strongly the available evidence supports the architectural interpretation.
Confidence semantics therefore serve two critical roles:
Without confidence semantics, extraction would produce overconfident, unreliable architectural reconstructions. With confidence semantics, extraction produces honest, auditable representations that acknowledge the limits of available information.
Architectural reconstruction is inherently uncertain. Repositories provide incomplete, ambiguous, and sometimes contradictory signals about system structure. Extracting architecture from these signals inevitably involves interpretation, judgment, and inference—all of which introduce uncertainty.
Sources of uncertainty:
1. Missing information
Some architectural elements exist only outside repositories:
When information is missing, extraction must either: - Mark elements as unknown (honest representation) - Infer elements from partial signals (risky interpretation)
2. Implicit conventions
Architectural intent may be encoded indirectly:
Implicit conventions are fragile and may: - Vary across teams and organizations - Be applied inconsistently - Evolve over time without documentation
3. Ambiguous evidence
A single artifact may admit multiple interpretations:
Ambiguity forces extraction to choose among plausible interpretations, introducing uncertainty.
4. Conflicting evidence
Different artifacts may suggest inconsistent architectures:
Conflicting evidence forces extraction to choose which sources to trust.
The cost of ignoring uncertainty:
If extraction ignores uncertainty and presents all interpretations as equally certain:
The value of representing uncertainty:
By making uncertainty explicit, extraction becomes:
Confidence semantics therefore transform uncertainty from a weakness into a feature—honest representation of knowledge limits.
crumbs: Part II — Signal Taxonomy & Evidence Model

title: 11  Confidence Semantics
text:
Operational constraints: Runtime policies, scaling requirements
Runtime environments: Container orchestration, service discovery
Organizational ownership: Team responsibilities, governance structures
External integrations: Third-party services, API dependencies





Naming patterns: Directory names suggesting component boundaries
Framework conventions: Framework-specific structural patterns
Organizational standards: Team-specific conventions





Directory named services/ could mean microservices or logical modules
Queue dependency could indicate async processing or message transport
Scheduler configuration could indicate workflows or maintenance tasks





Outdated documentation: Describes components that no longer exist
Legacy configuration: References deprecated services
Evolutionary drift: Architecture evolved but artifacts not updated





Overconfident decisions: Users may trust incorrect architectures
Misguided actions: Architecture decisions based on incorrect information
Wasted resources: Time spent investigating non-existent structures
Erosion of trust: Users lose confidence in extraction accuracy




Honest: Acknowledges what is known versus unknown
Useful: Guides users to focus attention appropriately
Improvable: Identifies areas where methodology needs improvement
Trustworthy: Builds confidence through transparency
crumbs: Part II — Signal Taxonomy & Evidence Model

title: 11  Confidence Semantics
text:
11.2 Confidence Object Structure
Confidence annotations are attached to architectural entities and fields. The minimal confidence representation ensures that uncertainty is captured consistently across the extraction pipeline.
Minimal confidence representation:
confidence:
  state: <observed | inferred | unknown>
  score: <optional-numeric-value>  # 0.0 to 1.0
  evidence_refs:
    - <evidence-id>
    - <evidence-id>
  confidence_details:
    evidence_strength: <strong | moderate | weak>
    evidence_count: <number>
    evidence_consistency: <high | medium | low>
    pattern_alignment: <strong | moderate | weak | none>
  assigned_by:
    method: <manual | calculated>
    version: <confidence-model-version>
    timestamp: <iso8601>
Field explanations:

11.2.1 state
The qualitative confidence state. Three canonical states are defined:

observed: Directly supported by explicit repository artifacts
inferred: Derived from indirect signals and interpretation
unknown: Insufficient evidence exists to determine presence or structure



11.2.2 score
An optional numeric confidence value between 0.0 (no confidence) and 1.0 (complete confidence). Numeric scores enable:

Granular comparison: Comparing confidence across elements
Thresholding: Filtering elements by confidence level
Aggregation: Calculating aggregate confidence for groups
Machine learning: Feeding confidence into ML models



11.2.3 evidence_refs
List of evidence object IDs supporting this confidence assignment. This linkage enables:

Traceability: Connecting confidence to supporting evidence
Validation: Verifying confidence is justified
Debugging: Identifying which evidence contributed to confidence
Review: Focusing review on relevant evidence



11.2.4 confidence_details
Detailed breakdown of factors contributing to confidence:

evidence_strength: Strongest evidence type (strong/moderate/weak)
evidence_count: Number of supporting evidence objects
evidence_consistency: How well evidence aligns (high/medium/low)
pattern_alignment: Alignment with known patterns (strong/moderate/weak/none)

These details enable confidence calculation and explanation.


11.2.5 assigned_by
Metadata about how confidence was assigned:

method: Manual assignment by reviewer or calculated by pipeline
version: Confidence model version for reproducibility
timestamp: When confidence was assigned
crumbs: Part II — Signal Taxonomy & Evidence Model

title: 11  Confidence Semantics
text:
11.3 Confidence States
Confidence Semantics v0.1 defines three canonical confidence states. These states represent increasing levels of uncertainty and guide review prioritization.

11.3.1 Observed
Definition: An element is classified as observed when its existence is directly supported by explicit repository artifacts.
Characteristics:

Explicit encoding: Architecture is directly declared in artifacts
Strong evidence: Evidence objects with strong strength ratings
Unambiguous interpretation: Only one plausible interpretation exists
Verifiable: Can be validated by inspecting source artifacts

Examples:
Observed component:
component:
  id: component.api_service
  type: Service
  confidence:
    state: observed
    score: 0.95
    evidence_refs:
      - evidence-001  # Server initialization code
      - evidence-002  # Deployment configuration
      - evidence-003  # API route definitions
    confidence_details:
      evidence_strength: strong
      evidence_count: 3
      evidence_consistency: high
      pattern_alignment: strong
Observed interface:
interface:
  id: interface.http_api
  type: API
  confidence:
    state: observed
    score: 0.92
    evidence_refs:
      - evidence-004  # Route definitions
      - evidence-005  # OpenAPI specification
      - evidence-006  # Controller implementations
Observed data entity:
data:
  id: data.payments
  confidence:
    state: observed
    score: 0.98
    evidence_refs:
      - evidence-007  # Schema migration
      - evidence-008  # ORM model
      - evidence-009  # Database DDL
Confidence implications:
Observed elements generally have: - High confidence scores (0.8-1.0) - Minimal review requirements (spot checks sufficient) - High reliability for architectural analysis
Observed elements represent the foundation of reliable extraction—the structures we can trust with high certainty.


11.3.2 Inferred
Definition: An element is classified as inferred when it is derived from indirect signals rather than explicit artifacts.
Characteristics:

Interpretive: Architecture is suggested rather than declared
Moderate evidence: Evidence objects with moderate strength ratings
Plausible but uncertain: Multiple interpretations may be possible
Validation required: Should be reviewed to confirm interpretation

Examples:
Inferred component:
component:
  id: component.payment_worker
  type: Worker
  confidence:
    state: inferred
    score: 0.65
    evidence_refs:
      - evidence-010  # Queue dependency
      - evidence-011  # Worker directory
      - evidence-012  # Scheduler configuration
    confidence_details:
      evidence_strength: moderate
      evidence_count: 3
      evidence_consistency: medium
      pattern_alignment: moderate
Inferred capability:
capability:
  id: capability.batch_processing
  confidence:
    state: inferred
    score: 0.58
    evidence_refs:
      - evidence-013  # Scheduler configuration
      - evidence-014  # Workflow definitions
    confidence_details:
      evidence_strength: moderate
      evidence_count: 2
      evidence_consistency: medium
      pattern_alignment: weak
Inferred workflow:
workflow:
  id: workflow.payment_processing
  confidence:
    state: inferred
    score: 0.72
    evidence_refs:
      - evidence-015  # DAG definition
      - evidence-016  # Task implementations
    confidence_details:
      evidence_strength: moderate
      evidence_count: 2
      evidence_consistency: high
      pattern_alignment: moderate
Confidence implications:
Inferred elements generally have: - Medium confidence scores (0.4-0.8) - Moderate review requirements (validate interpretation) - Questionable reliability without human confirmation
Inferred elements require human review to confirm that the interpretation is correct and to adjust confidence based on additional context.


11.3.3 Unknown
Definition: An element is classified as unknown when insufficient evidence exists to determine its presence or structure.
Characteristics:

Evidence absent: No or insufficient evidence available
Cannot be determined: Interpretation would be pure speculation
Honest representation: Explicitly acknowledges knowledge gap
Investigation opportunity: Indicates where additional information would be valuable

Examples:
Unknown constraint:
constraint:
  id: constraint.performance_slo
  confidence:
    state: unknown
    score: 0.0
    evidence_refs: []  # No supporting evidence
    confidence_details:
      evidence_strength: none
      evidence_count: 0
      evidence_consistency: none
      pattern_alignment: none
  notes: |
    No performance requirements found in repository.
    May exist in external documentation or organizational policies.
Unknown goal:
goal:
  id: goal.system_purpose
  confidence:
    state: unknown
    score: 0.15
    evidence_refs:
      - evidence-017  # Vague README mention
    confidence_details:
      evidence_strength: weak
      evidence_count: 1
      evidence_consistency: low
      pattern_alignment: none
  notes: |
    README mentions "payment processing" but lacks
    comprehensive system description. Goal is partially
    inferred from capabilities.
Unknown data ownership:
data:
  id: data.transaction_log
  confidence:
    state: unknown
    score: 0.0
    evidence_refs: []
    confidence_details:
      evidence_strength: none
      evidence_count: 0
      evidence_consistency: none
      pattern_alignment: none
  notes: |
    Transaction log table exists but ownership
    cannot be determined from available artifacts.
    Multiple services may write to this table.
Confidence implications:
Unknown elements generally have: - Low confidence scores (0.0-0.4) - High review requirements (investigation needed) - No reliability for architectural analysis without additional information
Unknown elements represent knowledge gaps that should be investigated rather than speculated. They highlight areas where additional documentation, architecture review, or instrumentation would improve extraction accuracy.
crumbs: Part II — Signal Taxonomy & Evidence Model

title: 11  Confidence Semantics
text:
11.4 Confidence Derivation
Confidence classifications are not arbitrary. They are determined by analyzing the evidence associated with an architectural element. Several factors influence confidence.

11.4.1 Evidence Strength
Definition: How directly artifacts express architectural structure.
Strength levels:

Strong: Explicit encoding (schemas, routes, workflows, interfaces)
Moderate: Strong suggestions (dependencies, configuration, entrypoints)
Weak: Indirect hints (naming, structure, documentation)

Impact on confidence:

Strong evidence: Base confidence 0.7-0.9
Moderate evidence: Base confidence 0.4-0.6
Weak evidence: Base confidence 0.1-0.3

Example:
# Strong evidence (schema definition)
evidence:
  strength: strong
  excerpt: "CREATE TABLE payments (...)"
# → Base confidence: 0.85

# Moderate evidence (dependency)
evidence:
  strength: moderate
  excerpt: "celery==5.3.0"
# → Base confidence: 0.55

# Weak evidence (directory name)
evidence:
  strength: weak
  excerpt: "workers/"
# → Base confidence: 0.25


11.4.2 Evidence Quantity
Definition: Number of independent evidence objects supporting the same interpretation.
Impact on confidence:
More evidence increases confidence, but with diminishing returns:
confidence_boost = log(count(evidence))
Examples:
# Single evidence object
evidence_count: 1
confidence_boost: log(1) = 0.0

# Three evidence objects
evidence_count: 3
confidence_boost: log(3) = 0.48

# Ten evidence objects
evidence_count: 10
confidence_boost: log(10) = 1.0


11.4.3 Evidence Consistency
Definition: Whether different evidence sources agree with each other.
Consistency levels:

High: All evidence points to the same interpretation
Medium: Most evidence agrees, minor inconsistencies
Low: Significant disagreements or contradictions

Impact on confidence:

High consistency: No penalty
Medium consistency: Small penalty (-0.1)
Low consistency: Large penalty (-0.3)

Example:
# High consistency
evidence:
  - schema: "CREATE TABLE payments (...)"
  - orm: "class Payment(db.Model): ..."
  - test: "def test_payment(): ..."
consistency: high
penalty: 0.0

# Low consistency
evidence:
  - doc: "Payment service is standalone microservice"
  - code: "Payments processed inline with API"
  - config: "No payment service configuration"
consistency: low
penalty: -0.3


11.4.4 Pattern Alignment
Definition: Whether extracted structures match well-known architectural patterns.
Alignment levels:

Strong: Exact match to well-known pattern
Moderate: Similar to known pattern with variations
Weak: Some similarity to patterns
None: No recognizable pattern

Impact on confidence:

Strong alignment: Small boost (+0.1)
Moderate alignment: Tiny boost (+0.05)
Weak alignment: No boost
No alignment: No boost

Example:
# Strong pattern alignment (typical 3-tier web app)
structure:
  - API layer (HTTP routes)
  - Service layer (business logic)
  - Data layer (database)
alignment: strong
boost: +0.1

# No pattern alignment (unusual architecture)
structure:
  - Mixed responsibilities
  - Unclear boundaries
alignment: none
boost: +0.0


11.4.5 Confidence Calculation Formula
Combining all factors:
base_confidence = strength_to_confidence(strength)
evidence_boost = log(count(evidence))
consistency_penalty = consistency_to_penalty(consistency)
pattern_boost = alignment_to_boost(alignment)

final_confidence = base_confidence +
                   evidence_boost +
                   consistency_penalty +
                   pattern_boost

# Clamp to [0.0, 1.0]
final_confidence = max(0.0, min(1.0, final_confidence))
Confidence state mapping:
final_confidence >= 0.8  → state: observed
final_confidence >= 0.4  → state: inferred
final_confidence < 0.4   → state: unknown
Example calculation:
# Worker component inference
evidence:
  strength: moderate  # Base: 0.55
  count: 3           # Boost: log(3) = 0.48
  consistency: high   # Penalty: 0.0
  alignment: moderate # Boost: 0.05

final_confidence = 0.55 + 0.48 + 0.0 + 0.05 = 1.08
# Clamped: 1.0
# State: observed
crumbs: Part II — Signal Taxonomy & Evidence Model

title: 11  Confidence Semantics
text:
11.5 Confidence Propagation
Confidence may propagate through relationships within the architecture graph. When one element has a certain confidence, related elements may inherit or derive confidence from that relationship.
Propagation scenarios:
1. Interface to capability propagation:
interface:
  id: interface.payment_api
  confidence:
    state: observed
    score: 0.92

capability:
  id: capability.create_payment
  confidence:
    state: inferred
    score: 0.75  # Inherited partially from interface
Rationale: If an interface is strongly observed, the capabilities it exposes can be inferred with moderate-high confidence.
2. Component to interface propagation:
component:
  id: component.api_service
  confidence:
    state: observed
    score: 0.95

interface:
  id: interface.http_api
  confidence:
    state: observed
    score: 0.90  # Derived from component
Rationale: If a component is strongly observed, its interfaces can be inferred with high confidence.
3. Workflow to component propagation:
workflow:
  id: workflow.payment_processing
  confidence:
    state: inferred
    score: 0.65

component:
  id: component.payment_worker
  confidence:
    state: inferred
    score: 0.58  # Derived from workflow
Rationale: If a workflow is inferred, the components implementing it can be inferred with similar confidence.
Propagation rules:

Directional: Confidence propagates from stronger to weaker elements
Attenuation: Propagated confidence is attenuated (reduced by 0.1-0.2)
Non-destructive: Propagation never reduces confidence below original evidence-based level
Explicit: Propagation should be documented in confidence metadata

Propagation formula:
propagated_confidence = source_confidence - propagation_attenuation
final_confidence = max(evidence_confidence, propagated_confidence)
Where propagation_attenuation is typically 0.1-0.2.
Propagation limitations:

No upward propagation: Weak elements cannot strengthen strong elements
Evidence priority: Explicit evidence always takes precedence over propagation
Documented assumptions: Propagation assumptions must be documented
crumbs: Part II — Signal Taxonomy & Evidence Model

title: 11  Confidence Semantics
text:
11.6 Confidence and Review Prioritization
Confidence semantics guide the human review process by identifying where human attention should be focused. In large-scale extraction pipelines, manual review resources are limited, so prioritization is essential.
Review priority matrix:



Confidence
Impact
Review Priority
Review Type




High
Critical
High
Validate key assumptions


High
Non-critical
Low
Spot check


Medium
Critical
Very High
Careful validation


Medium
Non-critical
Medium
Validate interpretation


Low
Critical
Very High
Investigate, possibly add evidence


Low
Non-critical
High
Review if time permits


Unknown
Critical
Mandatory
Must resolve before use


Unknown
Non-critical
Medium
Attempt to resolve



Impact categories:
Critical impact:

Core components (API services, data stores)
Key interfaces (public APIs, event schemas)
Essential workflows (payment processing, user authentication)
Critical data (PII, financial data)

Non-critical impact:

Peripheral components (utility services, helper modules)
Optional interfaces (debug endpoints, admin tools)
Secondary workflows (reporting, maintenance)
Non-critical data (logs, metrics)

Review workflows:
High-priority review (unknown + critical impact):
review_item:
  element: constraint.security_slo
  confidence: unknown
  impact: critical
  priority: mandatory
  action: "Must investigate security requirements before deployment"
Medium-priority review (inferred + moderate impact):
review_item:
  element: component.notification_worker
  confidence: inferred
  impact: moderate
  priority: high
  action: "Validate that notification service is correctly inferred"
Low-priority review (observed + non-critical):
review_item:
  element: component.logging_utility
  confidence: observed
  impact: low
  priority: low
  action: "Spot check to verify correctness"
Review effectiveness:
Confidence-based prioritization enables:

Efficient resource allocation: Human effort focused where most valuable
Risk reduction: High-risk, low-confidence elements receive attention
Quality improvement: Systematic review of uncertain elements
Scalability: Large-scale extraction remains practical
crumbs: Part II — Signal Taxonomy & Evidence Model

title: 11  Confidence Semantics
text:
11.7 Dataset-Level Confidence Metrics
Confidence annotations also support dataset-level quality analysis. When analyzing entire blueprint datasets, confidence metrics provide insights into overall extraction quality.
Confidence distribution metrics:
dataset_metrics:
  total_elements: 15420
  confidence_distribution:
    observed:
      count: 8234
      percentage: 53.4
      average_score: 0.87
    inferred:
      count: 5123
      percentage: 33.2
      average_score: 0.62
    unknown:
      count: 2063
      percentage: 13.4
      average_score: 0.15
Quality indicators:
High-quality dataset:

Observed > 50%
Inferred ~30-40%
Unknown < 15%
Average confidence > 0.65

Medium-quality dataset:

Observed ~30-50%
Inferred ~30-40%
Unknown ~15-25%
Average confidence ~0.55-0.65

Low-quality dataset:

Observed < 30%
Inferred ~30-40%
Unknown > 25%
Average confidence < 0.55

Trend analysis:
Tracking confidence metrics over time enables:

Improvement measurement: Is extraction getting more accurate?
Methodology validation: Are new techniques improving confidence?
Dataset comparison: Which repositories produce better extractions?
Issue identification: Where are extraction methods failing?

Example trend analysis:
confidence_trends:
  monthly:
    2026-01:
      observed_percentage: 48.2
      average_confidence: 0.62
    2026-02:
      observed_percentage: 51.1
      average_confidence: 0.65
    2026-03:
      observed_percentage: 53.4
      average_confidence: 0.67

  interpretation: >
    Confidence has improved over the last three months.
    This suggests that extraction methodology improvements
    (better signal detection, improved evidence collection) are
    effectively increasing extraction accuracy.
crumbs: Part II — Signal Taxonomy & Evidence Model

title: 11  Confidence Semantics
text:
11.8 Confidence as Architectural Honesty
Confidence semantics represent a philosophical commitment to architectural honesty. Rather than presenting extraction results as definitively correct, confidence semantics acknowledge the limits of available information.
The honesty principle:

Better to admit ignorance than to pretend knowledge.

Applied to extraction:

Mark fields as unknown rather than guessing
Assign low confidence to weak interpretations
Document assumptions and uncertainties
Invite human review of uncertain elements

Benefits of honesty:
For users:

Accurate understanding: Users know what is reliable versus uncertain
Informed decisions: Users can make appropriate decisions based on confidence
Risk awareness: Users understand where architectural understanding is weak
Trust building: Honesty builds trust in extraction accuracy

For extraction systems:

Targeted improvement: Unknown fields identify methodology gaps
Quality metrics: Confidence distribution measures overall quality
Iterative refinement: Systems can focus on improving low-confidence areas
Feedback loops: Review results inform confidence calibration

For architectural datasets:

Reliability: Datasets maintain quality by excluding speculation
Transparency: Users understand dataset limitations
Useability: Users can filter by confidence for appropriate use cases
Evolution: Datasets can be systematically improved over time

The alternative: false confidence
Without confidence semantics, extraction risks:

Misleading users: Presenting speculation as fact
Incorrect decisions: Actions based on incorrect information
Wasted effort: Time spent investigating non-existent structures
Erosion of trust: Users abandon extraction when it proves unreliable

Confidence semantics prevent these problems by making uncertainty explicit and actionable.
crumbs: Part II — Signal Taxonomy & Evidence Model

title: 11  Confidence Semantics
text:
11.9 Chapter Summary

Architectural reconstruction is inherently uncertain due to missing information, implicit conventions, ambiguous evidence, and conflicts
Confidence semantics provide a formal mechanism for representing uncertainty in extracted architectures
Confidence objects include: state (observed/inferred/unknown), score, evidence references, and detailed metadata
Three canonical confidence states: observed (explicit evidence), inferred (indirect signals), unknown (insufficient evidence)
Confidence is derived from evidence strength, quantity, consistency, and pattern alignment
Confidence calculation combines factors: base confidence + evidence boost - consistency penalty + pattern boost
Confidence propagates through architectural relationships with attenuation
Confidence guides review prioritization: low-confidence, high-impact elements receive highest priority
Dataset-level confidence metrics measure overall extraction quality and trends
Confidence represents architectural honesty: better to admit ignorance than pretend knowledge
Honesty builds trust, enables targeted improvement, and prevents misleading conclusions

Next Chapter: Chapter 10 begins Part III by introducing Mapping Principles—the rules that translate repository-derived evidence into canonical system ontology entities. Mapping bridges the gap between implementation artifacts and architectural structures.
crumbs: Part II — Signal Taxonomy & Evidence Model

title: 12  Mapping Principles and Canonicalization
text:
12.1 The Mapping Challenge
Repository artifacts do not directly correspond to system ontology elements. Instead, extraction must translate implementation structures into canonical architectural entities and relationships.
This translation process is called mapping. Mapping converts repository-derived evidence into the standardized vocabulary defined by the system ontology. The goal is to produce architectural representations that remain consistent across repositories, languages, and frameworks.
Without mapping, extraction would produce repository-specific representations that cannot be compared, analyzed, or aggregated. With mapping, extraction produces canonical representations that enable large-scale architecture datasets, pattern discovery, and architectural intelligence.
Repositories exhibit enormous diversity in how they represent the same architectural concepts. This diversity presents the core mapping challenge: how to translate diverse implementation representations into consistent architectural entities?
Diversity examples:
Same concept, different representations:
Variation dimensions:
The mapping objective:
Transform this diversity into canonical architectural representations:
Where “canonical” means: - Standardized: Using consistent terminology and structure - Technology-agnostic: Independent of language or framework - Architectural: Focused on structure rather than implementation - Comparable: Enabling cross-repository analysis
crumbs: Part III — Ontology Mapping: From Repo to System Graph

API Interface
FastAPI route
HTTP handler
(RestController?)
Express route


Data Entity
SQLAlchemy model
struct
JPA entity
Mongoose schema


Configuration
.env file
struct tags
(Configuration?)
config.json


Workflow
Airflow DAG
Cron job
(Scheduled?)
Bull queue





Language-specific syntax: Different languages use different constructs
Framework conventions: Different frameworks encourage different patterns
Organizational standards: Different teams use different naming and organization
Evolutionary drift: Systems evolve without updating conventions
Implementation preferences: Developers choose different approaches for the same problem



Diverse Implementation Artifacts
              ↓
           Mapping
              ↓
Canonical Ontology Entities
crumbs: Part III — Ontology Mapping: From Repo to System Graph

title: 12  Mapping Principles and Canonicalization
text:
12.2 Interpretability First
Mapping rules must remain interpretable. This is a non-negotiable requirement for reliable extraction systems.
Why interpretability matters:

Validation: Reviewers must verify that mappings are correct
Debugging: Errors must be traced to specific mapping rules
Evolution: Mappings must be improved over time
Trust: Users must understand how extraction produces results

Opaque mappings are problematic:
# Bad: Opaque mapping rule
def map_component(evidence):
    result = complex_ml_model(evidence)
    return result  # Black box, unexplainable
Interpretable mappings are necessary:
# Good: Interpretable mapping rule
def map_component(evidence):
    if evidence.type == 'route_definition':
        return Component(type='Service', role='API')
    elif evidence.type == 'worker_implementation':
        return Component(type='Worker', role='Background')
    elif evidence.type == 'scheduler_config':
        return Component(type='Scheduler', role='Orchestration')
    else:
        return Component(type='Unknown', role='Unknown')
Interpretability characteristics:
1. Explicit rules:
mapping_rules:
  - rule: route_to_service_component
    condition:
      artifact_type: code
      pattern: "@app.route|@GetMapping|http.HandleFunc"
    mapping:
      entity_type: Component
      subtype: Service
      role: API
2. Documented rationale:
mapping_rules:
  - rule: route_to_service_component
    rationale: |
      HTTP route definitions indicate that the containing
      component is a service that exposes an API interface.
      This is a consistent pattern across most web frameworks.
3. Reviewable logic:
mapping_rules:
  - rule: queue_to_worker_component
    logic:
      - detect queue dependency
      - detect consumer implementation
      - confirm processing pattern
    decision: component_type == 'Worker'
4. Debuggable outputs:
mapping_result:
  input_evidence: evidence-001
  applied_rule: route_to_service_component
  intermediate_steps:
    - detected: "@app.route('/payments')"
    - matched: route_definition_pattern
    - classified: API service
  output_entity: component.api_service
  confidence: 0.92
When machine learning is used:
Even when machine learning or pattern recognition assists extraction, the final mapping must still be expressed through clear, explainable rules:
ml_assisted_mapping:
  model_prediction:
    entity_type: Component
    subtype: Worker
    confidence: 0.78
  post_processing_rules:
    - rule: validate_worker_indicators
      checks:
        - queue_dependency_present
        - consumer_implementation_exists
        - background_execution_pattern
    final_decision:
      entity_type: Component
      subtype: Worker
      confidence: 0.85
      rationale: |
        ML prediction validated by worker-specific
        indicators. All checks passed.
Interpretability ensures that:

Mappings can be reviewed and validated
Errors can be debugged and corrected
Mappings can be improved systematically
Extraction results can be trusted
crumbs: Part III — Ontology Mapping: From Repo to System Graph

title: 12  Mapping Principles and Canonicalization
text:
12.3 Naming Canonicalization
Repositories frequently use inconsistent naming conventions. Different projects may refer to the same architectural concept using different terms. Naming canonicalization resolves these inconsistencies by translating repository-specific names into standardized ontology labels.
The naming problem:
Same concept, different names:



Concept
Repository A
Repository B
Repository C




Background processing
worker
consumer
processor


Payment processing
payment
billing
transaction


User management
user
account
customer


Data storage
storage
database
persistence


API layer
api
interface
endpoint



Canonicalization examples:
canonicalization_rules:
  # Component type normalization
  worker:
    variants: [worker, consumer, processor, job-runner, task]
    canonical: WorkerComponent

  service:
    variants: [service, server, application, app]
    canonical: ServiceComponent

  scheduler:
    variants: [scheduler, orchestrator, coordinator, cron]
    canonical: SchedulerComponent

  # Interface type normalization
  api:
    variants: [api, endpoint, route, handler]
    canonical: APIInterface

  event:
    variants: [event, message, topic, queue]
    canonical: EventInterface

  cli:
    variants: [cli, command, tool, utility]
    canonical: CLIInterface

  # Data type normalization
  table:
    variants: [table, entity, model, document]
    canonical: TableEntity

  collection:
    variants: [collection, bucket, container]
    canonical: CollectionEntity
Canonicalization process:
canonicalization_pipeline:
  input:
    repository_name: "PaymentProcessorService"
    detected_type: service

  step_1_normalize:
    lowercase: "paymentprocessorservice"
    remove_suffixes: "paymentprocessor"

  step_2_tokenization:
    tokens: ["payment", "processor"]

  step_3_mapping:
    payment -> canonical: "Payment"
    processor -> canonical: "Processor"

  step_4_assembly:
    canonical_name: "PaymentProcessorService"

  output:
    canonical_id: component.payment_processor_service
    canonical_name: PaymentProcessorService
    canonical_type: ServiceComponent
Benefits of canonical naming:

Searchability: Users can find related components across repositories
Comparability: Architectures can be compared despite different naming
Analysis: Pattern mining works on consistent terminology
Clarity: Canonical names have clear, unambiguous meaning

Handling ambiguous names:
ambiguous_name_handling:
  input: "service"

  context_analysis:
    - check: route_definitions_present
      if_true: canonical: ServiceComponent
    - check: worker_implementation_present
      if_true: canonical: WorkerComponent
    - check: scheduler_config_present
      if_true: canonical: SchedulerComponent

  default:
    if_no_context: canonical: UnknownComponent
    confidence: low
    notes: "Ambiguous term 'service' without context"
crumbs: Part III — Ontology Mapping: From Repo to System Graph

title: 12  Mapping Principles and Canonicalization
text:
12.4 Type Canonicalization
Beyond naming differences, repositories may represent similar architectural elements using different technical constructs. Type canonicalization abstracts these variations into common architectural types defined in the system ontology.
The type problem:
Same architectural role, different implementation:



Architectural Role
Python
Go
Java
JavaScript




Async processing
Celery
channel
ExecutorService
Worker


HTTP interface
FastAPI
net/http
Spring Boot
Express


Data persistence
SQLAlchemy
sqlx
Hibernate
Mongoose


Configuration
.env
struct
(Value?)
config.json


Logging
logging
log
SLF4J
console.log



Type canonicalization examples:
type_canonicalization_rules:
  # Async processing canonicalization
  async_processing:
    implementations:
      - celery: Python
      - bull: Node.js
      - sidekiq: Ruby
      - channel: Go
      - ExecutorService: Java
    canonical: WorkflowExecutionNode

  # HTTP interface canonicalization
  http_interface:
    implementations:
      - fastapi: Python
      - express: Node.js
      - http.HandleFunc: Go
      - @RestController: Java
      - @app.route: Python
    canonical: APIInterface

  # Data persistence canonicalization
  data_persistence:
    implementations:
      - sqlalchemy: Python
      - orm: Python
      - gorm: Go
      - hibernate: Java
      - mongoose: Node.js
    canonical: DataEntity
Canonicalization example:
input_evidence:
  repository: python-payment-system
  implementation:
    framework: celery
    code: |
      @app.task
      def process_payment(payment_id):
          # Process payment
          pass

canonicalization:
  detected_pattern: celery_task
  mapped_to: WorkflowExecutionNode
  canonical_entity:
    type: WorkflowExecutionNode
    role: BackgroundProcessing
    implementation: celery
  confidence: 0.92
Canonicalization benefits:

Abstraction: Focus on architectural role, not implementation
Comparison: Compare architectures across technology stacks
Pattern mining: Find patterns despite implementation differences
Flexibility: Accommodate new implementations without changing ontology

Preserving implementation details:
While canonicalizing types, we preserve implementation details as metadata:
canonical_entity:
  id: workflow.payment_processing
  type: WorkflowExecutionNode
  role: BackgroundProcessing
  canonical_type: WorkflowExecutionNode

  implementation_metadata:
    original_implementation: celery
    language: python
    framework: celery
    version: "5.3.0"
This allows both canonical analysis (what it is architecturally) and implementation-aware analysis (how it’s implemented).
crumbs: Part III — Ontology Mapping: From Repo to System Graph

title: 12  Mapping Principles and Canonicalization
text:
12.5 Relation Canonicalization
Architectural relationships between entities also require normalization. Repositories may express relationships in many forms, including function calls, configuration references, dependency declarations, and event subscriptions.
The relationship problem:
Same relationship, different representations:



Relationship
Code
Config
Dependency
Event




Depends on
import
import:
requires:
subscribes:


Exposes
export
routes:
provides:
publishes:


Reads from
query
db:
uses:
reads:


Writes to
insert
db:
modifies:
writes:



Canonical relationship types:
canonical_relationships:
  # Component relationships
  component_exposes_interface:
    variants: [exposes, provides, exports, routes]
    canonical: exposes

  component_depends_on_component:
    variants: [depends, imports, requires, uses]
    canonical: depends

  component_owns_data:
    variants: [owns, manages, controls, writes]
    canonical: owns

  component_reads_data:
    variants: [reads, queries, accesses, retrieves]
    canonical: reads

  # Workflow relationships
  workflow_triggers_component:
    variants: [triggers, invokes, executes, calls]
    canonical: triggers

  workflow_contains_task:
    variants: [contains, includes, has, steps]
    canonical: contains

  # Data relationships
  data_stored_in:
    variants: [stored_in, persisted_in, saved_to]
    canonical: stored_in

  data_accessed_by:
    variants: [accessed_by, read_by, queried_by]
    canonical: accessed_by
Relation canonicalization example:
input_relationship:
  repository: go-microservice
  source: "import 'github.com/user/payments'"
  relationship_type: import

canonicalization:
  detected_pattern: go_import
  mapped_to: component_depends_on_component
  canonical_relationship:
    source: component.order_service
    relation: depends
    target: component.payment_service
    type: ComponentDependency
  confidence: 0.95
Canonicalization process:
relationship_canonicalization_pipeline:
  input:
    raw_relationship: "import payments"
    context: source=order_service, target=payments

  step_1_classification:
    pattern_match: import_statement
    category: dependency

  step_2_semantic_analysis:
    source_type: Component
    target_type: Component
    semantic_role: dependency

  step_3_canonical_mapping:
    canonical_relation: depends

  step_4_confidence_assessment:
    evidence: explicit_import
    confidence: 0.95

  output:
    canonical_relationship:
      source: component.order_service
      relation: depends
      target: component.payment_service
      confidence: 0.95
Benefits of relation canonicalization:

Consistency: Same relationship expressed consistently across systems
Analysis: Relationship patterns can be mined across repositories
Validation: Architectural constraints can be checked
Visualization: Consistent graphs can be generated
crumbs: Part III — Ontology Mapping: From Repo to System Graph

title: 12  Mapping Principles and Canonicalization
text:
12.6 Entity and Relation Graphs
The result of mapping and canonicalization is a pair of structured graphs: the entity graph and the relation graph. Together, these graphs form the structural foundation of the blueprint.

12.6.1 Entity Graph
The entity graph contains the architectural entities identified during extraction.
Entity graph structure:
entity_graph:
  nodes:
    - id: component.api_service
      type: Component
      subtype: Service
      role: API
      capabilities:
        - capability.create_payment
        - capability.get_payment
      interfaces:
        - interface.http_api
      data:
        - data.payments
      confidence: 0.95

    - id: component.payment_worker
      type: Component
      subtype: Worker
      role: Background
      capabilities:
        - capability.process_payment
      interfaces:
        - interface.queue
      data:
        - data.payments
      confidence: 0.88

    - id: component.notification_service
      type: Component
      subtype: Service
      role: Notification
      capabilities:
        - capability.send_notification
      interfaces:
        - interface.http_api
        - interface.email
      confidence: 0.82
Entity types:
entity_types:
  Component:
    subtypes: [Service, Worker, Scheduler, Plugin]
    fields: [id, type, subtype, role, capabilities, interfaces, data]

  Interface:
    subtypes: [API, Event, CLI, RPC]
    fields: [id, type, subtype, contract, protocol]

  Data:
    subtypes: [Table, Collection, Document]
    fields: [id, type, subtype, schema, ownership]

  Workflow:
    subtypes: [Pipeline, DAG, Schedule]
    fields: [id, type, subtype, tasks, triggers]

  Capability:
    subtypes: [Functional, Operational]
    fields: [id, type, description, interfaces]


12.6.2 Relation Graph
The relation graph represents the relationships between entities.
Relation graph structure:
relation_graph:
  edges:
    - source: component.api_service
      relation: exposes
      target: interface.http_api
      type: ComponentExposesInterface
      confidence: 0.95

    - source: component.api_service
      relation: owns
      target: data.payments
      type: ComponentOwnsData
      confidence: 0.92

    - source: component.api_service
      relation: depends
      target: component.notification_service
      type: ComponentDependency
      confidence: 0.88

    - source: workflow.payment_processing
      relation: triggers
      target: component.payment_worker
      type: WorkflowTriggersComponent
      confidence: 0.85

    - source: component.payment_worker
      relation: reads
      target: data.payments
      type: ComponentReadsData
      confidence: 0.90
Relation types:
relation_types:
  ComponentExposesInterface:
    direction: component → interface
    semantics: component provides access through interface

  ComponentOwnsData:
    direction: component → data
    semantics: component is primary authority for data

  ComponentReadsData:
    direction: component → data
    semantics: component reads but does not own data

  ComponentDependency:
    direction: component → component
    semantics: source depends on target

  WorkflowTriggersComponent:
    direction: workflow → component
    semantics: workflow invokes component

  WorkflowContainsTask:
    direction: workflow → task
    semantics: workflow includes task in execution


12.6.3 Combined Graph Structure
The entity and relation graphs together form a complete architectural representation:
blueprint_graph:
  entities:
    component.api_service:
      type: Component
      subtype: Service
      role: API
      relations:
        - exposes: interface.http_api
        - owns: data.payments
        - depends: component.notification_service

    component.payment_worker:
      type: Component
      subtype: Worker
      role: Background
      relations:
        - triggered_by: workflow.payment_processing
        - reads: data.payments

  relationships:
    component.api_service → interface.http_api: exposes
    component.api_service → data.payments: owns
    component.api_service → component.notification_service: depends
    workflow.payment_processing → component.payment_worker: triggers
    component.payment_worker → data.payments: reads
Graph properties:

Directed: Relationships have direction (source → target)
Typed: Each relationship has a canonical type
Weighted: Relationships have confidence scores
Attributed: Relationships include metadata and evidence references
crumbs: Part III — Ontology Mapping: From Repo to System Graph

title: 12  Mapping Principles and Canonicalization
text:
12.7 Mapping Rules Interface
To ensure consistency and interoperability, mapping rules follow a standardized interface. This interface defines how mapping rules are specified, applied, and validated.
Mapping rule structure:
mapping_rule:
  id: rule_id
  version: "1.0"
  name: "Descriptive rule name"

  description: |
    Clear explanation of what this rule does and why.

  detection:
    artifact_types: [code, config, schema, deploy]
    patterns:
      - pattern: "@app.route|@GetMapping|http.HandleFunc"
        language: regex
      - pattern: fastapi|flask|django
        language: string_match

  mapping:
    entity_type: Component
    entity_subtype: Service
    entity_role: API
    canonical_type: ServiceComponent

  confidence:
    base_confidence: 0.8
    boost_conditions:
      - condition: openapi_spec_present
        boost: 0.1
      - condition: test_coverage_present
        boost: 0.05

  evidence_requirements:
    min_evidence: 1
    preferred_evidence: 2
    required_types: [code, config]

  validation:
    required_context:
      - route_definitions
      - server_initialization
    forbidden_context:
      - static_file_serving

  examples:
    - input:
        code: "@app.route('/payments')"
      output:
        entity_type: Component
        entity_subtype: Service
    - input:
        code: "@GetMapping('/payments')"
      output:
        entity_type: Component
        entity_subtype: Service

  metadata:
    author: "Blueprint Extraction Team"
    created: "2026-03-08"
    last_modified: "2026-03-08"
Rule application process:
rule_application:
  input_evidence:
    id: evidence-001
    artifact_type: code
    excerpt: "@app.route('/payments')"

  step_1_rule_matching:
    matched_rules:
      - rule: route_to_service_component
        match_score: 0.95

  step_2_context_validation:
    context_checks:
      - check: server_initialization_present
        result: true
      - check: static_file_serving
        result: false

  step_3_mapping:
    mapping_result:
      entity_type: Component
      entity_subtype: Service
      entity_role: API

  step_4_confidence_calculation:
    base_confidence: 0.8
    context_boost: 0.0
    final_confidence: 0.8

  step_5_output:
    entity:
      id: component.api_service
      type: Component
      subtype: Service
      role: API
      confidence: 0.8
      evidence_refs: [evidence-001]
Rule validation:
Mapping rules must be validated before use:
rule_validation:
  required_fields:
    - id ✓
    - name ✓
    - detection ✓
    - mapping ✓

  semantic_validation:
    - detection_patterns_valid: true
    - mapping_entity_type_valid: true
    - confidence_range_valid: true

  operational_validation:
    - rule_executable: true
    - produces_valid_output: true
    - evidence_traceable: true

  validation_result: PASSED
crumbs: Part III — Ontology Mapping: From Repo to System Graph

title: 12  Mapping Principles and Canonicalization
text:
12.8 Mapping Best Practices
Effective mapping follows several best practices that ensure quality, maintainability, and reliability.

12.8.1 1. Start Simple, Add Complexity Gradually
# Start with simple rule
mapping_rule_v1:
  detection:
    pattern: "@app.route"
  mapping:
    entity_type: Component

# Add complexity over time
mapping_rule_v2:
  detection:
    pattern: "@app.route|@GetMapping|http.HandleFunc"
    language: "web framework"
  mapping:
    entity_type: Component
    entity_subtype: Service
  context_validation:
    - server_initialization_required
  confidence_calculation:
    base: 0.8
    boost: test_coverage * 0.1


12.8.2 2. Document Everything
mapping_rule:
  documentation:
    purpose: "Identify API service components"
    rationale: |
      HTTP route definitions indicate API services because
      they expose HTTP interfaces. This pattern is consistent
      across web frameworks.
    assumptions:
      - Route definitions indicate external interfaces
      - Server initialization confirms service boundary
    known_limitations:
      - May misidentify internal-only routes
      - Requires context to distinguish services from modules
    validation_approach: "Check for server initialization"


12.8.3 3. Handle Edge Cases
mapping_rule:
  edge_case_handling:
    - case: "static file serving"
      detection: "@app.route('/static')"
      handling: |
        Static file routes do not indicate API services.
        Exclude this pattern or downgrade confidence.
      action: exclude

    - case: "internal routes"
      detection: "@app.route('/_internal')"
      handling: |
        Internal routes may indicate internal services or
        just internal endpoints. Require additional context.
      action: lower_confidence_by_0.2

    - case: "framework defaults"
      detection: "@app.route('/')"
      handling: |
        Root route alone insufficient. Look for additional
        routes or service-specific configuration.
      action: require_additional_evidence


12.8.4 4. Use Confidence Appropriately
mapping_rule:
  confidence_strategy:
    high_confidence:
      conditions:
        - explicit_route_definition
        - server_initialization
        - openapi_specification
      confidence: 0.9+

    medium_confidence:
      conditions:
        - explicit_route_definition
        - server_initialization
      confidence: 0.7-0.9

    low_confidence:
      conditions:
        - route_definition_only
        - no_server_context
      confidence: 0.4-0.7

    no_confidence:
      conditions:
        - conflicting_evidence
      confidence: 0.0-0.4


12.8.5 5. Preserve Evidence Traceability
mapping_rule:
  evidence_preservation:
    required_evidence_types:
      - code (route definitions)
      - config (server configuration)

    optional_evidence_types:
      - schema (OpenAPI spec)
      - test (API tests)

    evidence_linking:
      - entity.evidence_refs: all_supporting_evidence
      - entity.confidence_details: evidence_analysis
      - entity.mapping_details: rule_application_log
crumbs: Part III — Ontology Mapping: From Repo to System Graph

title: 12  Mapping Principles and Canonicalization
text:
12.9 Mapping as Structural Translation
Mapping and canonicalization transform repository evidence into standardized architectural structures. This translation is essential for producing useful, comparable blueprints.
Translation process summary:
Repository Evidence
        ↓
    Mapping Rules
        ↓
  Interpretability
        ↓
 Naming Canonicalization
        ↓
  Type Canonicalization
        ↓
Relation Canonicalization
        ↓
   Entity Graph
        ↓
  Relation Graph
        ↓
Canonical Blueprint
Key transformations:

Interpretability: Ensure mappings are explainable and reviewable
Naming normalization: Standardize names across repositories
Type normalization: Abstract implementation differences
Relation normalization: Standardize relationship representations
Graph construction: Assemble entities and relations into structured graphs

The transformation payoff:
Canonical entity and relation graphs provide a canonical architectural representation that can be used to:

Populate blueprint kernels with standardized entities
Generate blueprint views through projection rules
Support large-scale architecture analysis and comparison
Enable architecture pattern mining and discovery
Build architecture intelligence systems

Mapping is therefore the bridge that transforms implementation-specific observations into architecture-independent understanding.
crumbs: Part III — Ontology Mapping: From Repo to System Graph

title: 12  Mapping Principles and Canonicalization
text:
12.10 Chapter Summary

Mapping translates repository artifacts into canonical architectural entities and relationships
The mapping challenge arises from diversity in languages, frameworks, and conventions
Interpretability is a non-negotiable requirement for mapping rules
Naming canonicalization standardizes entity names across repositories
Type canonicalization abstracts implementation differences into architectural types
Relation canonicalization standardizes relationship representations
Mapping produces entity graphs (architectural entities) and relation graphs (relationships)
Mapping rules follow a standardized interface with detection, mapping, confidence, and validation
Best practices include starting simple, documenting everything, handling edge cases, using confidence appropriately, and preserving evidence traceability
Mapping transforms implementation-specific observations into architecture-independent understanding
Canonical graphs enable blueprint population, view generation, and large-scale architecture analysis

Next Chapter: Chapter 11 begins the detailed extraction methodology with Component Discovery—identifying and classifying the structural units of responsibility within a system. This chapter provides concrete examples and detailed methodology for identifying service, worker, scheduler, and plugin components.
crumbs: Part III — Ontology Mapping: From Repo to System Graph

title: 13  Component Discovery
text:
13.1 What Is a Component?
Components represent the structural units of responsibility within a system. They implement capabilities, expose interfaces, operate on data, and participate in workflows. During blueprint extraction, identifying components is one of the most critical steps because components define the structural decomposition of the system.
However, repositories rarely declare components explicitly. Component discovery therefore relies on identifying structural signals that indicate the presence of distinct execution or responsibility units. The goal is to produce a component inventory: a structured list of architectural components inferred from repository evidence.
Component discovery establishes the structural framework upon which the remainder of the blueprint is constructed. Once components are identified, subsequent extraction stages can determine interfaces, data ownership, and workflows.
Before discovering components, we must define what a component is in the context of blueprint extraction. Components are not arbitrary code units; they represent architecturally meaningful structural units.
Component characteristics:
1. Structural unit of responsibility:
A component represents a cohesive set of responsibilities that are logically grouped together. These responsibilities typically address a specific domain of functionality.
Example:
2. Exposes interfaces:
Components expose interfaces through which they interact with external actors or other components.
Example:
3. Operates on data:
Components own, read, or modify data entities as part of their responsibilities.
Example:
4. Participates in workflows:
Components execute tasks within orchestrated workflows or respond to events.
Example:
Component vs. module:
A component is not merely a code module. While modules organize code within a repository, components represent architectural boundaries that may span multiple modules or be contained within a single module.
Component types:
The component ontology defines several archetypes that appear frequently in software systems:
crumbs: Part III — Ontology Mapping: From Repo to System Graph

title: 13  Component Discovery
text:
component:
  id: component.payment_service
  responsibilities:
    - "Process payment transactions"
    - "Validate payment requests"
    - "Interact with payment gateways"
    - "Maintain payment records"



component:
  id: component.payment_service
  interfaces:
    - id: interface.http_api
      type: API
      protocol: HTTP
    - id: interface.payment_gateway
      type: RPC
      protocol: gRPC



component:
  id: component.payment_service
  data:
    - id: data.payments
      relationship: owns
    - id: data.payment_logs
      relationship: reads



component:
  id: component.payment_service
  workflows:
    - id: workflow.payment_processing
      role: primary
    - id: workflow.settlement
      role: participant


# Module: code organization
module:
  id: payment.models
  type: Python module
  contains: [Payment, Transaction, Refund]

# Component: architectural boundary
component:
  id: component.payment_service
  type: Service
  contains:
    - payment.models
    - payment.routes
    - payment.services
  responsibilities: [payment processing]


component_types:
  Service:
    role: Externally reachable application unit
    examples: [API services, web applications, microservices]
    typical_interfaces: [HTTP API, GraphQL API, gRPC]

  Worker:
    role: Asynchronous processing unit
    examples: [Background jobs, message consumers, task processors]
    typical_interfaces: [Queue, Topic, Event stream]

  Scheduler:
    role: Temporal coordination component
    examples: [Cron schedulers, workflow engines, job orchestrators]
    typical_interfaces: [Temporal trigger, DAG definition]

  Plugin:
    role: Extensibility mechanism
    examples: [Dynamic modules, extensions, add-ons]
    typical_interfaces: [Plugin API, registration mechanism]
crumbs: Part III — Ontology Mapping: From Repo to System Graph

title: 13  Component Discovery
text:
13.2 Component Discovery Strategy
Component discovery proceeds through a systematic strategy that combines signal detection, pattern matching, and evidence aggregation.
Discovery pipeline:
component_discovery_pipeline:
  step_1_signal_detection:
    action: "Scan repository for component signals"
    outputs:
      - structure_signals
      - dependency_signals
      - entrypoint_signals
      - config_signals
      - deploy_signals

  step_2_pattern_matching:
    action: "Match signals to component archetypes"
    outputs:
      - component_candidates
      - candidate_confidence_scores
      - supporting_evidence

  step_3_evidence_aggregation:
    action: "Aggregate evidence for each candidate"
    outputs:
      - component_evidence_sets
      - evidence_conflict_detection
      - aggregated_confidence_scores

  step_4_boundary_inference:
    action: "Infer component boundaries"
    outputs:
      - component_boundaries
      - responsibility_mapping
      - interface_associations

  step_5_inventory_generation:
    action: "Generate final component inventory"
    outputs:
      - component_inventory
      - confidence_assignments
      - evidence_references
Discovery principles:
1. Multi-signal approach:
No single signal is sufficient for component discovery. Multiple signals must converge on the same component interpretation.
multi_signal_detection:
  component: component.payment_worker

  signals_detected:
    - signal: queue_dependency
      strength: strong
    - signal: worker_directory
      strength: moderate
    - signal: task_definition
      strength: strong
    - signal: deployment_config
      strength: strong

  interpretation:
    type: WorkerComponent
    confidence: 0.92
    rationale: "Strong convergence of worker-related signals"
2. Boundary detection:
Component boundaries are inferred from structural and operational signals rather than explicitly declared.
boundary_detection:
  detected_boundary: api/ directory
  supporting_signals:
    - signal: directory_isolation
    - signal: independent_routes
    - signal: separate_config
    - signal: dedicated_entrypoint
  confidence: 0.88
  rationale: "Structural isolation with independent execution"
3. Responsibility inference:
Component responsibilities are derived from associated artifacts and interfaces.
responsibility_inference:
  component: component.payment_service
  detected_responsibilities:
    - source: HTTP routes
      responsibility: "Process payment requests"
      confidence: 0.95
    - source: Database operations
      responsibility: "Manage payment records"
      confidence: 0.90
    - source: Gateway integration
      responsibility: "Process payments"
      confidence: 0.88
crumbs: Part III — Ontology Mapping: From Repo to System Graph

title: 13  Component Discovery
text:
13.3 Service Component Discovery
Service components represent externally reachable application units. They typically appear as web servers, APIs, or application entrypoints that expose functionality through network interfaces.
Service component signals:
1. HTTP routing frameworks:
# FastAPI service
from fastapi import FastAPI
app = FastAPI()

@app.post("/payments")
def create_payment(request: PaymentRequest):
    return {"payment_id": payment.id}

if __name__ == "__main__":
    import uvicorn
    uvicorn.run(app, host="0.0.0.0", port=8000)

}

@RestController
@RequestMapping("/payments")
public class PaymentController {

}

title: 13  Component Discovery
text:
13.4 Worker Component Discovery
Worker components represent asynchronous processing units. They typically process queued tasks, background jobs, or event streams and execute outside the request-response cycle of HTTP services.
Worker component signals:
1. Message queue consumers:
# Celery worker
from celery import Celery

app = Celery('tasks', broker='redis://localhost:6379')

@app.task
def process_payment(payment_id):
    # Process payment asynchronously
    payment = Payment.query.get(payment_id)
    payment.status = 'processing'
    payment.process()
    payment.status = 'completed'
    db.session.commit()
    return payment.id

# Worker startup
if __name__ == '__main__':
    app.worker_main(['worker', '--loglevel=info'])
detected_component:
  id: component.payment_worker
  type: Worker
  subtype: QueueWorker
  signals:
    - type: dependency
      artifact: requirements.txt
      pattern: "celery"
      strength: strong
    - type: code
      artifact: worker.py
      pattern: "@app.task"
      strength: strong
    - type: entrypoint
      artifact: worker.py
      pattern: "app.worker_main"
      strength: strong
  confidence: 0.92
2. Background task frameworks:
// Bull queue worker
const Queue = require('bull');
const queue = new Queue('payment-processing', 'redis://localhost:6379');

queue.process('process-payment', async (job) => {
    const payment = await Payment.findById(job.data.paymentId);
    payment.status = 'processing';
    await payment.process();
    payment.status = 'completed';
    await payment.save();
    return payment.id;
});

// Worker startup
queue.on('completed', (job) => {
    console.log(`Job ${job.id} completed`);
});
detected_component:
  id: component.payment_worker
  type: Worker
  subtype: QueueWorker
  signals:
    - type: dependency
      artifact: package.json
      pattern: "bull"
      strength: strong
    - type: code
      artifact: worker.js
      pattern: "queue.process"
      strength: strong
    - type: code
      artifact: worker.js
      pattern: "async (job) =>"
      strength: strong
  confidence: 0.90
3. Job processing libraries:
# Sidekiq worker
class PaymentWorker
  include Sidekiq::Worker

  def perform(payment_id)
    payment = Payment.find(payment_id)
    payment.status = 'processing'
    payment.process!
    payment.status = 'completed'
    payment.save!
  end
end

# Worker startup
PaymentWorker.perform_async(payment.id)

  id: component.payment_worker

title: 13  Component Discovery
text:
13.5 Scheduler Component Discovery
Scheduler components coordinate time-based execution. They trigger workflows, pipelines, or periodic jobs based on schedules, time intervals, or temporal conditions.
Scheduler component signals:
1. Cron configurations:
# crontab
# Process payments every 5 minutes
*/5 * * * * /usr/bin/python /app/scripts/process_pending_payments.py

# Send daily payment reports
0 8 * * * /usr/bin/python /app/scripts/daily_report.py

# Weekly settlement
0 2 * * 0 /usr/bin/python /app/scripts/weekly_settlement.py
detected_component:
  id: component.payment_scheduler
  type: Scheduler
  subtype: CronScheduler
  signals:
    - type: config
      artifact: crontab
      pattern: "*/5 * * * *"
      strength: strong
    - type: config
      artifact: crontab
      pattern: "process_pending_payments.py"
      strength: moderate
  confidence: 0.88
2. DAG definitions in workflow systems:
# Airflow DAG
from airflow import DAG
from datetime import datetime, timedelta

default_args = {
    'owner': 'payments',
    'depends_on_past': False,
    'start_date': datetime(2026, 1, 1),
}

dag = DAG(
    'payment_processing',
    default_args=default_args,
    schedule_interval='@hourly',
    catchup=False,
)

task1 = PythonOperator(
    task_id='fetch_payments',
    python_callable=fetch_payments,
    dag=dag,
)

task2 = PythonOperator(
    task_id='process_payments',
    python_callable=process_payments,
    dag=dag,
)

task1 >> task2
detected_component:
  id: component.payment_scheduler
  type: Scheduler
  subtype: WorkflowScheduler
  signals:
    - type: dependency
      artifact: requirements.txt
      pattern: "airflow"
      strength: strong
    - type: code
      artifact: payment_dag.py
      pattern: "DAG("
      strength: strong
    - type: code
      artifact: payment_dag.py
      pattern: "schedule_interval='@hourly'"
      strength: strong
  confidence: 0.92
3. Scheduling frameworks:
# APScheduler
from apscheduler.schedulers.background import BackgroundScheduler

scheduler = BackgroundScheduler()

@scheduler.scheduled_job('cron', hour=0, minute=0)
def daily_settlement():
    # Run settlement at midnight
    settlement_service.settle()

@scheduler.scheduled_job('interval', minutes=5)
def process_pending():
    # Process every 5 minutes
    payment_service.process_pending()

scheduler.start()
detected_component:
  id: component.payment_scheduler
  type: Scheduler
  subtype: TaskScheduler
  signals:
    - type: dependency
      artifact: requirements.txt
      pattern: "apscheduler"
      strength: strong
    - type: code
      artifact: scheduler.py
      pattern: "BackgroundScheduler()"
      strength: strong
    - type: code
      artifact: scheduler.py
      pattern: "@scheduler.scheduled_job"
      strength: strong
  confidence: 0.90
4. Task orchestration configurations:
# Nomad job
job "payment-scheduler" {
  datacenters = ["dc1"]

  group "scheduler" {
    task "scheduler" {
      driver = "exec"

      config {
        command = "/usr/bin/python"
        args = ["/app/scheduler.py"]
      }

      resources {
        cpu    = 500
        memory = 256
      }
    }
  }
}
detected_component:
  id: component.payment_scheduler
  type: Scheduler
  subtype: JobScheduler
  signals:
    - type: deploy
      artifact: scheduler.nomad
      pattern: "job \"payment-scheduler\""
      strength: strong
    - type: deploy
      artifact: scheduler.nomad
      pattern: "command = \"/usr/bin/python\""
      strength: moderate
  confidence: 0.85

  required_signals:

      strength: strong

title: 13  Component Discovery
text:
13.6 Plugin Component Discovery
Plugin components represent extensibility mechanisms that allow systems to dynamically load additional behavior. Plugins enable modular architecture and third-party extensions.
Plugin component signals:
1. Plugin directories:
project/
├── core/
│   ├── app.py
│   └── plugin_loader.py
└── plugins/
    ├── payment_gateway_stripe/
    │   ├── __init__.py
    │   └── stripe.py
    ├── payment_gateway_paypal/
    │   ├── __init__.py
    │   └── paypal.py
    └── payment_gateway_braintree/
        ├── __init__.py
        └── braintree.py
detected_component:
  id: component.payment_gateway_plugin
  type: Plugin
  subtype: PaymentGateway
  signals:
    - type: structure
      artifact: plugins/
      pattern: "plugins/ directory"
      strength: moderate
    - type: structure
      artifact: plugins/payment_gateway_stripe/
      pattern: "plugin subdirectory"
      strength: moderate
  confidence: 0.75
2. Dynamic module loading:
# Plugin loader
import importlib
import os

class PluginManager:
    def __init__(self):
        self.plugins = {}

    def load_plugins(self, plugin_dir):
        for plugin_name in os.listdir(plugin_dir):
            plugin_path = os.path.join(plugin_dir, plugin_name)
            if os.path.isdir(plugin_path):
                module = importlib.import_module(f"plugins.{plugin_name}")
                self.plugins[plugin_name] = module.load()

# Usage
manager = PluginManager()
manager.load_plugins('plugins')
detected_component:
  id: component.payment_gateway_plugin
  type: Plugin
  subtype: PaymentGateway
  signals:
    - type: code
      artifact: plugin_loader.py
      pattern: "importlib.import_module"
      strength: strong
    - type: code
      artifact: plugin_loader.py
      pattern: "def load_plugins"
      strength: strong
  confidence: 0.85
3. Extension registration mechanisms:
# Plugin registration
class PaymentGatewayPlugin:
    @classmethod
    def register(cls, app):
        app.register_payment_gateway(cls.name, cls)

class StripePlugin(PaymentGatewayPlugin):
    name = "stripe"

    @classmethod
    def register(cls, app):
        super().register(app)
        app.add_route("/payments/stripe", cls.process)

# Auto-registration
import plugins.stripe
import plugins.paypal
import plugins.braintree
detected_component:
  id: component.payment_gateway_plugin

  signals:

      strength: strong
    - type: code

      strength: strong

    - stripe
    - paypal

  notification_providers:
    - email
    - sms

Plugin component pattern:

  required_signals:
    - signal: plugin_mechanism
      strength: strong

      strength: strong

title: 13  Component Discovery
text:
13.7 Inferring Component Responsibilities
Once candidate components are identified, extraction must infer their responsibilities. Responsibilities are derived from the artifacts associated with each component.
Responsibility inference methodology:
1. From API controllers:
# payment_controller.py
@app.post("/payments")
def create_payment(request: PaymentRequest):
    return payment_service.create(request)

@app.get("/payments/{payment_id}")
def get_payment(payment_id: str):
    return payment_service.get(payment_id)

@app.post("/payments/{payment_id}/refund")
def refund_payment(payment_id: str, amount: float):
    return payment_service.refund(payment_id, amount)
inferred_responsibilities:
  component: component.payment_service
  source: API controllers

  responsibilities:
    - responsibility: "Create payment transactions"
      evidence: "@app.post(\"/payments\")"
      confidence: 0.95

    - responsibility: "Retrieve payment information"
      evidence: "@app.get(\"/payments/{payment_id}\")"
      confidence: 0.95

    - responsibility: "Process payment refunds"
      evidence: "@app.post(\"/payments/{payment_id}/refund\")"
      confidence: 0.95
2. From message handlers:
# worker.py
@app.task
def process_payment(payment_id):
    # Process payment
    payment = Payment.query.get(payment_id)
    gateway.process(payment)
    notification.send(payment)

@app.task
def settle_payment(payment_id):
    # Settlement process
    payment = Payment.query.get(payment_id)
    payment.settle()
inferred_responsibilities:
  component: component.payment_worker
  source: Message handlers

  responsibilities:
    - responsibility: "Process payment transactions"
      evidence: "@app.task process_payment"
      confidence: 0.90

    - responsibility: "Settle completed payments"
      evidence: "@app.task settle_payment"
      confidence: 0.90
3. From database migrations:
# migrations/001_create_payments.py
def upgrade():
    op.create_table(
        'payments',
        sa.Column('id', sa.Integer(), primary_key=True),
        sa.Column('amount', sa.Numeric(10, 2), nullable=False),
        sa.Column('status', sa.String(50), nullable=False),
        sa.Column('created_at', sa.DateTime(), nullable=False),
    )

def downgrade():
    op.drop_table('payments')
inferred_responsibilities:
  component: component.payment_service
  source: Database migrations

  responsibilities:
    - responsibility: "Manage payment records"
      evidence: "create_table('payments')"
      confidence: 0.88

    - responsibility: "Maintain payment state"
      evidence: "status column"
      confidence: 0.85
4. From workflow definitions:
# payment_dag.py
task1 = PythonOperator(
    task_id='fetch_payments',
    python_callable=fetch_payments,
    dag=dag,
)

task2 = PythonOperator(
    task_id='process_payments',
    python_callable=process_payments,
    dag=dag,
)

task3 = PythonOperator(
    task_id='send_notifications',
    python_callable=send_notifications,
    dag=dag,
)

  component: component.payment_service
  source: Workflow definitions

  responsibilities:
    - responsibility: "Fetch pending payments"
      evidence: "task_id='fetch_payments'"
      confidence: 0.82

    - responsibility: "Process payment transactions"
      evidence: "task_id='process_payments'"
      confidence: 0.82

    - responsibility: "Send payment notifications"
      evidence: "task_id='send_notifications'"
      confidence: 0.82
Responsibility consolidation:
component_responsibilities:
  component: component.payment_service

  responsibilities:
    - responsibility: "Create payment transactions"
      sources: [api_controllers, workflows]
      confidence: 0.95

    - responsibility: "Process payment transactions"
      sources: [message_handlers, workflows]
      confidence: 0.92

    - responsibility: "Manage payment records"
