title: 13  Component Discovery
text:
13.8 Inferring Component Boundaries
Component boundaries are rarely defined explicitly in repositories. Instead, boundaries must be inferred from structural and behavioral signals.
Boundary inference indicators:
1. Directory isolation:
project/
├── api/              # API service boundary
│   ├── routes.py
│   ├── controllers.py
│   └── services.py
├── workers/          # Worker boundary
│   ├── payment_worker.py
│   └── notification_worker.py
└── shared/           # Shared utilities (not a component)
    ├── utils.py
    └── models.py
detected_boundary:
  component: component.api_service
  boundary_type: directory_isolation
  evidence:
    - signal: "api/ directory exists"
    - signal: "contains routes.py, controllers.py, services.py"
    - signal: "separate from workers/ directory"
  confidence: 0.85
2. Independent entrypoints:
# api/main.py (API service entrypoint)
from fastapi import FastAPI
app = FastAPI()
if __name__ == "__main__":
    uvicorn.run(app, host="0.0.0.0", port=8000)

# workers/worker.py (Worker entrypoint)
from celery import Celery
app = Celery('tasks')
if __name__ == '__main__':
    app.worker_main(['worker'])
detected_boundary:
  component: component.api_service
  boundary_type: independent_entrypoint
  evidence:
    - signal: "api/main.py contains uvicorn.run()"
    - signal: "workers/worker.py contains worker_main()"
    - signal: "separate startup scripts"
  confidence: 0.92
3. Separate deployment configurations:
# docker-compose.yml
services:
  api:
    image: payment-app:latest
    ports:
      - "8000:8000"
    command: python api/main.py

  worker:
    image: payment-app:latest
    command: celery -A workers worker
    depends_on:
      - redis
detected_boundary:
  component: component.api_service
  boundary_type: separate_deployment
  evidence:
    - signal: "api service in docker-compose.yml"
    - signal: "worker service in docker-compose.yml"
    - signal: "separate service definitions"
  confidence: 0.90
4. Dedicated runtime processes:
# k8s/api-deployment.yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: api-service
spec:
  replicas: 3
  template:
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: api
        image: payment-app:latest
        command: ["python", "api/main.py"]

# k8s/worker-deployment.yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: worker-service
spec:
  replicas: 2
  template:
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: worker
        image: payment-app:latest
        command: ["celery", "-A", "workers", "worker"]
detected_boundary:
  component: component.api_service
  boundary_type: dedicated_process
  evidence:
    - signal: "separate Kubernetes deployments"
    - signal: "different commands for api and worker"
    - signal: "independent scaling"
  confidence: 0.95
5. Distinct dependency sets:
# api/requirements.txt
fastapi==0.104.0
uvicorn==0.24.0
pydantic==2.5.0

# workers/requirements.txt
celery==5.3.0
redis==5.0.0
detected_boundary:
  component: component.api_service
  boundary_type: distinct_dependencies
  evidence:
    - signal: "api/requirements.txt contains fastapi, uvicorn"
    - signal: "workers/requirements.txt contains celery, redis"
    - signal: "separate dependency manifests"
  confidence: 0.82
Boundary confirmation:
When multiple boundary indicators align, confidence in component boundaries increases:
component_boundary_assessment:
  component: component.api_service

  boundary_signals:
    - signal: directory_isolation
      strength: moderate
    - signal: independent_entrypoint
      strength: strong
    - signal: separate_deployment
      strength: strong
    - signal: dedicated_process
      strength: strong
    - signal: distinct_dependencies
      strength: moderate

  boundary_confidence: 0.92
  rationale: "Multiple strong boundary indicators confirm component boundary"

title: 13  Component Discovery
text:
13.9 Producing the Component Inventory
The final output of component discovery is the component inventory. The component inventory is a structured list describing the components that compose the system.
Component inventory structure:
component_inventory:
  version: "1.0"
  extraction_date: "2026-03-08"
  repository: "payment-system"
  total_components: 4

  components:
    - id: component.api_service
      type: Component
      subtype: Service
      role: API

      responsibilities:
        - "Create payment transactions"
        - "Retrieve payment information"
        - "Process payment refunds"
        - "Manage payment records"

      interfaces:
        - id: interface.http_api
          type: API
          protocol: HTTP
          confidence: 0.95

      data:
        - id: data.payments
          relationship: owns
          confidence: 0.90

      workflows:
        - id: workflow.payment_processing
          role: primary
          confidence: 0.85

      confidence: 0.92
      evidence_refs:
        - evidence-001  # FastAPI app
        - evidence-002  # Route definitions
        - evidence-003  # Server initialization

    - id: component.payment_worker
      type: Component
      subtype: Worker
      role: Background

      responsibilities:
        - "Process payment transactions"
        - "Settle completed payments"
        - "Send payment notifications"

      interfaces:
        - id: interface.queue
          type: Event
          protocol: Redis/Celery
          confidence: 0.90

      data:
        - id: data.payments
          relationship: reads
          confidence: 0.88

      workflows:
        - id: workflow.payment_processing
          role: worker
          confidence: 0.85

      confidence: 0.88
      evidence_refs:
        - evidence-004  # Celery task
        - evidence-005  # Queue dependency
        - evidence-006  # Worker startup

    - id: component.notification_service
      type: Component
      subtype: Service
      role: Notification

      responsibilities:
        - "Send email notifications"
        - "Send SMS notifications"
        - "Send push notifications"

      interfaces:
        - id: interface.http_api
          type: API
          protocol: HTTP
          confidence: 0.82

      data:
        - id: data.notifications
          relationship: owns
          confidence: 0.85

      workflows:
        - id: workflow.payment_processing
          role: participant
          confidence: 0.75

      confidence: 0.82
      evidence_refs:
        - evidence-007  # Notification endpoints
        - evidence-008  # Email integration
        - evidence-009  # SMS integration

    - id: component.payment_scheduler
      type: Component
      subtype: Scheduler
      role: Orchestration

      responsibilities:
        - "Schedule payment processing"
        - "Trigger settlement workflows"
        - "Generate daily reports"

      interfaces:
        - id: interface.temporal_trigger
          type: Temporal
          protocol: Cron
          confidence: 0.88

      workflows:
        - id: workflow.payment_processing
          role: scheduler
          confidence: 0.80
        - id: workflow.settlement
          role: primary
          confidence: 0.85

      confidence: 0.85
      evidence_refs:
        - evidence-010  # Cron configuration
        - evidence-011  # Workflow DAG
        - evidence-012  # Scheduler startup

  quality_metrics:
    average_confidence: 0.87
    high_confidence_components: 3
    medium_confidence_components: 1
    low_confidence_components: 0

  extraction_notes:
    - "All components have strong supporting evidence"
    - "Boundaries are clearly defined by deployment configs"
    - "Worker component depends on Redis queue (external service)"
Inventory validation:
Component inventory should satisfy several quality checks:
inventory_validation:
  checks:
    - check: "All components have at least one responsibility"
      status: PASS
    - check: "All components have supporting evidence"
      status: PASS
    - check: "Component boundaries are well-defined"
      status: PASS
    - check: "Confidence scores are reasonable (> 0.7)"
      status: PASS
    - check: "No conflicting component definitions"
      status: PASS

  validation_result: VALID

title: 13  Component Discovery
text:
13.10 Chapter Summary

Components represent structural units of responsibility that implement capabilities and expose interfaces
Components differ from code modules; they represent architectural boundaries, not just code organization
Component discovery uses a systematic pipeline: signal detection → pattern matching → evidence aggregation → boundary inference → inventory generation
Four main component archetypes: Service (externally reachable), Worker (asynchronous processing), Scheduler (time-based coordination), Plugin (extensibility)
Service components are identified by HTTP frameworks, server initialization, API controllers, startup scripts, and container port exposure
Worker components are identified by queue consumers, background task frameworks, job libraries, queue configuration, and worker startup commands
Scheduler components are identified by cron configurations, DAG definitions, scheduling frameworks, and task orchestration
Plugin components are identified by plugin directories, dynamic module loading, registration mechanisms, and configuration-driven discovery
Component responsibilities are inferred from API controllers, message handlers, database migrations, and workflow definitions
Component boundaries are inferred from directory isolation, independent entrypoints, separate deployment, dedicated processes, and distinct dependencies
The component inventory is the final output, containing structured component definitions with responsibilities, interfaces, data, workflows, confidence, and evidence references
Component inventory forms the foundation of the component layer within the blueprint kernel

Next Chapter: Chapter 12 introduces Interface and Contract Extraction—identifying and reconstructing the interaction surfaces through which components expose capabilities and coordinate behavior. This chapter covers API interfaces, event interfaces, CLI interfaces, and contract reconstruction.
crumbs: Part III — Ontology Mapping: From Repo to System Graph

title: 14  Interface and Contract Extraction
text:
14.1 What Is an Interface?
Interfaces define how a system interacts with external actors and internal components. They represent the points of interaction through which capabilities are accessed and coordinated. During blueprint extraction, interfaces are reconstructed from repository artifacts that expose interaction surfaces.
Interface extraction therefore focuses on identifying these interaction mechanisms and reconstructing the contracts that govern their behavior. The output of this stage is an interface graph together with a set of contract objects that formally describe interaction semantics.
Interfaces are critical architectural elements because they define:
An interface is a well-defined interaction surface that specifies how capabilities are invoked or accessed. Interfaces define the contract between providers and consumers of functionality.
Interface characteristics:
1. Interaction surface:
Interfaces provide access points through which functionality is invoked.
2. Contract specification:
Interfaces define the rules governing interactions, including request formats, response structures, and error handling.
3. Capability access:
Interfaces provide access to specific capabilities or operations.
4. Protocol definition:
Interfaces specify the communication protocol used for interaction.
Interface types:
The interface ontology defines several common interface archetypes:
crumbs: Part III — Ontology Mapping: From Repo to System Graph

title: 14  Interface and Contract Extraction
text:
interface:
  id: interface.payment_api
  type: API
  role: "External interaction surface"
  exposed_by: component.payment_service
  description: "HTTP API for payment processing"


interface:
  id: interface.payment_api
  contract:
    request_format: JSON
    response_format: JSON
    authentication: Bearer token
    rate_limit: 100 requests/minute
    error_codes: [400, 401, 404, 500]


interface:
  id: interface.payment_api
  capabilities:
    - capability.create_payment
    - capability.get_payment
    - capability.refund_payment


interface:
  id: interface.payment_api
  protocol:
    name: HTTP
    version: 1.1
    methods: [POST, GET, PUT, DELETE]
    content_type: application/json


interface_types:
  API:
    role: "Synchronous request-response interaction"
    protocols: [HTTP, HTTPS, gRPC, GraphQL]
    typical_consumers: [web applications, mobile apps, external systems]
    examples: [REST APIs, GraphQL endpoints, gRPC services]

  Event:
    role: "Asynchronous message-based interaction"
    protocols: [message queues, event streams, pub/sub]
    typical_consumers: [workers, microservices, event handlers]
    examples: [Kafka topics, RabbitMQ queues, Redis streams]

  CLI:
    role: "Command-line interaction"
    protocols: [stdin/stdout, command-line arguments]
    typical_consumers: [operators, automation scripts, cron jobs]
    examples: [CLI tools, administrative commands, batch jobs]

  RPC:
    role: "Remote procedure call interaction"
    protocols: [gRPC, Thrift, Protocol Buffers]
    typical_consumers: [microservices, internal services]
    examples: [gRPC services, Thrift interfaces]
crumbs: Part III — Ontology Mapping: From Repo to System Graph

title: 14  Interface and Contract Extraction
text:
14.2 API Interface Extraction
API interfaces are the most common form of system interface. They are typically implemented through web frameworks that define route handlers and request processing logic.
API interface signals:
1. Route declarations within web frameworks:
# FastAPI routes
from fastapi import FastAPI, HTTPException
from pydantic import BaseModel

app = FastAPI()

class PaymentRequest(BaseModel):
    amount: float
    currency: str
    payment_method: str

class PaymentResponse(BaseModel):
    payment_id: str
    status: str
    created_at: str

@app.post("/payments", response_model=PaymentResponse)
def create_payment(request: PaymentRequest):
    # Process payment
    payment = payment_service.create(request)
    return PaymentResponse(
        payment_id=payment.id,
        status=payment.status,
        created_at=payment.created_at.isoformat()
    )

@app.get("/payments/{payment_id}", response_model=PaymentResponse)
def get_payment(payment_id: str):
    payment = payment_service.get(payment_id)
    if not payment:
        raise HTTPException(status_code=404, detail="Payment not found")
    return PaymentResponse(
        payment_id=payment.id,
        status=payment.status,
        created_at=payment.created_at.isoformat()
    )
extracted_interface:
  id: interface.payment_api
  type: API
  protocol: HTTP

  operations:
    - operation: create_payment
      method: POST
      path: /payments
      request:
        content_type: application/json
        schema:
          amount: float
          currency: str
          payment_method: str
      response:
        content_type: application/json
        schema:
          payment_id: str
          status: str
          created_at: str
      confidence: 0.95
      evidence: evidence-001

    - operation: get_payment
      method: GET
      path: /payments/{payment_id}
      parameters:
        - name: payment_id
          type: string
          location: path
          required: true
      response:
        content_type: application/json
        schema:
          payment_id: str
          status: str
          created_at: str
      confidence: 0.95
      evidence: evidence-001

  exposed_by: component.payment_service
  confidence: 0.95
  evidence_refs: [evidence-001, evidence-002]
2. Controller classes or handler functions:
// Spring Boot controller
@RestController
@RequestMapping("/payments")
public class PaymentController {

    @Autowired
    private PaymentService paymentService;

    @PostMapping
    public ResponseEntity<Payment> createPayment(@RequestBody PaymentRequest request) {
        Payment payment = paymentService.create(request);
        return ResponseEntity.ok(payment);
    }

    @GetMapping("/{paymentId}")
    public ResponseEntity<Payment> getPayment(@PathVariable String paymentId) {
        Payment payment = paymentService.get(paymentId);
        if (payment == null) {
            return ResponseEntity.notFound().build();
        }
        return ResponseEntity.ok(payment);
    }

    @PostMapping("/{paymentId}/refund")
    public ResponseEntity<Payment> refundPayment(
        @PathVariable String paymentId,
        @RequestBody RefundRequest request
    ) {
        Payment payment = paymentService.refund(paymentId, request.getAmount());
        return ResponseEntity.ok(payment);
    }
}
extracted_interface:
  id: interface.payment_api
  type: API
  protocol: HTTP

  operations:
    - operation: create_payment
      method: POST
      path: /payments
      request_body: PaymentRequest
      response: Payment
      confidence: 0.93
      evidence: evidence-003

    - operation: get_payment
      method: GET
      path: /payments/{paymentId}
      path_parameters:
        - name: paymentId
          type: String
      response: Payment
      confidence: 0.93
      evidence: evidence-003

    - operation: refund_payment
      method: POST
      path: /payments/{paymentId}/refund
      path_parameters:
        - name: paymentId
          type: String
      request_body: RefundRequest
      response: Payment
      confidence: 0.93
      evidence: evidence-003

  exposed_by: component.payment_service
  confidence: 0.93
  evidence_refs: [evidence-003, evidence-004]
3. OpenAPI or Swagger specifications:
# openapi.yaml

      confidence: 0.98

    - operation: get_payment
      method: GET

      summary: "Get payment details"
      parameters:

          in: path

4. RPC service definitions:

syntax = "proto3";

package payment;

service PaymentService {
  rpc CreatePayment (PaymentRequest) returns (PaymentResponse);
  rpc GetPayment (PaymentId) returns (PaymentResponse);
  rpc RefundPayment (RefundRequest) returns (PaymentResponse);
}

  double amount = 1;
  string currency = 2;

API interface extraction pattern:
api_interface_extraction_pattern:
  required_signals:
    - signal: route_definition
      strength: strong
    - signal: request_handling
      strength: strong

  supporting_signals:
    - signal: request_schema
      strength: moderate
    - signal: response_schema
      strength: moderate
    - signal: authentication_mechanism
      strength: moderate

  contract_extraction:
    - extract: operation_names
      from: route_declarations
    - extract: request_schemas
      from: request_models or openapi_spec
    - extract: response_schemas
      from: response_models or openapi_spec
    - extract: error_handling
      from: exception_handling or status_codes

  output:
    entity:
      type: Interface
      subtype: API
      operations: [...]
      contracts: [...]
    confidence: 0.85-0.98

title: 14  Interface and Contract Extraction
text:
14.3 Event Interface Extraction
Many modern systems rely on asynchronous communication through event streams or messaging infrastructure. Event interfaces expose the system through topics, queues, or event schemas that allow components or external systems to publish and consume events.
Event interface signals:
1. Message topic definitions:
# event_topics.py
from kafka import KafkaProducer, KafkaConsumer
import json

KAFKA_BROKER = 'localhost:9092'

TOPICS = {
    'payment_created': 'payments.created',
    'payment_completed': 'payments.completed',
    'payment_failed': 'payments.failed',
    'refund_requested': 'payments.refund.requested',
}

class PaymentEventProducer:
    def __init__(self):
        self.producer = KafkaProducer(
            bootstrap_servers=KAFKA_BROKER,
            value_serializer=lambda v: json.dumps(v).encode('utf-8')
        )

    def publish_payment_created(self, payment):
        self.producer.send(
            TOPICS['payment_created'],
            value={
                'payment_id': payment.id,
                'amount': float(payment.amount),
                'status': payment.status,
                'created_at': payment.created_at.isoformat()
            }
        )

class PaymentEventConsumer:
    def __init__(self, topic):
        self.consumer = KafkaConsumer(
            topic,
            bootstrap_servers=KAFKA_BROKER,
            value_deserializer=lambda m: json.loads(m.decode('utf-8'))
        )

    def consume_events(self):
        for message in self.consumer:
            yield message.value
extracted_interface:
  id: interface.payment_events
  type: Event
  protocol: Kafka
  broker: localhost:9092

  topics:
    - topic: payments.created
      event_type: payment_created
      producer: component.payment_service
      consumers: [component.notification_service, component.analytics_service]
      schema:
        payment_id: string
        amount: float
        status: string
        created_at: string (datetime)
      confidence: 0.90
      evidence: evidence-007

    - topic: payments.completed
      event_type: payment_completed
      producer: component.payment_worker
      consumers: [component.notification_service]
      schema:
        payment_id: string
        amount: float
        status: string
        completed_at: string (datetime)
      confidence: 0.90
      evidence: evidence-007

    - topic: payments.failed
      event_type: payment_failed
      producer: component.payment_worker
      consumers: [component.notification_service]
      schema:
        payment_id: string
        amount: float
        status: string
        error_code: string
        failed_at: string (datetime)
      confidence: 0.90
      evidence: evidence-007

    - topic: payments.refund.requested
      event_type: refund_requested
      producer: component.api_service
      consumers: [component.payment_worker]
      schema:
        payment_id: string
        amount: float
        reason: string
        requested_at: string (datetime)
      confidence: 0.90
      evidence: evidence-007

  exposed_by: component.payment_service
  confidence: 0.90
  evidence_refs: [evidence-007, evidence-008]
2. Event schema files:
// payment_schemas.json
{
  "payment_created": {
    "type": "object",
    "properties": {
      "payment_id": {"type": "string"},
      "amount": {"type": "number", "format": "decimal"},
      "currency": {"type": "string"},
      "status": {"type": "string"},
      "created_at": {"type": "string", "format": "date-time"}
    },
    "required": ["payment_id", "amount", "currency", "status", "created_at"]

  "payment_completed": {
    "type": "object",
    "properties": {
      "payment_id": {"type": "string"},
      "amount": {"type": "number"},
      "status": {"type": "string"},
      "gateway_response": {"type": "object"},
      "completed_at": {"type": "string", "format": "date-time"}
    },
    "required": ["payment_id", "status", "completed_at"]
  }
}

  type: Event

          payment_id: string
          amount: number (decimal)
          currency: string

          created_at: string (date-time)

3. Consumer or subscriber implementations:
# notification_consumer.py
from consumers import PaymentEventConsumer

class NotificationConsumer:
    def __init__(self):
        self.consumer = PaymentEventConsumer('payments.completed')

    def start(self):
        for event in self.consumer.consume_events():
            if event['status'] == 'completed':
                self.send_success_notification(event)

    def send_success_notification(self, event):
        # Send notification
        pass

# analytics_consumer.py
class AnalyticsConsumer:
    def __init__(self):
        self.consumer = PaymentEventConsumer('payments.created')

    def start(self):
        for event in self.consumer.consume_events():
            self.record_payment_metric(event)

      confidence: 0.88

      confidence: 0.88

    - event: payment_failed

      purpose: "Send failure notifications"
      confidence: 0.88

  confidence: 0.88

  evidence_refs: [evidence-011]
Event interface extraction pattern:
event_interface_extraction_pattern:
  required_signals:
    - signal: topic_definition
      strength: strong
    - signal: event_schema
      strength: moderate

  supporting_signals:
    - signal: consumer_implementation
      strength: moderate
    - signal: broker_configuration
      strength: moderate
    - signal: producer_implementation
      strength: strong

    - extract: topic_names
      from: topic_definitions or broker_config

      type: Interface
      subtype: Event
      topics: [...]
      event_schemas: [...]
      consumers: [...]

title: 14  Interface and Contract Extraction
text:
14.4 CLI Interface Extraction
Command-line interfaces represent another common form of interaction surface. Many systems expose operational capabilities through command-line tools that orchestrate tasks, manage workflows, or interact with system services.
CLI interface signals:
1. Command registration logic:
# cli.py
import click
from services import payment_service

@click.group()
def cli():
    """Payment system CLI tool"""
    pass

@cli.command()
@click.argument('amount', type=float)
@click.argument('currency', type=str)
@click.option('--method', default='credit_card', help='Payment method')
def create_payment(amount, currency, method):
    """Create a new payment"""
    payment = payment_service.create({
        'amount': amount,
        'currency': currency,
        'payment_method': method
    })
    click.echo(f"Payment created: {payment.id}")

@cli.command()
@click.argument('payment_id')
def get_payment(payment_id):
    """Get payment details"""
    payment = payment_service.get(payment_id)
    click.echo(f"Payment {payment.id}: {payment.status} - ${payment.amount}")

@cli.command()
@click.argument('payment_id')
@click.argument('amount', type=float)
def refund_payment(payment_id, amount):
    """Refund a payment"""
    payment = payment_service.refund(payment_id, amount)
    click.echo(f"Refund processed: {payment.id}")

@cli.command()
def process_pending():
    """Process all pending payments"""
    results = payment_service.process_pending()
    click.echo(f"Processed {results['processed']} payments")

if __name__ == '__main__':
    cli()
extracted_interface:
  id: interface.payment_cli
  type: CLI
  protocol: Command Line

  commands:
    - command: create_payment
      arguments:
        - name: amount
          type: float
          required: true
        - name: currency
          type: string
          required: true
      options:
        - name: --method
          default: credit_card
          help: Payment method
      description: "Create a new payment"
      confidence: 0.90
      evidence: evidence-012

    - command: get_payment
      arguments:
        - name: payment_id
          type: string
          required: true
      description: "Get payment details"
      confidence: 0.90
      evidence: evidence-012

    - command: refund_payment
      arguments:
        - name: payment_id
          type: string
          required: true
        - name: amount
          type: float
          required: true
      description: "Refund a payment"
      confidence: 0.90
      evidence: evidence-012

    - command: process_pending
      arguments: []
      description: "Process all pending payments"
      confidence: 0.90
      evidence: evidence-012

  exposed_by: component.payment_service
  confidence: 0.90
  evidence_refs: [evidence-012]
2. Argument parsing definitions:
// main.go
package main

import (
    "flag"
    "fmt"
    "os"
)

func main() {
    createCmd := flag.NewFlagSet("create", flag.ExitOnError)
    amount := createCmd.Float("amount", 0, "Payment amount")
    currency := createCmd.String("currency", "USD", "Payment currency")
    method := createCmd.String("method", "credit_card", "Payment method")

    getCmd := flag.NewFlagSet("get", flag.ExitOnError)
    paymentID := getCmd.String("id", "", "Payment ID")

    refundCmd := flag.NewFlagSet("refund", flag.ExitOnError)
    refundID := refundCmd.String("id", "", "Payment ID to refund")
    refundAmount := refundCmd.Float("amount", 0, "Refund amount")

    if len(os.Args) < 2 {
        fmt.Println("Expected 'create', 'get', or 'refund' subcommands")
        os.Exit(1)
    }

    switch os.Args[1] {
    case "create":
        createCmd.Parse(os.Args[2:])
        paymentService.Create(*amount, *currency, *method)
    case "get":
        getCmd.Parse(os.Args[2:])
        paymentService.Get(*paymentID)
    case "refund":
        refundCmd.Parse(os.Args[2:])
        paymentService.Refund(*refundID, *refundAmount)
    default:
        fmt.Println("Unknown subcommand")
    }
}
extracted_interface:
  id: interface.payment_cli
  type: CLI
  protocol: Command Line

  commands:
    - command: create
      flags:
        - name: -amount
          type: float
          default: 0
          description: Payment amount
        - name: -currency
          type: string
          default: USD
          description: Payment currency
        - name: -method
          type: string
          default: credit_card
          description: Payment method
      confidence: 0.88
      evidence: evidence-013

    - command: get
      flags:
        - name: -id
          type: string
          required: true
          description: Payment ID
      confidence: 0.88
      evidence: evidence-013

    - command: refund
      flags:
        - name: -id
          type: string
          required: true
          description: Payment ID to refund
        - name: -amount
          type: float
          required: true
          description: Refund amount
      confidence: 0.88
      evidence: evidence-013

  exposed_by: component.payment_service
  confidence: 0.88
  evidence_refs: [evidence-013]
3. Command modules or subcommands:
#!/bin/bash
# payment-cli.sh

# Main command dispatcher
case "$1" in
    create)
        python scripts/create_payment.py "${@:2}"
        ;;
    get)
        python scripts/get_payment.py "${@:2}"
        ;;
    refund)
        python scripts/refund_payment.py "${@:2}"
        ;;
    process)
        python scripts/process_pending.py "${@:2}"
        ;;
    *)
        echo "Usage: $0 {create|get|refund|process} [args...]"
        exit 1
        ;;
esac

CLI interface extraction pattern:
cli_interface_extraction_pattern:
  required_signals:
    - signal: command_registration

title: 14  Interface and Contract Extraction
text:
14.5 Contract Reconstruction
Interfaces alone describe interaction entrypoints. Contracts define the rules governing those interactions. Contract extraction attempts to reconstruct information such as request structures, response formats, validation rules, and error handling.
Contract extraction elements:
1. Request and response structures:
# API contract extraction
class PaymentRequest(BaseModel):
    """Request schema for creating payment"""
    amount: float = Field(..., gt=0, description="Payment amount")
    currency: str = Field(..., min_length=3, max_length=3, description="Currency code")
    payment_method: str = Field(..., description="Payment method")
    metadata: Optional[Dict[str, Any]] = Field(None, description="Additional metadata")

    class Config:
        json_schema_extra = {
            "example": {
                "amount": 100.00,
                "currency": "USD",
                "payment_method": "credit_card"
            }
        }
extracted_contract:
  operation: create_payment
  type: API

  request:
    content_type: application/json
    schema:
      type: object
      properties:
        amount:
          type: float
          constraints:
            gt: 0
          description: Payment amount
          required: true
        currency:
          type: string
          constraints:
            min_length: 3
            max_length: 3
          description: Currency code
          required: true
        payment_method:
          type: string
          description: Payment method
          required: true
        metadata:
          type: object
          description: Additional metadata
          required: false
    validation:
      - field: amount
        rule: gt 0
      - field: currency
        rule: min_length 3, max_length 3
    example:
      amount: 100.00
      currency: USD
      payment_method: credit_card

  response:
    content_type: application/json
    schema:
      type: object
      properties:
        payment_id:
          type: string
          description: Payment identifier
        status:
          type: string
          description: Payment status
        amount:
          type: float
          description: Payment amount
        created_at:
          type: string
          format: date-time
          description: Creation timestamp
    example:
      payment_id: pay_123456
      status: pending
      amount: 100.00
      created_at: "2026-03-08T10:30:00Z"

  confidence: 0.92
  evidence: evidence-016
2. Parameter schemas:
// Path parameter extraction
@GetMapping("/payments/{paymentId}")
public ResponseEntity<Payment> getPayment(
    @PathVariable String paymentId,
    @RequestParam(defaultValue = "false") boolean includeDetails
) {
    // Implementation
}
extracted_contract:
  operation: get_payment
  type: API

  parameters:
    - name: paymentId
      location: path
      type: string
      required: true
      description: Payment identifier
      confidence: 0.90
    - name: includeDetails
      location: query
      type: boolean
      required: false
      default: false
      description: Include detailed information
      confidence: 0.90

  confidence: 0.90
  evidence: evidence-017
3. Event payload formats:
// Event schema
{
  "type": "object",
  "properties": {
    "payment_id": {
      "type": "string",
      "description": "Unique payment identifier"
    },
    "amount": {
      "type": "number",
      "format": "decimal",
      "description": "Payment amount"
    },
    "currency": {
      "type": "string",
      "enum": ["USD", "EUR", "GBP"],
      "description": "Currency code"
    },
    "status": {
      "type": "string",
      "enum": ["pending", "processing", "completed", "failed"],
      "description": "Payment status"
    },
    "timestamp": {
      "type": "string",
      "format": "date-time",
      "description": "Event timestamp"
    }
  },
  "required": ["payment_id", "amount", "currency", "status", "timestamp"]
}
extracted_contract:
  event: payment_created
  type: Event

  payload:
    content_type: application/json
    schema:
      type: object
      properties:
        payment_id:
          type: string
          description: Unique payment identifier
          required: true
        amount:
          type: number
          format: decimal
          description: Payment amount
          required: true
        currency:
          type: string
          enum: [USD, EUR, GBP]
          description: Currency code
          required: true
        status:
          type: string
          enum: [pending, processing, completed, failed]
          description: Payment status
          required: true
        timestamp:
          type: string
          format: date-time
          description: Event timestamp
          required: true

  confidence: 0.92
  evidence: evidence-018
4. Command arguments and options:
# CLI contract extraction
@click.command()
@click.argument('amount', type=float)
@click.argument('currency', type=str)
@click.option('--method', default='credit_card', help='Payment method (credit_card, debit_card, paypal)')
@click.option('--description', help='Payment description')
@click.option('--metadata', help='JSON metadata')
def create_payment(amount, currency, method, description, metadata):
    """Create a new payment

    Arguments:
        amount: Payment amount (must be > 0)
        currency: Currency code (3-letter ISO code)

    Options:
        --method: Payment method (default: credit_card)
        --description: Payment description
        --metadata: JSON metadata
    """
    pass
extracted_contract:
  command: create_payment
  type: CLI

  arguments:
    - name: amount
      type: float
      required: true
      validation:
        - rule: gt 0
      description: Payment amount
    - name: currency
      type: string
      required: true
      validation:
        - rule: length 3
      description: Currency code (3-letter ISO)

  options:
    - name: --method
      type: string
      required: false
      default: credit_card
      choices: [credit_card, debit_card, paypal]
      description: Payment method
    - name: --description
      type: string
      required: false
      description: Payment description
    - name: --metadata
      type: string
      required: false
      description: JSON metadata

  confidence: 0.88
  evidence: evidence-019
5. Validation constraints when visible:
# Validation rules extraction
class PaymentRequest(BaseModel):
    amount: Decimal = Field(
        ...,
        gt=Decimal('0'),
        le=Decimal('999999.99'),
        description="Payment amount (0.01 - 999999.99)"
    )
    currency: str = Field(
        ...,
        min_length=3,
        max_length=3,
        regex=r'^[A-Z]{3}$',
        description="3-letter ISO currency code"
    )
    payment_method: str = Field(
        ...,
        regex=r'^(credit_card|debit_card|paypal|bank_transfer)$',
        description="Payment method"
    )
extracted_contract:
  operation: create_payment
  type: API

  validation_constraints:
    amount:
      - type: gt
        value: 0
      - type: le
        value: 999999.99
      description: Payment amount (0.01 - 999999.99)
    currency:
      - type: min_length
        value: 3
      - type: max_length
        value: 3
      - type: regex
        value: ^[A-Z]{3}$
      description: 3-letter ISO currency code
    payment_method:
      - type: regex
        value: ^(credit_card|debit_card|paypal|bank_transfer)$
      description: Payment method

  confidence: 0.90
  evidence: evidence-020
Contract reconstruction challenges:
1. Partial contracts:
Not all contract information is explicitly defined. Some aspects must be inferred:
partial_contract:
  operation: update_payment
  type: API

  known_elements:
    - request: partial schema inferred from code
    - response: unknown (not documented)
    - error_handling: inferred from exception handling

  unknown_elements:
    - response_schema
    - error_codes
    - rate_limits

  confidence: 0.65
  notes: "Contract partially reconstructed from code"
2. Implicit contracts:
Some contracts are not defined at all but inferred from usage:
implicit_contract:
  operation: internal_payment_process
  type: Event

  source: "inferred from message handler"
  payload:
    amount: inferred from usage
    payment_id: inferred from database query
    timestamp: assumed based on conventions

  confidence: 0.55
  notes: "Contract inferred from code usage patterns"

title: 14  Interface and Contract Extraction
text:
14.6 Interface Graph Construction
Once interfaces and contracts are identified, extraction constructs the interface graph. The interface graph represents relationships between components and their interaction surfaces.
Interface graph structure:
interface_graph:
  nodes:
    # API interfaces
    - id: interface.payment_http_api
      type: API
      protocol: HTTP
      exposed_by: component.payment_service
      operations: [create_payment, get_payment, refund_payment]

    - id: interface.notification_http_api
      type: API
      protocol: HTTP
      exposed_by: component.notification_service
      operations: [send_email, send_sms]

    # Event interfaces
    - id: interface.payment_events
      type: Event
      protocol: Kafka
      exposed_by: component.payment_service
      topics: [payment_created, payment_completed, payment_failed]

    - id: interface.notification_events
      type: Event
      protocol: Kafka
      exposed_by: component.notification_service
      topics: [notification_sent, notification_failed]

    # CLI interfaces
    - id: interface.payment_cli
      type: CLI
      protocol: Command Line
      exposed_by: component.payment_service
      commands: [create_payment, get_payment, refund_payment]

  edges:
    # Component exposes interface
    - source: component.payment_service
      relation: exposes
      target: interface.payment_http_api
      type: ComponentExposesInterface

    - source: component.payment_service
      relation: exposes
      target: interface.payment_events
      type: ComponentExposesInterface

    - source: component.payment_service
      relation: exposes
      target: interface.payment_cli
      type: ComponentExposesInterface

    # Component consumes interface
    - source: component.notification_service
      relation: consumes
      target: interface.payment_events
      type: ComponentConsumesInterface

    - source: component.analytics_service
      relation: consumes
      target: interface.payment_events
      type: ComponentConsumesInterface

    # Interface exposes capability
    - source: interface.payment_http_api
      relation: exposes
      target: capability.create_payment
      type: InterfaceExposesCapability

    - source: interface.payment_http_api
      relation: exposes
      target: capability.get_payment
      type: InterfaceExposesCapability

    # Workflow invokes interface
    - source: workflow.payment_processing
      relation: invokes
      target: interface.payment_events
      type: WorkflowInvokesInterface
Interface graph analysis:
The interface graph reveals important architectural patterns:
graph_analysis:
  external_interfaces:
    - interface.payment_http_api
    - interface.notification_http_api
    - interface.payment_cli
    description: "Interfaces accessible to external actors"

  internal_interfaces:
    - interface.payment_events
    - interface.notification_events
    description: "Interfaces for internal component communication"

  interface_types_distribution:
    API: 2
    Event: 2
    CLI: 1

  component_interface_relationships:
    component.payment_service:
      exposes: [payment_http_api, payment_events, payment_cli]
      consumes: []

    component.notification_service:
      exposes: [notification_http_api, notification_events]
      consumes: [payment_events]

    component.analytics_service:
      exposes: []
      consumes: [payment_events]

  critical_interfaces:
    - interface.payment_http_api
      reason: "Primary external API for payment processing"
      consumers: [external_users, external_systems]

    - interface.payment_events
      reason: "Core event-driven communication"
      consumers: [notification_service, analytics_service]
crumbs: Part III — Ontology Mapping: From Repo to System Graph

title: 14  Interface and Contract Extraction
text:
14.7 Chapter Summary

Interfaces are well-defined interaction surfaces that specify how capabilities are accessed
Interfaces define contracts between providers and consumers, including request formats, response structures, and protocols
Four main interface types: API (synchronous request-response), Event (asynchronous messages), CLI (command-line), RPC (remote procedure call)
API interfaces are extracted from route declarations, controllers, OpenAPI specifications, and RPC definitions
Event interfaces are extracted from topic definitions, event schemas, consumer implementations, and broker configurations
CLI interfaces are extracted from command registration, argument parsing, command modules, and embedded documentation
Contract reconstruction extracts request/response structures, parameter schemas, event payloads, command arguments, and validation constraints
Contracts may be partial or implicit when not explicitly defined
The interface graph represents relationships between components and their interaction surfaces
Interface graph analysis reveals external vs. internal interfaces, type distribution, component relationships, and critical interfaces
Interfaces and contracts together represent the system’s communication layer

Next Chapter: Chapter 13 introduces Data Model and Ownership Extraction—identifying persistent data structures, determining which components control them, and detecting potential consistency risks. This chapter covers ORM signals, migration files, storage type identification, and ownership inference.
crumbs: Part III — Ontology Mapping: From Repo to System Graph

title: 15  Data Model and Ownership Extraction
text:
15.1 Why Data Matters in Architecture
Data is one of the most stable structural elements of a system. While components and interfaces may change frequently, data models often persist across system evolution. For this reason, blueprint extraction treats the data layer as a critical architectural dimension.
The goal of this stage is to reconstruct data entities and their ownership relationships from repository artifacts. This involves identifying persistent data structures, determining which components control them, and detecting potential consistency risks within the system.
The output consists of data entities and an ownership map describing how components interact with persistent data. Together, these artifacts represent the data layer of the blueprint.
Data is the foundation upon which system capabilities are built. Understanding data structure and ownership is essential for architectural understanding because data:
1. Represents system state:
Data entities capture the persistent state that the system maintains over time. Understanding what data exists and how it’s structured reveals what the system is designed to track and manage.
2. Defines component boundaries:
Data ownership often defines component boundaries. Components that own distinct data entities typically have distinct responsibilities and can be deployed independently.
3. Indicates architectural constraints:
Data access patterns reveal architectural constraints such as consistency requirements, performance characteristics, and scalability considerations.
4. Exposes integration points:
Data entities often represent integration points between components or systems. Understanding these relationships reveals how components coordinate.
crumbs: Part III — Ontology Mapping: From Repo to System Graph
15  Data Model and Ownership Extraction

title: 15  Data Model and Ownership Extraction
text:
data_insights:
  payment_data:
    structure: transactions, refunds, settlements
    meaning: system tracks financial transactions
    implications: requires ACID properties, audit trails

  user_data:
    structure: profiles, preferences, permissions
    meaning: system manages user identities
    implications: requires privacy controls, authentication


boundary_insights:
  payment_service:
    owns: [payments, transactions]
    boundary: "Financial transaction management"

  user_service:
    owns: [users, profiles, permissions]
    boundary: "User identity and access management"

  notification_service:
    owns: [notifications, templates]
    boundary: "Notification delivery"


constraint_insights:
  shared_data_access:
    pattern: multiple services access same data
    constraint: requires coordination mechanisms
    implications: eventual consistency, distributed transactions

  data_replication:
    pattern: data replicated across regions
    constraint: requires synchronization
    implications: conflict resolution, consistency trade-offs


integration_insights:
  payment_processing:
    data_flow: payment_service → payment_worker → database
    integration: queue-based data passing
    coordination: asynchronous processing

  user_authentication:
    data_flow: api_service → user_service → database
    integration: service-to-service calls
    coordination: synchronous queries
crumbs: Part III — Ontology Mapping: From Repo to System Graph
15  Data Model and Ownership Extraction

title: 15  Data Model and Ownership Extraction
text:
15.2 Data Entity Extraction
Data entity extraction identifies persistent data structures from repository artifacts. These entities represent tables, collections, documents, or other storage structures that hold system state.
Data entity signals:
1. ORM model definitions:
# models.py
from sqlalchemy import Column, Integer, String, Numeric, DateTime, ForeignKey
from sqlalchemy.ext.declarative import declarative_base
from sqlalchemy.orm import relationship

Base = declarative_base()

class Payment(Base):
    __tablename__ = 'payments'

    id = Column(Integer, primary_key=True)
    amount = Column(Numeric(10, 2), nullable=False)
    currency = Column(String(3), nullable=False)
    status = Column(String(50), nullable=False)
    payment_method = Column(String(100))
    created_at = Column(DateTime, nullable=False)
    updated_at = Column(DateTime, nullable=False)

    # Relationships
    transactions = relationship("Transaction", back_populates="payment")
    refunds = relationship("Refund", back_populates="payment")

class Transaction(Base):
    __tablename__ = 'transactions'

    id = Column(Integer, primary_key=True)
    payment_id = Column(Integer, ForeignKey('payments.id'), nullable=False)
    gateway = Column(String(100), nullable=False)
    gateway_transaction_id = Column(String(255), nullable=False)
    amount = Column(Numeric(10, 2), nullable=False)
    status = Column(String(50), nullable=False)
    created_at = Column(DateTime, nullable=False)

    # Relationships
    payment = relationship("Payment", back_populates="transactions")

class Refund(Base):
    __tablename__ = 'refunds'

    id = Column(Integer, primary_key=True)
    payment_id = Column(Integer, ForeignKey('payments.id'), nullable=False)
    amount = Column(Numeric(10, 2), nullable=False)
    reason = Column(String(500))
    status = Column(String(50), nullable=False)
    created_at = Column(DateTime, nullable=False)

    # Relationships
    payment = relationship("Payment", back_populates="refunds")
extracted_data_entities:

# migrations/001_create_payments.py
from alembic import op
import sqlalchemy as sa

def upgrade():
    op.create_table(
        'payments',
        sa.Column('id', sa.Integer(), nullable=False),
        sa.Column('amount', sa.Numeric(10, 2), nullable=False),
        sa.Column('currency', sa.String(3), nullable=False),
        sa.Column('status', sa.String(50), nullable=False),
        sa.Column('payment_method', sa.String(100), nullable=True),
        sa.Column('created_at', sa.DateTime(), nullable=False),
        sa.Column('updated_at', sa.DateTime(), nullable=False),
        sa.PrimaryKeyConstraint('id')
    )

    op.create_index('idx_payments_status', 'payments', ['status'])
    op.create_index('idx_payments_created_at', 'payments', ['created_at'])

def downgrade():
    op.drop_index('idx_payments_created_at', table_name='payments')
    op.drop_index('idx_payments_status', table_name='payments')
    op.drop_table('payments')

title: 15  Data Model and Ownership Extraction
text:
15.3 Storage Type Identification
Different systems employ different storage technologies depending on their operational requirements. Extraction therefore attempts to identify the type of storage systems used by the repository.
Storage type signals:
1. Relational databases:
# PostgreSQL configuration
DATABASE_URL = "postgresql://user:pass@localhost:5432/payment_db"

# SQLAlchemy connection
from sqlalchemy import create_engine
engine = create_engine(DATABASE_URL)
detected_storage:
  type: RelationalDatabase
  vendor: PostgreSQL
  connection_string: postgresql://user:pass@localhost:5432/payment_db
  signals:
    - dependency: psycopg2 or sqlalchemy
    - connection_string: postgresql://
    - usage: ORM models, migrations
  confidence: 0.95
2. Document stores:
// MongoDB connection
const mongoose = require('mongoose');
mongoose.connect('mongodb://localhost:27017/payment_db', {
  useNewUrlParser: true,
  useUnifiedTopology: true
});
detected_storage:
  type: DocumentStore
  vendor: MongoDB
  connection_string: mongodb://localhost:27017/payment_db
  signals:
    - dependency: mongoose
    - connection_string: mongodb://
    - usage: Mongoose schemas
  confidence: 0.93
3. Key–value stores:
# Redis configuration
REDIS_HOST = 'localhost'
REDIS_PORT = 6379
REDIS_DB = 0

import redis
redis_client = redis.Redis(
    host=REDIS_HOST,
    port=REDIS_PORT,
    db=REDIS_DB
)
detected_storage:
  type: KeyValueStore
  vendor: Redis
  connection: localhost:6379/0
  signals:
    - dependency: redis
    - connection: localhost:6379
    - usage: caching, session storage, job queues
  confidence: 0.92
4. Object storage:
# S3 configuration
import boto3
s3_client = boto3.client('s3')

BUCKET_NAME = 'payment-documents'

# Store payment receipt
def store_receipt(payment_id, receipt_pdf):
    s3_client.upload_fileobj(
        receipt_pdf,
        BUCKET_NAME,
        f'receipts/{payment_id}.pdf'
    )
detected_storage:
  type: ObjectStorage
  vendor: AWS S3
  bucket: payment-documents
  signals:
    - dependency: boto3
    - usage: upload_fileobj, s3_client
    - purpose: document storage, receipts
  confidence: 0.88
5. Analytical data stores:
# ClickHouse configuration
from clickhouse_driver import Client

client = Client(host='localhost', port=9000)

# Query analytics data
result = client.execute(
    'SELECT payment_method, COUNT(*), SUM(amount) '
    'FROM payments_analytics '
    'WHERE created_at >= now() - INTERVAL 1 DAY '
    'GROUP BY payment_method'
)
detected_storage:
  type: AnalyticalStore
  vendor: ClickHouse
  connection: localhost:9000
  signals:
    - dependency: clickhouse_driver
    - usage: analytics queries, aggregation
    - purpose: reporting, analytics
  confidence: 0.85
Storage type implications:
Different storage types have different operational and architectural implications:
storage_implications:
  RelationalDatabase:
    operational_role: "Transactional workloads"
    characteristics:
      - ACID properties
      - Structured schemas
      - Complex queries
      - Strong consistency
    typical_use_cases:
      - Financial transactions
      - User accounts
      - Order management

  DocumentStore:
    operational_role: "Flexible schema applications"
    characteristics:
      - Schema flexibility
      - Nested documents
      - Horizontal scaling
      - Eventual consistency
    typical_use_cases:
      - Product catalogs
      - User profiles
      - Content management

  KeyValueStore:
    operational_role: "High-performance caching"
    characteristics:
      - Key-value access
      - In-memory performance
      - Simple data models
      - TTL support
    typical_use_cases:
      - Session storage
      - Caching
      - Rate limiting

  ObjectStorage:
    operational_role: "Blob storage"
    characteristics:
      - Large files
      - Metadata support
      - CDN integration
      - Unlimited scalability
    typical_use_cases:
      - File storage
      - Media assets
      - Document archives

  AnalyticalStore:
    operational_role: "Analytics and reporting"
    characteristics:
      - Columnar storage
      - Fast aggregation
      - Time-series optimization
      - Compression
    typical_use_cases:
      - Business intelligence
      - Log analysis
      - Event analytics

title: 15  Data Model and Ownership Extraction
text:
15.4 Data Lifecycle Signals
Data artifacts can also reveal information about the lifecycle of stored information. Lifecycle signals indicate how data evolves over time, including creation, transformation, and deletion processes.
Lifecycle signal types:
1. Migration histories:
# migrations/001_initial.py
def upgrade():
    op.create_table('payments', ...)
    op.create_table('transactions', ...)

# migrations/002_add_refunds.py
def upgrade():
    op.create_table('refunds', ...)

# migrations/003_add_indexes.py
def upgrade():
    op.create_index('idx_payments_status', 'payments', ['status'])

# migrations/004_soft_delete.py
def upgrade():
    op.add_column('payments', sa.Column('deleted_at', sa.DateTime()))
lifecycle_analysis:
  data.payments:
    created_at: migration_001_initial
    schema_evolution:
      - migration_002_add_refunds: "Added refund capability"
      - migration_003_add_indexes: "Added performance indexes"
      - migration_004_soft_delete: "Added soft delete support"
    lifecycle_stage: mature
    confidence: 0.95
2. Archival scripts:
# scripts/archive_old_payments.py
from datetime import datetime, timedelta

def archive_payments(older_than_days=365):
    cutoff_date = datetime.now() - timedelta(days=older_than_days)

    # Move old payments to archive table
    query = """
        INSERT INTO payments_archive
        SELECT * FROM payments
        WHERE created_at < %s
    """
    execute_query(query, [cutoff_date])

    # Delete from main table
    delete_query = """
        DELETE FROM payments
        WHERE created_at < %s
    """
    execute_query(delete_query, [cutoff_date])
lifecycle_analysis:
  data.payments:
    archival_policy: "Archive payments older than 365 days"
    archival_destination: payments_archive
    retention_period: 365 days
    lifecycle_stage: archivable
    confidence: 0.88
3. Data retention configurations:
# config/data_retention.yaml
retention_policies:
  payments:
    active_retention: 365 days
    archive_retention: 7 years
    delete_after_archive: true

  transactions:
    active_retention: 90 days
    archive_retention: 5 years
    delete_after_archive: false

  logs:
    active_retention: 30 days
    archive_retention: 1 year
    delete_after_archive: true
lifecycle_analysis:
  data.payments:
    active_retention: 365 days
    archive_retention: 7 years
    delete_after_archive: true
    lifecycle_stage: active → archived → deleted
    confidence: 0.90

  data.transactions:
    active_retention: 90 days
    archive_retention: 5 years
    delete_after_archive: false
    lifecycle_stage: active → archived (permanent)
    confidence: 0.90
4. Batch processing pipelines:
# workflows/cleanup_stale_payments.py
def cleanup_stale_payments():
    """Remove failed payments older than 30 days"""

    query = """
        UPDATE payments
        SET status = 'archived'
        WHERE status = 'failed'
        AND created_at < NOW() - INTERVAL '30 days'
    """
    execute_query(query)

    logger.info(f"Cleaned up {query.rowcount} stale payments")
lifecycle_analysis:
  data.payments:
    cleanup_policy: "Archive failed payments older than 30 days"
    cleanup_action: status update to 'archived'
    lifecycle_stage: failed → archived
    confidence: 0.85
Lifecycle significance:
Understanding data lifecycle helps reconstruct the temporal dimension of data management:
lifecycle_significance:
  understanding_gained:
    - "How long data remains active"
    - "When data is archived or deleted"
    - "What transformation processes exist"
    - "What retention policies apply"

  architectural_implications:
    - "Storage tiering (hot vs. cold storage)"
    - "Compliance requirements (data retention)"
    - "Performance optimization (archival queries)"
    - "Cost management (storage costs)"

title: 15  Data Model and Ownership Extraction
text:
15.5 Ownership Inference
Once data entities are identified, extraction must determine which components own or control those entities. Ownership refers to the component responsible for maintaining the authoritative state of a data entity.
Ownership signal types:
1. Data access layers associated with specific services:
# payment_service/repositories/payment_repository.py
class PaymentRepository:
    """Payment data access layer"""

    def create(self, payment_data):
        payment = Payment(**payment_data)
        db.session.add(payment)
        db.session.commit()
        return payment

    def get(self, payment_id):
        return Payment.query.get(payment_id)

    def update(self, payment_id, updates):
        payment = Payment.query.get(payment_id)
        for key, value in updates.items():
            setattr(payment, key, value)
        db.session.commit()
        return payment

    def delete(self, payment_id):
        payment = Payment.query.get(payment_id)
        db.session.delete(payment)
        db.session.commit()
ownership_inference:
  data_entity: data.payments
  owning_component: component.payment_service
  ownership_type: strong

  signals:
    - signal: data_access_layer_location
      evidence: "payment_service/repositories/payment_repository.py"
      strength: strong

    - signal: service_direct_database_access
      evidence: "PaymentRepository directly manipulates Payment model"
      strength: strong

  ownership_confidence: 0.92
  rationale: "Payment service contains dedicated repository for payments"
2. Migration files located within component-specific directories:
payment_service/
├── repositories/
│   └── payment_repository.py
├── models/
│   └── payment.py
└── migrations/
    ├── 001_create_payments.py
    ├── 002_add_transactions.py
    └── 003_add_refunds.py
ownership_inference:
  data_entity: data.payments
  owning_component: component.payment_service
  ownership_type: strong

  signals:
    - signal: migration_location
      evidence: "payment_service/migrations/001_create_payments.py"
      strength: strong

    - signal: model_location
      evidence: "payment_service/models/payment.py"
      strength: strong

  ownership_confidence: 0.95
  rationale: "Migrations and models are co-located with payment service"
3. Service-level configuration referencing particular databases:
# payment_service/config.py
class Config:
    DATABASE_URI = os.getenv('DATABASE_URI')
    DATABASE_SCHEMA = 'payment'

# notification_service/config.py
class Config:
    DATABASE_URI = os.getenv('DATABASE_URI')
    DATABASE_SCHEMA = 'notification'
ownership_inference:
  data.payments:
    owning_component: component.payment_service
    schema: payment
    ownership_confidence: 0.90

  data.notifications:
    owning_component: component.notification_service
    schema: notification
    ownership_confidence: 0.90
4. Write operations performed by specific components:
# payment_service/services/payment_service.py
class PaymentService:
    def create_payment(self, request):
        """Create new payment (write operation)"""
        payment = self.repository.create(request.dict())
        return payment

    def process_payment(self, payment_id):
        """Process payment (write operation)"""
        payment = self.repository.get(payment_id)
        payment.status = 'processing'
        self.repository.update(payment_id, {'status': 'processing'})
        # ... processing logic ...
        payment.status = 'completed'
        self.repository.update(payment_id, {'status': 'completed'})
        return payment
# analytics_service/services/analytics_service.py
class AnalyticsService:
    def get_payment_stats(self, date_range):
        """Get payment statistics (read-only operation)"""
        query = """
            SELECT COUNT(*), SUM(amount)
            FROM payments
            WHERE created_at BETWEEN %s AND %s
        """
        return execute_query(query, date_range)
ownership_inference:
  data.payments:
    owning_component: component.payment_service
    ownership_type: write_operations

    component_access:
      - component: payment_service
        access_type: [read, write]
        operations: [create, update, delete]
        ownership_indication: strong

      - component: analytics_service
        access_type: [read]
        operations: [query, aggregate]
        ownership_indication: none (read-only access)

  ownership_confidence: 0.88
  rationale: "Payment service performs write operations, analytics service only reads"
Ownership confidence levels:
ownership_confidence:
  strong:
    indicators:
      - dedicated_repository_class
      - migrations_co_located
      - write_operations_present
    confidence: 0.90-0.98

  moderate:
    indicators:
      - read_operations_present
      - shared_repository
      - cross_service_access
    confidence: 0.70-0.85

  weak:
    indicators:
      - indirect_access
      - no_clear_ownership
      - ambiguous_responsibility
    confidence: 0.40-0.65

  unknown:
    indicators:
      - insufficient_evidence
      - conflicting_signals
    confidence: 0.0-0.35

title: 15  Data Model and Ownership Extraction
text:
15.6 Detecting Consistency Risks
Repository artifacts can also reveal potential data consistency risks. One common risk is the presence of multiple writers to the same data entity. When multiple components write to the same storage resource, coordination mechanisms are required to maintain consistency.
Consistency risk signals:
1. Multiple services referencing the same schema:
# payment_service/repositories/payment_repository.py
class PaymentRepository:
    def update_status(self, payment_id, status):
        payment = Payment.query.get(payment_id)
        payment.status = status
        db.session.commit()

# payment_worker/repositories/payment_repository.py
class PaymentRepository:
    def update_status(self, payment_id, status):
        payment = Payment.query.get(payment_id)
        payment.status = status
        db.session.commit()
consistency_risk:
  data_entity: data.payments
  risk_type: multiple_writers
  severity: high

  writers:
    - component: payment_service
      operations: [update_status, create_payment]
    - component: payment_worker
      operations: [update_status, process_payment]

  coordination_required: true
  coordination_mechanisms:
    - mechanism: database_transactions
      status: present
    - mechanism: optimistic_locking
      status: unknown
    - mechanism: message_queue
      status: present

  risk_confidence: 0.88
  recommendation: "Investigate coordination mechanisms and potential race conditions"
2. Duplicated data access layers across components:
payment_service/
└── repositories/
    └── payment_repository.py

payment_worker/
└── repositories/
    └── payment_repository.py

analytics_service/
└── repositories/
    └── payment_repository.py
consistency_risk:
  data_entity: data.payments
  risk_type: duplicated_access_layers
  severity: medium

  duplicated_repositories:
    - payment_service/repositories/payment_repository.py
    - payment_worker/repositories/payment_repository.py
    - analytics_service/repositories/payment_repository.py

  potential_issues:
    - "Inconsistent business logic across repositories"
    - "Different validation rules"
    - "Code duplication and maintenance burden"

  risk_confidence: 0.85
  recommendation: "Consider consolidating repositories into shared library"
3. Replication or synchronization scripts:
# scripts/sync_payments_to_analytics.py
def sync_payments_to_analytics():
    """Sync payment data to analytics database"""

    # Read from operational database
    payments = query_operational_db("""
        SELECT * FROM payments
        WHERE updated_at > last_sync_time
    """)

    # Write to analytics database
    for payment in payments:
        upsert_analytics_db(payment)

    logger.info(f"Synced {len(payments)} payments to analytics")
consistency_risk:
  data_entity: data.payments
  risk_type: replication_delay
  severity: medium

  replication_flow:
    source: operational_database.payments
    destination: analytics_database.payments
    mechanism: scheduled_sync_script
    sync_frequency: hourly

  potential_issues:
    - "Eventual consistency between databases"
    - "Sync failures cause data divergence"
    - "Analytics data may be stale"

  risk_confidence: 0.82
  recommendation: "Monitor sync latency and implement error handling"
4. Cross-database transactions:
# payment_service/services/payment_service.py
def create_payment_with_log(self, request):
    """Create payment and log to separate database"""

    # Transaction in main database
    with db.session.begin():
        payment = Payment(**request.dict())
        db.session.add(payment)
        payment_id = payment.id

    # Log to separate database (no transaction!)
    log_db.execute(
        "INSERT INTO payment_logs (payment_id, action) VALUES (?, ?)",
        [payment_id, 'created']
    )

    return payment
consistency_risk:
  risk_type: distributed_transaction
  severity: high

  involved_databases:
    - operational_database.payments
    - log_database.payment_logs

  issue: "No atomic transaction across databases"
  potential_failures:
    - "Payment created but log not written"
    - "Inconsistent state between databases"

  risk_confidence: 0.90
  recommendation: "Implement saga pattern or use distributed transaction coordinator"
Consistency risk assessment:
consistency_risk_matrix:
  risk_severity:
    critical:
      - multiple_writers_without_coordination
      - distributed_transactions_without_rollback
    high:
      - shared_write_access
      - replication_without_conflict_resolution
    medium:
      - duplicated_access_layers
      - eventual_consistency_requirements
    low:
      - read_access_multiple_components
      - replication_with_conflict_resolution

  recommended_actions:
    critical:
      - "Implement coordination mechanisms"
      - "Add distributed transaction support"
      - "Use message queues for write serialization"
    high:
      - "Review ownership boundaries"
      - "Implement conflict resolution"
      - "Add monitoring for consistency violations"
    medium:
      - "Consolidate shared code"
      - "Document replication patterns"
      - "Add sync monitoring"
    low:
      - "Document read-only access"
      - "Monitor replication lag"

title: 15  Data Model and Ownership Extraction
text:
15.8 Chapter Summary

Data is a stable structural element that often persists across system evolution
Data extraction identifies persistent data structures and ownership relationships
Data entities are extracted from ORM models, migration files, DDL statements, and schema definitions
Storage types include relational databases, document stores, key-value stores, object storage, and analytical stores
Each storage type has different operational roles and typical use cases
Data lifecycle signals reveal how data evolves through creation, transformation, archival, and deletion
Ownership inference identifies components that control data entities through data access layers, migration locations, configuration, and write operations
Consistency risks are detected through multiple writers, duplicated access layers, replication scripts, and cross-database transactions
The output consists of data entities (with schema, relationships, lifecycle) and an ownership map (with component access and consistency risks)
Data entities and ownership relationships form the data layer of the blueprint
Understanding data ownership reveals component boundaries, architectural constraints, and integration points

Next Chapter: Chapter 14 introduces Workflow and Control-Plane Extraction—identifying coordination mechanisms that orchestrate how work flows across the architecture. This chapter covers workflow signals, scheduler and trigger mechanisms, state stores, execution semantics, and control-plane identification.

title: 16  Workflow and Control-Plane Extraction
text:
16.1 What Is a Workflow?
While components define structural units of responsibility and interfaces define interaction surfaces, many systems rely on explicit coordination mechanisms that orchestrate how work flows across the architecture. These coordination mechanisms are represented as workflows.
Workflows describe ordered or conditional execution across components, tasks, or services. They often encode the operational logic that determines how capabilities are realized over time. Blueprint extraction therefore reconstructs both the workflow structures and the control-plane mechanisms that govern execution.
The result is a representation of the system’s control flow architecture, revealing how capabilities are operationalized through orchestrated execution across components and tasks.
A workflow is a structured definition of execution order and dependencies between tasks or operations. Workflows encode the orchestration logic that determines when and how tasks execute.
Workflow characteristics:
1. Task coordination:
Workflows coordinate the execution of multiple tasks in a specified order or according to defined conditions.
2. Dependency specification:
Workflows explicitly define dependencies between tasks, ensuring that prerequisite tasks complete before dependent tasks begin.
3. Conditional execution:
Workflows may include conditional logic that determines which tasks execute based on runtime conditions.
4. Error handling and retries:
Workflows define how to handle failures, including retry logic and fallback behaviors.
Workflow types:
The workflow ontology defines several common workflow archetypes:
crumbs: Part III — Ontology Mapping: From Repo to System Graph

title: 16  Workflow and Control-Plane Extraction
text:
workflow:
  id: workflow.payment_processing
  description: "Process payment through multiple stages"

  tasks:
    - task: validate_payment
      type: validation
      position: 1

    - task: charge_gateway
      type: external_call
      position: 2
      depends_on: validate_payment

    - task: record_transaction
      type: database_write
      position: 3
      depends_on: charge_gateway

    - task: send_notification
      type: notification
      position: 4
      depends_on: record_transaction


workflow:
  id: workflow.settlement
  description: "Settle completed payments"

  dependencies:
    - task: aggregate_payments
      depends_on: []

    - task: calculate_fees
      depends_on: [aggregate_payments]

    - task: transfer_to_bank
      depends_on: [calculate_fees]

    - task: update_payment_status
      depends_on: [transfer_to_bank]


workflow:
  id: workflow.payment_handling
  description: "Handle payment with conditional logic"

  conditional_logic:
    - condition: payment.status == 'failed'
      execute_tasks: [retry_payment, notify_support]

    - condition: payment.status == 'completed'
      execute_tasks: [send_confirmation, update_analytics]

    - condition: payment.amount > 10000
      execute_tasks: [manual_review_required]


workflow:
  id: workflow.payment_processing
  description: "Process payment with error handling"

  error_handling:
    task: charge_gateway
      on_failure:
        strategy: retry
        max_retries: 3
        backoff: exponential
      fallback_task: queue_for_manual_review


workflow_types:
  Pipeline:
    role: "Sequential execution of tasks"
    characteristics:
      - Linear task sequence
      - Each task depends on previous
      - No branching
    examples: [ETL pipelines, batch processing]

  DAG:
    role: "Directed acyclic graph of tasks"
    characteristics:
      - Task dependencies form graph
      - Multiple dependency paths possible
      - No cycles
    examples: [Data processing workflows, CI/CD pipelines]

  Schedule:
    role: "Time-based execution"
    characteristics:
      - Triggered by time
      - Cron expressions or intervals
      - Recurring execution
    examples: [Periodic jobs, maintenance tasks]

  Event:
    role: "Event-driven execution"
    characteristics:
      - Triggered by events
      - Message queues or webhooks
      - Reactive execution
    examples: [Event handlers, message consumers]
