title: 17  Kernel Slot Filling Rules
text:
17.6 Chapter Summary

Blueprint Kernel is the minimal structural representation of system architecture
Kernel consists of six mandatory slots: Goal, Capabilities, Components, Interfaces, Data, Workflows
Each slot captures a distinct architectural dimension and requires specific evidence
Evidence requirements ensure kernel slots are populated with traceable evidence
Goal slot requires synthesis from documentation and capabilities (high complexity)
Capabilities slot is directly observable from interfaces and workflows (medium complexity)
Components slot requires boundary inference (high complexity)
Interfaces slot is explicitly defined in code (medium complexity)
Data slot is explicitly defined in schemas (low complexity)
Workflows slot requires dependency analysis (high complexity)
Optional Constraint slot captures architectural limitations and guarantees
Constraint extraction faces inconsistency, incompleteness, and ambiguity challenges
Triangular consistency checks validate relationships between capabilities, interfaces, and workflows
Consistency validation ensures each capability has interface and workflow support
Kernel completion produces a coherent architectural representation with all slots populated
Quality metrics measure slot completion, confidence levels, and consistency scores
Completed kernel serves as canonical representation for blueprint view generation

Next Chapter: Chapter 16 introduces Views Generation—how to project the canonical kernel into different analytical perspectives. This chapter covers the product view (user-visible functionality), architecture view (structural relationships), operations view (runtime topology), and security/trust view (boundaries and access control).

title: 18  Views Generation (Projections)
text:
18.1 Kernel-First, Views-Later Approach
The Blueprint Kernel represents the canonical structural model of a system. However, different stakeholders require different perspectives on that structure. Developers, operators, and reviewers often focus on distinct aspects of the system architecture. A single representation cannot simultaneously optimize for all of these viewpoints.
Blueprint views solve this problem by providing projections of the kernel. A view is a derived representation that reorganizes kernel information to emphasize a particular architectural dimension. Views do not introduce new architectural facts; they rearrange existing kernel entities and relationships into different analytical perspectives.
Blueprint extraction therefore follows a kernel-first, views-later approach. Once the kernel has been populated and validated, views are generated through deterministic projection rules. This approach ensures that all views remain consistent with the underlying architectural model.
The extraction process prioritizes kernel construction over view generation. This ordering ensures architectural integrity and enables flexible view generation.
Why kernel-first matters:
View generation pipeline:
Projection vs. extraction:

title: 18  Views Generation (Projections)
text:
kernel_first_principles:
  architectural_integrity:
    principle: "Kernel is the single source of truth"
    benefit: "Prevents inconsistencies between views"
    mechanism: "Views are derived from kernel, not independently"

  evidence_traceability:
    principle: "Evidence traces to kernel, not to views"
    benefit: "Simplifies audit and validation"
    mechanism: "Kernel entities reference evidence directly"

  update_consistency:
    principle: "Kernel updates propagate to all views"
    benefit: "Views automatically stay synchronized"
    mechanism: "Deterministic projection rules"

  flexibility:
    principle: "New views can be added without changing kernel"
    benefit: "Enables future extensibility"
    mechanism: "View generation is independent of kernel structure"

view_generation_pipeline:
  step_1_kernel_construction:
    action: "Construct canonical kernel from evidence"
    output: "BlueprintKernel with six slots"

  step_2_kernel_validation:
    action: "Validate kernel completeness and consistency"
    output: "Validated kernel with quality metrics"

  step_3_view_projection:
    action: "Generate views through projection rules"
    output: "Multiple view representations"

  step_4_view_validation:
    action: "Validate views against kernel"
    output: "Consistent, validated views"

  dependency: "Each step depends on previous step completion"

projection_vs_extraction:
  extraction:
    process: "Infer architecture from repository artifacts"
    direction: "Artifacts → Kernel"
    complexity: "High - requires interpretation and evidence"
    uncertainty: "Significant - involves inference"

  projection:
    process: "Reorganize kernel into perspectives"
    direction: "Kernel → Views"
    complexity: "Low - deterministic transformation"
    uncertainty: "Minimal - no new inference"

title: 18  Views Generation (Projections)
text:
18.2 Product View
The product view describes the system from the perspective of user-visible functionality. It focuses on the capabilities the system provides and the interfaces through which those capabilities are accessed. The purpose of this view is to explain what the system does rather than how it is implemented.
Product view characteristics:
product_view:
  focus: "User-visible functionality"
  audience: [product_managers, users, stakeholders]
  questions_answered:
    - "What can this system do?"
    - "How do users interact with it?"
    - "What problems does it solve?"
  excludes:
    - "Implementation details"
    - "Component structure"
    - "Data models"
    - "Operational aspects"
Product view projection rules:
product_view_projection:
  source_slots:
    - Goal (primary)
    - Capabilities (primary)
    - Interfaces (primary)

  projection_rules:
    - rule: "Include all capabilities"
      source: kernel.Capabilities
      output: view.capabilities

    - rule: "Include external interfaces"
      source: kernel.Interfaces
      filter: "interfaces accessible to external users"
      output: view.interfaces

    - rule: "Include high-level goal"
      source: kernel.Goal
      output: view.system_purpose

    - rule: "Exclude internal interfaces"
      source: kernel.Interfaces
      filter: "exclude interfaces for internal use"
      output: none

    - rule: "Exclude implementation details"
      source: kernel.Components
      output: none

    - rule: "Exclude data models"
      source: kernel.Data
      output: none

    - rule: "Exclude workflows"
      source: kernel.Workflows
      output: none
Product view example:
product_view:
  view_name: Product View
  view_type: user_facing
  generated_from: kernel.payment_system_v1

  system_purpose:
    value: "Process payment transactions and manage refunds for e-commerce platforms"
    confidence: 0.70

  capabilities:
    - id: capability.create_payment
      name: "Create Payment"
      description: "Create new payment transactions"
      interfaces:
        - interface: POST /payments
          protocol: HTTP
          access: public
      confidence: 0.95

    - id: capability.process_payment
      name: "Process Payment"
      description: "Process payment through payment gateway"
      interfaces:
        - interface: payment_events (payment.created)
          protocol: Event
          access: internal
      confidence: 0.90

    - id: capability.refund_payment
      name: "Refund Payment"
      description: "Process payment refunds"
      interfaces:
        - interface: POST /payments/{id}/refund
          protocol: HTTP
          access: public
      confidence: 0.92

    - id: capability.send_notification
      name: "Send Notifications"
      description: "Send payment status notifications"
      interfaces:
        - interface: payment_events (payment.completed)
          protocol: Event
          access: internal
      confidence: 0.88

  external_interfaces:
    - id: interface.payment_api
      type: API
      protocol: HTTP
      access: public
      capabilities:
        - create_payment
        - refund_payment
      confidence: 0.98

  user_stories:
    - story: "As a merchant, I want to create payments"
      capability: create_payment
      interface: POST /payments

    - story: "As a merchant, I want to refund payments"
      capability: refund_payment
      interface: POST /payments/{id}/refund

  limitations:
    - note: "Product view is partial, inferred from capabilities"
    - confidence: "Product intent may not be explicitly documented"
    - recommendation: "Review with product team for validation"
Product view generation challenges:
product_view_challenges:
  challenge_1_implicit_product_intent:
    problem: "Repositories rarely encode product intent explicitly"
    signals:
      - README files may be incomplete
      - Product documentation may be outdated
      - User stories may be missing
    mitigation: "Infer product view from capabilities and interfaces"
    confidence: "Lower confidence for product view"

  challenge_2_capability_to_feature_mapping:
    problem: "Technical capabilities may not map directly to user features"
    signals:
      - Multiple capabilities may form one feature
      - One capability may support multiple features
      - Feature boundaries may be ambiguous
    mitigation: "Group capabilities into logical features"
    confidence: "Requires domain knowledge"

  challenge_3_user_interface_abstraction:
    problem: "API interfaces may not match user interaction model"
    signals:
      - APIs expose technical operations
      - Users interact through different abstractions
      - Command-line vs. GUI differences
    mitigation: "Map interfaces to user actions"
    confidence: "Moderate"

  recommendation: "Product view should be constructed cautiously and validated with product stakeholders"

title: 18  Views Generation (Projections)
text:
18.3 Architecture View
The architecture view is the primary artifact produced by blueprint extraction. This view focuses on the structural composition of the system and the relationships between its core elements.
Architecture view characteristics:
architecture_view:
  focus: "Structural composition and relationships"
  audience: [architects, developers, system_designers]
  questions_answered:
    - "How is the system structured?"
    - "What components exist and how do they relate?"
    - "How do components interact?"
    - "What data do components operate on?"
  includes:
    - "Component structure"
    - "Interface relationships"
    - "Data ownership"
    - "Workflow coordination"
  excludes:
    - "Implementation details"
    - "Runtime deployment"
    - "Operational monitoring"
Architecture view projection rules:
architecture_view_projection:
  source_slots:
    - Components (primary)
    - Interfaces (primary)
    - Data (primary)
    - Workflows (primary)
    - Capabilities (secondary)

  projection_rules:
    - rule: "Include all components"
      source: kernel.Components
      output: view.components

    - rule: "Include all interfaces"
      source: kernel.Interfaces
      output: view.interfaces

    - rule: "Include component-interface relationships"
      source: kernel.Components, kernel.Interfaces
      transform: "map components to interfaces they expose"
      output: view.component_interfaces

    - rule: "Include data entities"
      source: kernel.Data
      output: view.data_entities

    - rule: "Include data ownership"
      source: kernel.Components, kernel.Data
      transform: "map components to data they own"
      output: view.data_ownership

    - rule: "Include workflows"
      source: kernel.Workflows
      output: view.workflows

    - rule: "Include workflow-component relationships"
      source: kernel.Workflows, kernel.Components
      transform: "map workflows to components they invoke"
      output: view.workflow_components

    - rule: "Include capability-component relationships"
      source: kernel.Capabilities, kernel.Components
      transform: "map capabilities to components that implement them"
      output: view.capability_components
Architecture view example:
architecture_view:
  view_name: Architecture View
  view_type: structural
  generated_from: kernel.payment_system_v1

  components:
    - id: component.payment_service
      type: Service
      responsibilities:
        - "Handle payment API requests"
        - "Manage payment data"
        - "Coordinate payment processing"
      interfaces:
        - exposes: interface.payment_api
          type: API
      data:
        - owns: data.payments
        - owns: data.transactions
        - owns: data.refunds
      workflows:
        - implements: workflow.payment_processing
      confidence: 0.95

    - id: component.payment_worker
      type: Worker
      responsibilities:
        - "Process payments asynchronously"
        - "Handle gateway interactions"
        - "Record transaction results"
      interfaces:
        - exposes: interface.payment_events
          type: Event
      data:
        - reads: data.payments
        - writes: data.transactions
      workflows:
        - participates_in: workflow.payment_processing
      confidence: 0.92

    - id: component.notification_service
      type: Service
      responsibilities:
        - "Send payment notifications"
        - "Manage notification templates"
      interfaces:
        - exposes: interface.notification_api
          type: API
        - consumes: interface.payment_events
          type: Event
      data:
        - owns: data.notifications
      workflows:
        - participates_in: workflow.payment_processing
      confidence: 0.88

    - id: component.orchestration_engine
      type: ControlPlane
      responsibilities:
        - "Coordinate workflow execution"
        - "Manage task dependencies"
        - "Track workflow state"
      interfaces:
        - exposes: interface.workflow_management_api
          type: API
      workflows:
        - controls: workflow.payment_processing
        - controls: workflow.daily_settlement
      confidence: 0.90

  interfaces:
    - id: interface.payment_api
      type: API
      protocol: HTTP
      exposed_by: component.payment_service
      operations:
        - POST /payments (create_payment)
        - GET /payments/{id} (get_payment)
        - POST /payments/{id}/refund (refund_payment)
      confidence: 0.98

    - id: interface.payment_events
      type: Event
      protocol: Kafka
      exposed_by: component.payment_worker
      consumed_by: component.notification_service
      events:
        - payment.created
        - payment.completed
        - payment.failed
      confidence: 0.92

  data_entities:
    - id: data.payments
      type: Table
      storage: PostgreSQL
      owned_by: component.payment_service
      read_by: component.payment_worker
      schema:
        - id (primary_key)
        - amount (decimal)
        - currency (string)
        - status (string)
        - created_at (timestamp)
      confidence: 0.98

    - id: data.transactions
      type: Table
      storage: PostgreSQL
      owned_by: component.payment_service
      schema:
        - id (primary_key)
        - payment_id (foreign_key)
        - gateway (string)
        - amount (decimal)
        - status (string)
      confidence: 0.98

  workflows:
    - id: workflow.payment_processing
      type: DAG
      controlled_by: component.orchestration_engine
      implemented_by: component.payment_service
      participates:
        - component.payment_worker
        - component.notification_service
      tasks:
        - validate_payment (component.payment_service)
        - charge_gateway (component.payment_worker)
        - record_transaction (component.payment_service)
        - send_notification (component.notification_service)
      confidence: 0.98

  component_graph:
    nodes: [payment_service, payment_worker, notification_service, orchestration_engine]
    edges:
      - payment_service → payment_api (exposes)
      - payment_service → payment_worker (coordinates)
      - payment_worker → payment_events (exposes)
      - payment_service → payments (owns)
      - payment_worker → transactions (writes)
      - notification_service → payment_events (consumes)
      - orchestration_engine → payment_processing (controls)

  architectural_patterns:
    - pattern: Service-Worker separation
      description: "API service and background worker separation"
      components: [payment_service, payment_worker]

    - pattern: Event-driven communication
      description: "Async communication through events"
      interfaces: [payment_events]

    - pattern: Centralized orchestration
      description: "Workflow engine coordinates execution"
      component: orchestration_engine
Architecture view significance:
architecture_view_significance:
  primary_artifact:
    reason: "Most accurate representation of system structure"
    confidence: "High - directly maps to kernel"
    completeness: "Comprehensive - includes all structural elements"

  use_cases:
    - system_understanding: "Understand system architecture at a glance"
    - design_review: "Evaluate architectural decisions"
    - impact_analysis: "Assess impact of changes"
    - comparison: "Compare architectures across systems"
    - documentation: "Document system structure"

  advantages:
    - completeness: "All structural elements represented"
    - consistency: "Consistent with kernel structure"
    - accuracy: "Based on extracted evidence"
    - traceability: "Elements reference evidence"

title: 18  Views Generation (Projections)
text:
18.4 Operations View
The operations view highlights the runtime and operational structure of the system. While the architecture view focuses on structural relationships, the operations view emphasizes how the system is deployed, executed, and monitored in practice.
Operations view characteristics:
operations_view:
  focus: "Runtime and operational structure"
  audience: [devops_engineers, sre, operations_teams]
  questions_answered:
    - "How is the system deployed?"
    - "What are the runtime dependencies?"
    - "How is the system monitored?"
    - "What are the deployment units?"
  includes:
    - "Deployment topology"
    - "Runtime dependencies"
    - "Monitoring and logging"
    - "Scaling configuration"
  excludes:
    - "Internal component structure"
    - "Business logic"
    - "Data model details"
Operations view projection rules:
operations_view_projection:
  source_slots:
    - Components (filtered by deployment signals)
    - Constraints (operational)
    - Evidence (deployment and observability)

  projection_rules:
    - rule: "Include deployment units"
      source: kernel.Components
      filter: "components with deployment configuration"
      transform: "extract deployment information"
      output: view.deployment_units

    - rule: "Include runtime dependencies"
      source: kernel.Components
      filter: "components with dependency signals"
      transform: "extract external service dependencies"
      output: view.runtime_dependencies

    - rule: "Include monitoring configuration"
      source: deployment and observability signals
      filter: "monitoring, logging, metrics configuration"
      transform: "extract monitoring setup"
      output: view.monitoring

    - rule: "Include operational constraints"
      source: kernel.Constraints
      filter: "performance, deployment, regulatory constraints"
      output: view.operational_constraints

    - rule: "Include infrastructure requirements"
      source: deployment signals
      filter: "infrastructure definitions, resource requirements"
      output: view.infrastructure_requirements
Operations view example:
operations_view:
  view_name: Operations View
  view_type: operational
  generated_from: kernel.payment_system_v1 and deployment_evidence

  deployment_units:
    - unit: payment_service
      type: API Service
      deployment:
        - platform: Kubernetes
          replicas: 3
          resource_requests:
            cpu: "500m"
            memory: "512Mi"
          resource_limits:
            cpu: "2000m"
            memory: "2048Mi"
        - container: payment-app:latest
          image: payment-app:latest
          ports: [8000]
          environment:
            - DATABASE_URL
            - REDIS_URL
      endpoints:
        - type: HTTP
          port: 8000
          protocol: HTTP
      health_checks:
        - liveness: /health
        - readiness: /ready
      confidence: 0.92

    - unit: payment_worker
      type: Worker
      deployment:
        - platform: Kubernetes
          replicas: 2
          resource_requests:
            cpu: "1000m"
            memory: "1024Mi"
          resource_limits:
            cpu: "3000m"
            memory: "3072Mi"
        - container: payment-worker:latest
          image: payment-worker:latest
      dependencies:
        - external_service: Redis
          connection: redis:6379
          purpose: "Message queue"
      health_checks:
        - liveness: process_health_check
      confidence: 0.90

    - unit: notification_service
      type: API Service
      deployment:
        - platform: Kubernetes
          replicas: 1
          resource_requests:
            cpu: "200m"
            memory: "256Mi"
      endpoints:
        - type: HTTP
          port: 8001
      confidence: 0.88

  runtime_dependencies:
    - dependency: PostgreSQL
      type: Database
      connection: postgresql://payment-db:5432/payment_db
      used_by: [payment_service]
      criticality: critical

    - dependency: Redis
      type: Cache and Message Queue
      connection: redis://redis:6379/0
      used_by: [payment_worker, payment_service]
      criticality: critical

    - dependency: Payment Gateway API
      type: External Service
      connection: https://api.payment-gateway.com
      used_by: [payment_worker]
      criticality: critical

  monitoring:
    logging:
      - level: INFO
      - format: JSON
      - destination: stdout

    metrics:
      - type: Prometheus
        endpoint: /metrics
        port: 8000
        metrics:
          - payment_requests_total
          - payment_processing_duration_seconds
          - payment_errors_total

    tracing:
      - provider: OpenTelemetry
        sample_rate: 0.1
        export_to: Jaeger

    alerting:
      - alert: HighErrorRate
        condition: rate(errors[5m]) > 0.1
        severity: warning
      - alert: ProcessingDelay
        condition: processing_duration > 30s
        severity: warning

  operational_constraints:
    - constraint: Payment processing timeout
      type: Performance
      value: "30 seconds maximum"
      confidence: 0.75

    - constraint: API rate limiting
      type: Performance
      value: "100 requests per minute"
      confidence: 0.82

    - constraint: PCI DSS compliance
      type: Regulatory
      value: "Payment data must comply with PCI DSS"
      confidence: 0.85

  infrastructure_requirements:
    - requirement: Database storage
      type: storage
      size: "100 GB initially, scaling to 1 TB"
      type: "PostgreSQL with replication"

    - requirement: High availability
      type: reliability
      specification: "99.9% uptime SLA"

    - requirement: Disaster recovery
      type: reliability
      specification: "Backup every hour, 30-day retention"
Operations view value:
operations_view_value:
  operational_insights:
    - deployment_topology: "How services are deployed and scaled"
    - resource_requirements: "CPU, memory, storage needs"
    - external_dependencies: "Critical external services"
    - monitoring_coverage: "What is monitored and how"
    - failure_modes: "What happens when things fail"

  practical_uses:
    - capacity_planning: "Plan infrastructure resources"
    - incident_response: "Understand system topology during outages"
    - performance_tuning: "Identify bottlenecks"
    - cost_optimization: "Right-size infrastructure"
    - security_hardening: "Identify security boundaries"

title: 18  Views Generation (Projections)
text:
18.5 Security and Trust View
The security and trust view focuses on system boundaries and trust relationships. Security concerns often appear indirectly in repositories through authentication mechanisms, authorization rules, encryption libraries, or access control configuration.
Security view characteristics:
security_view:
  focus: "System boundaries and trust relationships"
  audience: [security_engineers, architects, compliance_officers]
  questions_answered:
    - "Where are the trust boundaries?"
    - "How is authentication handled?"
    - "What are the authorization rules?"
    - "What data is sensitive?"
  includes:
    - "Trust boundaries between components"
    - "Authentication and authorization mechanisms"
    - "Sensitive data access paths"
    - "Security-relevant interfaces"
  excludes:
    - "Non-security functionality"
    - "Business logic details"
Security view projection rules:
security_view_projection:
  source_slots:
    - Components (security-relevant)
    - Interfaces (security-relevant)
    - Constraints (security)
    - Evidence (security hooks)

  projection_rules:
    - rule: "Include trust boundaries"
      source: kernel.Components
      filter: "components with security boundaries"
      transform: "identify component isolation"
      output: view.trust_boundaries

    - rule: "Include authentication mechanisms"
      source: security_evidence
      filter: "authentication middleware, JWT tokens"
      transform: "extract authentication setup"
      output: view.authentication

    - rule: "Include authorization rules"
      source: security_evidence
      filter: "authorization policies, role-based access"
      transform: "extract authorization rules"
      output: view.authorization

    - rule: "Include sensitive data access"
      source: kernel.Data
      filter: "data with security classification"
      transform: "map data to access controls"
      output: view.sensitive_data_access

    - rule: "Include security constraints"
      source: kernel.Constraints
      filter: "security constraints"
      output: view.security_constraints
Security view example:
security_view:
  view_name: Security and Trust View
  view_type: security
  generated_from: kernel.payment_system_v1 and security_evidence

  trust_boundaries:
    - boundary: external_api_boundary
      description: "Boundary between external users and API service"
      components: [component.payment_service]
      controls:
        - authentication: JWT required
        - rate_limiting: 100 req/min
        - tls: HTTPS only
      confidence: 0.90

    - boundary: internal_service_boundary
      description: "Boundary between API service and internal workers"
      components: [component.payment_service, component.payment_worker]
      controls:
        - authentication: Service-to-service tokens
        - network: Private network
        - encryption: TLS for internal traffic
      confidence: 0.88

    - boundary: data_boundary
      description: "Boundary around payment data"
      components: [component.payment_service]
      data: [data.payments, data.transactions]
      controls:
        - encryption: AES-256 at rest
        - access_control: Role-based
        - audit_logging: All access logged
      confidence: 0.92

  authentication:
    - mechanism: JWT
      provider: "Custom implementation"
      token_issuer: "payment-system"
      token_expiration: "1 hour"
      refresh_token: Supported
      confidence: 0.88

    - mechanism: Service-to-service
      type: API Keys
      rotation: "Every 90 days"
      scope: "Internal service communication"
      confidence: 0.85

  authorization:
    - model: Role-Based Access Control (RBAC)
      roles:
        - role: admin
          permissions: [read, write, delete, admin]
        - role: merchant
          permissions: [read, write]
        - role: auditor
          permissions: [read]
      confidence: 0.82

    - api_endpoint_authorization:
      - endpoint: POST /payments
        required_roles: [merchant, admin]
      - endpoint: GET /payments/{id}
        required_roles: [merchant, admin, auditor]
      - endpoint: POST /payments/{id}/refund
        required_roles: [merchant, admin]
      confidence: 0.88

  sensitive_data_access:
    - data: data.payments
      classification: PII + Financial
      encryption: AES-256
      access_controls:
        - component: component.payment_service
          access: read_write
          audit: true
        - component: component.payment_worker
          access: read_write
          audit: true
      retention: "7 years"
      confidence: 0.92

    - data: data.transactions
      classification: Financial
      encryption: AES-256
      access_controls:
        - component: component.payment_service
          access: read_write
          audit: true
      retention: "7 years"
      confidence: 0.92

  security_constraints:
    - constraint: PCI DSS compliance
      type: Regulatory
      requirements:
        - "Encryption at rest"
        - "Encryption in transit"
        - "Access logging"
        - "Regular audits"
      confidence: 0.85

    - constraint: Payment data encryption
      type: Security
      requirements:
        - "AES-256 encryption"
        - "Key rotation every 90 days"
      confidence: 0.92

    - constraint: API authentication
      type: Security
      requirements:
        - "JWT authentication for all endpoints"
        - "HTTPS only"
      confidence: 0.90

  security_events:
    - event: failed_authentication
      response: "Log and alert on repeated failures"
    - event: unauthorized_access_attempt
      response: "Block IP and alert"
    - event: data_access_violation
      response: "Alert and audit"
Security view significance:
security_view_significance:
  security_insights:
    - trust_boundaries: "Where system boundaries exist"
    - attack_surface: "What interfaces are exposed"
    - data_classification: "What data is sensitive"
    - access_controls: "How access is managed"
    - compliance_requirements: "What regulations apply"

  practical_uses:
    - security_review: "Identify security vulnerabilities"
    - penetration_testing: "Guide security testing"
    - compliance_audit: "Verify regulatory compliance"
    - incident_response: "Understand security posture"
    - threat_modeling: "Identify attack vectors"

title: 18  Views Generation (Projections)
text:
18.6 Projection as Deterministic Transformation
Blueprint views are not extracted independently. Instead, they are generated from the kernel through deterministic projection rules. This approach ensures that all views remain consistent with the underlying architectural model.
Deterministic projection principles:
deterministic_projection:
  principle_1_single_source:
    name: "Single source of truth"
    rule: "Views derived only from kernel"
    benefit: "Consistency across views"

  principle_2_no_inference:
    name: "No new inference in views"
    rule: "Views only reorganize existing kernel information"
    benefit: "No additional uncertainty introduced"

  principle_3_reproducible:
    name: "Reproducible generation"
    rule: "Same kernel produces same views"
    benefit: "Predictable and testable"

  principle_4_validatable:
    name: "Validatable against kernel"
    rule: "Views can be validated against kernel"
    benefit: "Quality assurance possible"
Projection rule template:
projection_rule_template:
  rule_id: "rule_identifier"
  rule_name: "Descriptive rule name"
  rule_description: "Clear explanation of what rule does"

  source:
    slots: ["kernel.Slot1", "kernel.Slot2"]
    filters: ["filter_criteria"]
    transformations: ["transformation_steps"]

  output:
    target_view: "ViewType"
    output_structure: "Output schema"

  validation:
    checks: ["validation_criteria"]
    error_handling: "error_response"

  example:
    input: "Sample kernel data"
    output: "Sample view data"
View generation algorithm:
view_generation_algorithm:
  input: "Completed and validated kernel"

  step_1_select_view_type:
    action: "Select view type to generate"
    options: [product, architecture, operations, security]

  step_2_apply_projection_rules:
    action: "Apply view-specific projection rules"
    process: "Transform kernel according to rules"

  step_3_filter_elements:
    action: "Filter elements based on view focus"
    criteria: "View-specific inclusion/exclusion"

  step_4_reorganize_structure:
    action: "Reorganize elements for view perspective"
    transformation: "Structural reorganization"

  step_5_validate_view:
    action: "Validate view against kernel"
    checks: "Completeness, consistency, traceability"

  step_6_generate_output:
    action: "Generate final view representation"
    format: "Structured view document"

  output: "Generated view ready for use"
View validation against kernel:
view_validation:
  completeness_check:
    - check: "All required elements present"
      severity: error
    - check: "No missing kernel elements referenced"
      severity: error

  consistency_check:
    - check: "View elements consistent with kernel"
      severity: error
    - check: "No contradictions between view and kernel"
      severity: error

  traceability_check:
    - check: "View elements reference kernel elements"
      severity: warning
    - check: "No orphaned elements in view"
      severity: warning

  quality_check:
    - check: "View quality metrics acceptable"
      severity: warning
    - check: "Confidence scores reasonable"
      severity: info

title: 18  Views Generation (Projections)
text:
18.7 Chapter Summary

Views are projections of the canonical kernel that emphasize different architectural perspectives
Kernel-first, views-later approach ensures architectural integrity and enables flexible view generation
Product view focuses on user-visible functionality and interfaces accessible to external users
Product view is derived from Goal, Capabilities, and external Interfaces
Product view faces challenges: implicit product intent, capability-to-feature mapping, user interface abstraction
Architecture view is the primary artifact, focusing on structural composition and relationships
Architecture view includes components, interfaces, data, workflows, and their relationships
Architecture view is most accurate and comprehensive representation of system structure
Operations view highlights runtime and operational structure including deployment, dependencies, monitoring
Operations view is derived from deployment configuration and observability signals
Security and trust view focuses on system boundaries, authentication, authorization, and sensitive data
Security view identifies trust boundaries, access controls, and compliance requirements
Views are generated through deterministic projection rules from the kernel
Projection ensures consistency, reproducibility, and validity across all views
Views can be validated against the kernel for quality assurance
Different views serve different audiences and answer different questions about the system

Next Chapter: Chapter 17 introduces Blueprint Pack Output Standard—the structured output format for extracted blueprints. This chapter covers pack directory structure, extraction reports, review-oriented output, diff-friendly representation, and Git review compatibility.

title: 19  Blueprint Pack Output Standard
text:
19.1 Pack as the Output Unit
Blueprint extraction does not end when a kernel has been filled or views have been generated. The extracted result must be emitted in a form that is reviewable, versionable, and operationally usable.
For this reason, this volume defines the Blueprint Pack as the standard output unit of extraction. A blueprint pack is not merely a blueprint file; it is a structured output package that contains the blueprint itself together with the supporting materials required for audit, review, and iterative refinement.
The pack standard has three objectives: preserve the extracted architecture in a canonical form, preserve the evidence and review context that justify it, and make changes easy to inspect through normal version-control workflows. A blueprint pack is therefore the portable, reviewable artifact produced by the extraction pipeline.
The extraction pipeline produces more than one kind of result. It produces a kernel-level architectural representation, one or more projected views, evidence references, extraction diagnostics, and review metadata. These outputs must remain connected.
Why a pack structure matters:
Pack vs. simple blueprint file:

title: 19  Blueprint Pack Output Standard
text:
pack_rationale:
  connection_requirement:
    problem: "Multiple extraction outputs must remain connected"
    without_pack:
      - blueprint: "Stored without evidence context"
        consequence: "Auditability lost"
      - evidence: "Stored without blueprint"
        consequence: "Cannot be used as architectural knowledge"
      - metadata: "Stored in separate locations"
        consequence: "Difficult to correlate"

    with_pack:
      - blueprint: "Packaged with evidence"
        consequence: "Fully auditable"
      - evidence: "Packaged with blueprint"
        consequence: "Usable architectural knowledge"
      - metadata: "Packaged together"
        consequence: "Easy to correlate"

  version_friendliness:
    problem: "Extraction must work with Git-based workflows"
    without_pack:
      - format: "Opaque binary or complex format"
        consequence: "Diff reviews difficult"
      - structure: "Unstructured or inconsistent"
        consequence: "Meaningful changes hard to identify"

    with_pack:
      - format: "YAML/JSON text files"
        consequence: "Git diff works naturally"
      - structure: "Standardized directory layout"
        consequence: "Changes easily located"
      - determinism: "Stable field ordering"
        consequence: "Minimal noise in diffs"

comparison:
  simple_blueprint_file:
    structure: "Single YAML/JSON file"
    contents:
      - "Kernel structure"
      - "View definitions"
    missing:
      - "Evidence references"
      - "Extraction diagnostics"
      - "Review metadata"
      - "Decision history"
    use_cases:
      - "Simple visualization"
      - "Final documentation"
    limitations:
      - "Not auditable"
      - "Not reviewable"
      - "Not debuggable"

  blueprint_pack:
    structure: "Directory with multiple files"
    contents:
      - "Blueprint (kernel + views)"
      - "Evidence bundle"
      - "Extraction report"
      - "Review metadata"
      - "Decision log"
    includes:
      - "Full evidence context"
      - "Extraction quality metrics"
      - "Review decisions"
      - "Change history"
    use_cases:
      - "Architectural analysis"
      - "Design review"
      - "Pattern discovery"
      - "Documentation"
    advantages:
      - "Fully auditable"
      - "Systematically reviewable"
      - "Easily debuggable"
      - "Version-control friendly"

title: 19  Blueprint Pack Output Standard
text:
19.2 Pack Directory Structure
A blueprint pack should use a stable and predictable directory layout. This structure ensures consistency across extraction runs and enables tooling to process packs programmatically.
Canonical pack structure:
blueprint_pack_structure:
  root: "blueprint-pack/"

  directories:
    - path: "/"
      description: "Pack root directory"

    - path: "/blueprint.yaml"
      description: "Canonical kernel-centered blueprint"
      required: true

    - path: "/views/"
      description: "Projected representations derived from kernel"
      required: true

    - path: "/views/architecture.yaml"
      description: "Architecture view (primary output)"
      required: true

    - path: "/views/product.yaml"
      description: "Product view (user-facing)"
      required: false

    - path: "/views/ops.yaml"
      description: "Operations view (operational structure)"
      required: false

    - path: "/views/security.yaml"
      description: "Security/trust view (boundaries and access)"
      required: false

    - path: "/evidence/"
      description: "Evidence indexes and supporting excerpts"
      required: true

    - path: "/evidence/index.yaml"
      description: "Catalog of all evidence objects"
      required: true

    - path: "/evidence/snippets/"
      description: "Artifact fragments for quick inspection"
      required: true

    - path: "/report/"
      description: "Extraction diagnostics and assessment artifacts"
      required: true

    - path: "/report/extraction-report.yaml"
      description: "Quality and uncertainty ledger"
      required: true

    - path: "/review/"
      description: "Human review outcomes and decision history"
      required: true

    - path: "/review/decision-log.yaml"
      description: "Architectural decision records (ADR)"
      required: true

    - path: "/review/status.yaml"
      description: "Current review state and maturity level"
      required: true

    - path: "/metadata/"
      description: "Pack-level metadata"
      required: true

    - path: "/metadata/pack-meta.yaml"
      description: "Source repository, extraction version, timestamps"
      required: true
Directory separation rationale:
separation_rationale:
  blueprint_yaml:
    location: "Root level"
    reason: "Primary output, easily accessible"
    access: "Direct reference in tools"

  views_directory:
    location: "Separate directory"
    reason: "Multiple views, organized together"
    access: "Through index or direct file access"

  evidence_directory:
    location: "Separate directory"
    reason: "Large volume, separate concern"
    access: "Through evidence index"

  report_directory:
    location: "Separate directory"
    reason: "Diagnostic information, separate from blueprint"
    access: "For quality assessment"

  review_directory:
    location: "Separate directory"
    reason: "Review metadata, separate from extraction output"
    access: "For governance and audit"
Example pack file tree:
blueprint-pack/
├── blueprint.yaml
├── views/
│   ├── architecture.yaml
│   ├── product.yaml
│   ├── ops.yaml
│   └── security.yaml
├── evidence/
│   ├── index.yaml
│   └── snippets/
│       ├── evidence-001.txt
│       ├── evidence-002.txt
│       └── ...
├── report/
│   └── extraction-report.yaml
├── review/
│   ├── decision-log.yaml
│   └── status.yaml
└── metadata/
    └── pack-meta.yaml

title: 19  Blueprint Pack Output Standard
text:
19.3 Blueprint YAML Structure
The blueprint.yaml file contains the canonical kernel-centered blueprint. This file represents the primary output of extraction.
Blueprint YAML example:
blueprint:
  version: "1.0"
  extraction_spec: "Blueprint Extraction Spec v0.1"

  system:
    id: "payment-system-v1"
    name: "Payment Processing System"
    repository: "github.com/example/payment-system"
    commit: "abc123def456"
    extracted_at: "2026-03-08T10:30:00Z"
    extractor: "blueprint-extractor v1.2"

  kernel:
    Goal:
      value: "Process payment transactions and manage refunds for e-commerce platforms"
      confidence: 0.70
      status: inferred
      evidence_refs: [e-001, e-002]

    Capabilities:
      - id: cap.create_payment
        name: "Create Payment"
        description: "Create new payment transactions"
        confidence: 0.95
        status: observed
        evidence_refs: [e-003, e-004]

      - id: cap.process_payment
        name: "Process Payment"
        description: "Process payment through payment gateway"
        confidence: 0.90
        status: observed
        evidence_refs: [e-005, e-006]

    Components:
      - id: comp.payment_service
        type: Service
        name: "Payment Service"
        confidence: 0.95
        status: observed
        evidence_refs: [e-010, e-011, e-012]

      - id: comp.payment_worker
        type: Worker
        name: "Payment Worker"
        confidence: 0.92
        status: observed
        evidence_refs: [e-013, e-014, e-015]

    Interfaces:
      - id: int.payment_api
        type: API
        name: "Payment API"
        protocol: HTTP
        confidence: 0.98
        status: observed
        evidence_refs: [e-019, e-020]

      - id: int.payment_events
        type: Event
        name: "Payment Events"
        protocol: Kafka
        confidence: 0.92
        status: observed
        evidence_refs: [e-021, e-022]

    Data:
      - id: data.payments
        type: Table
        name: "Payments"
        storage: PostgreSQL
        confidence: 0.98
        status: observed
        evidence_refs: [e-024, e-025, e-026]

      - id: data.transactions
        type: Table
        name: "Transactions"
        storage: PostgreSQL
        confidence: 0.98
        status: observed
        evidence_refs: [e-027, e-028]

    Workflows:
      - id: wf.payment_processing
        type: DAG
        name: "Payment Processing"
        confidence: 0.98
        status: observed
        evidence_refs: [e-032, e-033]

    Constraints:
      - id: cons.api_authentication
        type: Security
        name: "API Authentication Required"
        value: "All API endpoints require authentication"
        confidence: 0.88
        status: observed
        evidence_refs: [e-036]

  quality_metrics:
    slot_completion: "6/6 (100%)"
    average_confidence: 0.92
    high_confidence_items: "14/16 (87.5%)"
    medium_confidence_items: "2/16 (12.5%)"
    consistency_score: 0.92
    evidence_ratio: 0.95

title: 19  Blueprint Pack Output Standard
text:
19.4 Evidence Bundle Structure
The evidence bundle contains all evidence objects generated during extraction. This bundle forms the audit trail for the blueprint.
Evidence index structure:
evidence_index:
  version: "1.0"
  total_evidence: 42

  by_type:
    code: 15
    config: 12
    schema: 8
    deploy: 4
    doc: 3

  by_strength:
    strong: 18
    moderate: 16
    weak: 8

  by_category:
    entrypoint: 8
    config: 7
    deploy: 6
    structure: 5
    dependency: 4
    docs: 3
    test: 3
    observability: 3
    security: 3

  conflicts: 3

  evidence_objects:
    - id: e-001
      type: code
      category: entrypoint
      strength: strong
      source: api/main.py
      location: lines 1-15
      excerpt: |
        from fastapi import FastAPI
        app = FastAPI()
        ...
      supports:
        - entity: comp.payment_service
        - entity: int.payment_api
      confidence_implication: observed

    - id: e-002
      type: doc
      category: docs
      strength: moderate
      source: README.md
      location: lines 1-10
      excerpt: |
        # Payment Processing System

        This system processes payment transactions
        for e-commerce platforms.
      supports:
        - entity: kernel.Goal
      confidence_implication: inferred

    # ... more evidence objects
Evidence snippets directory:
evidence/snippets/
├── e-001.txt
├── e-002.txt
├── e-003.txt
└── ...
Snippet file format:
# Evidence ID: e-001
# Source: api/main.py
# Location: lines 1-15
# Strength: strong

from fastapi import FastAPI
from pydantic import BaseModel

app = FastAPI()

class PaymentRequest(BaseModel):
    amount: float
    currency: str
    payment_method: str

@app.post("/payments")
def create_payment(request: PaymentRequest):
    return payment_service.create(request)

if __name__ == "__main__":
    import uvicorn
    uvicorn.run(app, host="0.0.0.0", port=8000)

title: 19  Blueprint Pack Output Standard
text:
19.5 Extraction Report
Every blueprint pack must contain an extraction report. The extraction report records what the extraction pipeline found, what it could not determine, and where uncertainty remains. It serves as the operational summary of the extraction run.
Extraction report structure:
extraction_report:
  version: "1.0"
  generated_at: "2026-03-08T10:30:00Z"
  extraction_run_id: "run-20260308-103000"

  summary:
    total_elements_extracted: 42
    elements_with_high_confidence: 32
    elements_with_medium_confidence: 8
    elements_with_low_confidence: 2
    elements_marked_unknown: 0
    overall_quality_score: 0.92

  gaps:
    - id: gap-001
      slot: Goal
      severity: medium
      description: "Goal is inferred from capabilities, not explicitly documented"
      impact: "Product view may be incomplete"
      recommendation: "Validate with product team"
      confidence_impact: "Goal confidence reduced to 0.70"

    - id: gap-002
      slot: Constraints
      severity: low
      description: "Performance constraints partially documented"
      impact: "Operations view may miss some constraints"
      recommendation: "Review SLA documentation"
      confidence_impact: "Constraint confidence reduced to 0.75"

  conflicts:
    - id: conflict-001
      type: documentation_vs_implementation
      severity: low
      description: "README describes separate microservices, code shows monolithic structure"
      evidence:
        - documentation: "README says 'microservices architecture'"
        - implementation: "Single FastAPI application with background workers"
      resolution: pending
      current_handling: "Followed implementation evidence (stronger)"
      recommended_action: "Review with architecture team"

    - id: conflict-002
      type: outdated_configuration
      severity: low
      description: "Docker Compose references old queue configuration"
      evidence:
        - config: "docker-compose.yml references redis:6379/0"
        - code: "Redis connection string uses redis:6379/1"
      resolution: resolved
      resolution_method: "Preferred code implementation (more recent)"
      final_decision: "Used code-based configuration"

  confidence_listing:
    by_slot:
      Goal:
        observed: 0
        inferred: 1
        unknown: 0
        average_confidence: 0.70

      Capabilities:
        observed: 4
        inferred: 0
        unknown: 0
        average_confidence: 0.94

      Components:
        observed: 4
        inferred: 0
        unknown: 0
        average_confidence: 0.91

      Interfaces:
        observed: 2
        inferred: 0
        unknown: 0
        average_confidence: 0.95

      Data:
        observed: 4
        inferred: 0
        unknown: 0
        average_confidence: 0.98

      Workflows:
        observed: 2
        inferred: 0
        unknown: 0
        average_confidence: 0.95

    by_confidence_level:
      observed:
        count: 16
        percentage: 38.1%
        avg_confidence: 0.96

      inferred:
        count: 1
        percentage: 2.4%
        avg_confidence: 0.70

      unknown:
        count: 0
        percentage: 0.0%

  diagnostics:
    extraction_duration: "45 seconds"
    files_processed: 156
    evidence_collected: 42
    signals_detected: 89
    mapping_conflicts: 3
    validation_errors: 0

  recommendations:
    high_priority:
      - "Validate inferred Goal with product team"
      - "Review microservices vs. monolithic architecture decision"

    medium_priority:
      - "Document performance constraints more explicitly"
      - "Add more unit tests to improve signal detection"

    low_priority:
      - "Update Docker Compose to match code configuration"

title: 19  Blueprint Pack Output Standard
text:
19.6 Review-Oriented Output
A blueprint pack is designed to support human review as a normal part of extraction. The output must make it easy for a reviewer to answer questions about what was extracted, what evidence supports it, and where uncertainty remains.
Review directory structure:
review_directory:
  decision_log:
    file: "review/decision-log.yaml"
    purpose: "Record architectural decisions made during review"
    format: "Architectural Decision Records (ADR)"

  status:
    file: "review/status.yaml"
    purpose: "Track current review state and maturity level"
    format: "Status tracking"
Decision log format:
decision_log:
  version: "1.0"
  last_updated: "2026-03-08T14:30:00Z"

  decisions:
    - id: adr-001
      title: "Architecture pattern selection"
      status: accepted
      date: "2026-03-08"
      context: |
        README describes microservices architecture, but code shows
        monolithic application with background workers.

      decision: |
        Treat system as monolithic with service/worker separation
        rather than true microservices. This aligns with implementation
        evidence which is stronger than documentation.

      consequences:
        - "Architecture view shows service/worker separation"
        - "Deployment shows single application with workers"
        - "No cross-service network boundaries identified"

      alternatives_considered:
        - "True microservices (rejected: no evidence)"
        - "Modular monolith (considered: partial evidence)"

      reviewer: "architect@example.com"
      confidence: 0.85

    - id: adr-002
      title: "Goal statement validation"
      status: accepted
      date: "2026-03-08"
      context: |
        Goal is inferred from capabilities rather than explicitly
        documented in repository.

      decision: |
        Accept inferred goal: "Process payment transactions and manage
        refunds for e-commerce platforms". This aligns with capabilities
        and is reasonable, but confidence remains lower due to lack of
        explicit documentation.

      consequences:
        - "Goal marked as inferred with 0.70 confidence"
        - "Product view may be incomplete"
        - "Recommend explicit documentation in future"

      alternatives_considered:
        - "Mark as unknown (rejected: sufficient supporting evidence)"

      reviewer: "product_manager@example.com"
      confidence: 0.70

    - id: adr-003
      title: "Payment worker component validation"
      status: confirmed
      date: "2026-03-08"
      context: |
        Payment worker identified from Celery task definitions,
        queue dependencies, and deployment configuration.

      decision: |
        Confirm component.payment_worker as legitimate worker
        component. Strong evidence supports this interpretation:
        - Celery task definitions (strong)
        - Queue dependency (strong)
        - Worker deployment config (strong)

      consequences:
        - "Component marked as observed with 0.92 confidence"
        - "No changes needed"

      reviewer: "architect@example.com"
      confidence: 0.92
Status file format:
review_status:
  version: "1.0"
  last_updated: "2026-03-08T15:00:00Z"

  blueprint_maturity:
    current_level: usable
    progression:
      - level: draft
        date: "2026-03-08T10:30:00Z"
        notes: "Initial extraction completed"
      - level: usable
        date: "2026-03-08T15:00:00Z"
        notes: "Review completed, 2 ADRs accepted"

    next_level: reference
    requirements_for_next_level:
      - "All high-impact items reviewed"
      - "All unknown fields resolved"
      - "Consistency validation passed"

  review_progress:
    total_review_items: 16
    reviewed: 16
    confirmed: 14
    corrected: 2
    deferred: 0

  review_summary:
    overall_quality: good
    major_issues: 0
    minor_issues: 2
    action_items:
      - "Document goal explicitly in README"
      - "Clarify microservices vs. monolithic in documentation"

title: 19  Blueprint Pack Output Standard
text:
19.7 Diff-Friendly Representation
Because blueprint packs are expected to evolve with repositories over time, their contents must be diff-friendly. This is a critical requirement for operational usability.
Diff-friendly requirements:
diff_friendly_requirements:
  stable_field_ordering:
    requirement: "Fields must appear in consistent order"
    reason: "Prevents spurious diffs from reordering"
    implementation: "YAML preserves insertion order"

  deterministic_serialization:
    requirement: "Same content produces identical output"
    reason: "Prevents cosmetic changes in diffs"
    implementation: "No random elements or timestamps"

  one_logical_object_per_file:
    requirement: "Each file contains one logical object"
    reason: "Changes isolated to relevant files"
    implementation: "Separate files for different concerns"

  minimal_regenerated_metadata:
    requirement: "Avoid regenerated timestamps in output"
    reason: "Prevents noise in every diff"
    implementation: "Use extraction_run_id, not timestamps in content"

  explicit_identifiers:
    requirement: "Every entity has stable identifier"
    reason: "Enables tracking across versions"
    implementation: "Hash-based or sequential IDs"
Diff-friendly example:
before: "Version 1.0"
blueprint:
  kernel:
    Capabilities:
      - id: cap.create_payment
        name: "Create Payment"
        confidence: 0.95
      - id: cap.process_payment
        name: "Process Payment"
        confidence: 0.90

after: "Version 1.1"
blueprint:
  kernel:
    Capabilities:
      - id: cap.create_payment
        name: "Create Payment"
        confidence: 0.95
      - id: cap.process_payment
        name: "Process Payment"
        confidence: 0.90
      - id: cap.send_notification
        name: "Send Notification"
        confidence: 0.88

git_diff:
  added_lines: 4
  removed_lines: 0
  noise: 0
  clarity: "High - only shows actual change"
Diff-unfriendly example:
before: "Version 1.0"
blueprint:
  kernel:
    Capabilities:
      - id: cap.create_payment
        name: "Create Payment"
        confidence: 0.95
        generated_at: "2026-03-08T10:30:00Z"
      - id: cap.process_payment
        name: "Process Payment"
        confidence: 0.90
        generated_at: "2026-03-08T10:30:01Z"

after: "Version 1.1"
blueprint:
  kernel:
    Capabilities:
      - id: cap.create_payment
        name: "Create Payment"
        confidence: 0.95
        generated_at: "2026-03-08T11:00:00Z"
      - id: cap.process_payment
        name: "Process Payment"
        confidence: 0.90
        generated_at: "2026-03-08T11:00:01Z"
      - id: cap.send_notification
        name: "Send Notification"
        confidence: 0.88
        generated_at: "2026-03-08T11:00:02Z"

git_diff:
  added_lines: 12
  removed_lines: 6
  noise: 8
  clarity: "Low - includes timestamp noise"

title: 19  Blueprint Pack Output Standard
text:
19.8 Git Review Compatibility
The blueprint pack standard is intentionally designed to fit ordinary version-control practices. A reviewer should be able to inspect a blueprint update through a normal Git diff and understand what changed.
Git review workflow:
git_review_workflow:
  scenario: "Repository updated, blueprint regenerated"

  git_diff_interpretation:
    added_capability:
      change: "New capability added"
      file: "blueprint.yaml"
      lines: "4 lines added"
      meaning: "New feature detected in code"

    component_boundary_change:
      change: "Component responsibility modified"
      file: "views/architecture.yaml"
      lines: "2 lines modified"
      meaning: "Architectural change in component"

    evidence_addition:
      change: "New evidence collected"
      file: "evidence/index.yaml"
      lines: "3 lines added"
      meaning: "Better support for existing interpretation"

    confidence_increase:
      change: "Confidence score improved"
      file: "blueprint.yaml"
      lines: "1 line modified"
      meaning: "Additional evidence strengthens interpretation"

  review_questions_answerable:
    - question: "What kernel fields changed?"
      answer: "Check blueprint.yaml diff"
      effort: "Trivial"

    - question: "Which entities were added or removed?"
      answer: "Check entity IDs in diff"
      effort: "Trivial"

    - question: "Did confidence increase or decrease?"
      answer: "Check confidence values in diff"
      effort: "Trivial"

    - question: "Were new gaps or conflicts discovered?"
      answer: "Check report/extraction-report.yaml"
      effort: "Easy"

    - question: "Did review status change?"
      answer: "Check review/status.yaml"
      effort: "Easy"
Pull request template:
# Blueprint Update for payment-system

## Summary
Blueprint regenerated after repository update to abc456

## Changes

### Kernel
- Added capability: `cap.send_notification`
  - Evidence: [e-039, e-040]
  - Confidence: 0.88

### Views
- Architecture view: Updated component responsibilities
- Product view: Added send_notification capability

### Evidence
- Added 3 new evidence objects
- Total evidence: 45 (was 42)

### Report
- No new gaps
- 1 new conflict resolved
- Average confidence: 0.93 (was 0.92)

### Review
- Status: usable (unchanged)
- 1 new ADR required

## Review Focus
- Verify send_notification capability is correct
- Review architectural impact of new capability
- Validate evidence quality

## Checklist
- [ ] Kernel changes make sense
- [ ] Evidence supports new elements
- [ ] No spurious diffs
- [ ] Review status appropriate

title: 19  Blueprint Pack Output Standard
text:
19.9 Standard Output as Operational Discipline
The blueprint pack standard completes the extraction pipeline by defining how extracted architecture is materialized. This standard ensures that extraction outputs are operationally usable, auditable, and maintainable at scale.
Operational benefits:
operational_benefits:
  auditability:
    benefit: "Every architectural claim traceable to evidence"
    mechanism: "Evidence bundle with explicit references"
    impact: "Trust in extraction results"

  reviewability:
    benefit: "Human review integrated into workflow"
    mechanism: "Review directory with decision log"
    impact: "Quality improvement over time"

  version_control:
    benefit: "Changes tracked through Git"
    mechanism: "Diff-friendly YAML structure"
    impact: "Evolution management and history"

  scalability:
    benefit: "Large-scale extraction feasible"
    mechanism: "Standardized pack structure"
    impact: "Automated processing of many blueprints"

  interoperability:
    benefit: "Tools can process packs uniformly"
    mechanism: "Canonical file structure"
    impact: "Toolchain development"
Pack usage workflow:
pack_usage_workflow:
  extraction:
    input: "Repository"
    process: "Extraction pipeline"
    output: "Blueprint pack"

  review:
    input: "Blueprint pack"
    process: "Human review"
    output: "Updated pack with ADRs"

  version_control:
    input: "Updated pack"
    process: "Git commit and PR"
    output: "Version history"

  analysis:
    input: "Multiple packs"
    process: "Pattern discovery tools"
    output: "Architectural insights"

title: 19  Blueprint Pack Output Standard
text:
19.10 Chapter Summary

Blueprint Pack is the standard output unit of extraction, containing blueprint plus supporting materials
Pack connects blueprint, evidence, report, and review metadata in single output container
Pack structure includes blueprint.yaml, views/, evidence/, report/, review/, metadata/ directories
blueprint.yaml contains canonical kernel-centered blueprint with six slots
Evidence bundle includes evidence index and snippets for audit trail
Extraction report records gaps, conflicts, confidence listings, and diagnostics
Review directory contains decision log (ADR format) and status tracking
Pack supports human review with structured decision records
Diff-friendly representation enables Git-based version control
Diff-friendly requires stable field ordering, deterministic serialization, minimal noise
Git review compatibility enables easy inspection of architectural changes
Pack standard makes extraction operationally usable, auditable, and maintainable at scale
Pack enables large-scale blueprint mining and architectural intelligence

Next Chapter: Chapter 18 begins Part V by introducing Validation: Schema and Semantics—the quality gate that ensures extracted blueprints are structurally sound and architecturally plausible. This chapter covers DSL validation, reference integrity, kernel completeness, and anomaly detection.

title: 20  Validation: Schema and Semantics
text:
20.1 Why Validation Matters
Blueprint extraction produces structured architectural representations from repository artifacts. Before these representations can be accepted as usable blueprints, they must pass a validation stage.
Validation ensures that the extracted blueprint is not only syntactically correct, but also structurally coherent and architecturally plausible. Two categories of validation are required: schema validation, which verifies structural correctness, and semantic validation, which verifies architectural consistency.
Together, these checks prevent malformed or misleading blueprints from entering blueprint libraries or architecture datasets. Validation acts as the first quality gate between automated extraction and human review.
Validation serves as the critical bridge between automated extraction and architectural understanding. Without validation, extraction pipelines could produce misleading, incomplete, or structurally unsound representations that would degrade the quality of blueprint datasets and mislead architectural analysis.
Validation purposes:
Validation as quality gate:

title: 20  Validation: Schema and Semantics
text:
validation_purposes:
  quality_assurance:
    purpose: "Ensure extraction quality meets standards"
    without_validation:
      - "Malformed blueprints enter datasets"
      - "Inconsistent representations propagate"
      - "Extraction errors compound"
    with_validation:
      - "Low-quality blueprints rejected"
      - "Extraction errors caught early"

  correctness_verification:
    purpose: "Verify architectural claims are valid"
    without_validation:
      - "Unverified architectural claims accepted"
      - "Misinterpretations become canonical"
      - "Analysis based on incorrect information"
    with_validation:
      - "Architectural claims validated"
      - "Misinterpretations flagged"
      - "Analysis based on verified information"

  consistency_enforcement:
    purpose: "Ensure internal consistency of blueprints"
    without_validation:
      - "Inconsistent relationships accepted"
      - "Contradictory information coexists"
      - "Blueprints become unreliable"
    with_validation:
      - "Inconsistencies detected and resolved"
      - "Contradictions flagged"

  anomaly_detection:
    purpose: "Identify potential extraction errors"
    without_validation:

    with_validation:

quality_gate_position:
  position: "First quality gate"

  input: "Extracted blueprint with evidence"
  output: "Validated blueprint or rejection reason"

  consequences:
    - pass: "Blueprint proceeds to review"
