      problem: "Unreliable architectural knowledge"
      impact: "Poor pattern discovery"
Evidence quality metrics:
evidence_quality:
  evidence_strength_distribution:
    strong: 18
    moderate: 16
    weak: 8
    total: 42

  metrics:
    strong_ratio: 18 / 42 = 0.43
    moderate_ratio: 16 / 42 = 0.38
    weak_ratio: 8 / 42 = 0.19

  interpretation:
    good_balance: "Strong evidence dominates, but includes moderate and weak"
    note: "Good mix of explicit and inferred information"

  evidence_coverage:
    average_evidence_per_entity: 2.5
    range: 1-5
    interpretation: "2.5 evidence items per entity is good"

  conflict_handling:
    conflicts_detected: 3
    conflicts_preserved: 3
    resolution_rate: 0.0
    interpretation: "Conflicts preserved for review, not auto-resolved"
crumbs: Part V — Validation, Review, and Quality Gates

title: 22  Extraction Quality Metrics
text:
22.5 Extraction Maturity Levels
To summarize extraction quality in an operationally meaningful way, this framework defines Extraction Maturity Levels (EML). These levels represent increasing levels of extraction reliability and completeness.
EML definitions:
extraction_maturity_levels:
  EML_0_no_structured_extraction:
    level: 0
    name: "No Structured Extraction"
    description: "No structured blueprint representation exists"
    characteristics:
      - "No extraction pipeline"
      - "No systematic architecture documentation"
      - "Only informal documentation exists"
    typical_state:
      - "Repository exists but no extraction"
      - "Documentation may be incomplete or absent"
    confidence: N/A
    evidence: N/A
    action_needed: "Implement extraction pipeline"

  EML_1_partial_signals_detected:
    level: 1
    name: "Partial Signals Detected"
    description: "Repository signals have been detected but not yet organized"
    characteristics:
      - "Signal detection implemented"
      - "Signals identified but not aggregated"
      - "No structured blueprint output"
    typical_state:
      - "Signal inventory exists"
      - "Evidence collection may be partial"
    confidence: "Variable"
    evidence: "Partial"
    action_needed: "Implement evidence aggregation and mapping"

  EML_2_kernel_partially_reconstructed:
    level: 2
    name: "Kernel Partially Reconstructed"
    description: "Parts of the kernel have been reconstructed"
    characteristics:
      - "Some kernel slots populated"
      - "Partial evidence support"
      - "Draft blueprint exists but incomplete"
    typical_state:
      - "Kernel draft with 2-4 slots populated"
      - "Some confidence in populated slots"
    confidence: "Variable (0.3-0.7)"
    evidence: "Partial"
    action_needed: "Complete remaining kernel slots and strengthen evidence"

  EML_3_evidence_backed_blueprint_draft:
    level: 3
    name: "Evidence-Backed Blueprint Draft"
    description: "Blueprint draft produced with traceable evidence and validation checks"
    characteristics:
      - "All kernel slots populated"
      - "Strong evidence support"
      - "Validation checks passed"
      - "Draft blueprint is coherent"
    typical_state:
      - "Complete kernel with all slots"
      - "Strong confidence (>0.7)"
      - "Validation passed"
    confidence: "High (0.7-0.9)"
    evidence: "Strong"
    action_needed: "Human review to validate and approve"

  EML_4_reviewed_and_validated_blueprint:
    level: 4
    name: "Reviewed and Validated Blueprint"
    description: "Blueprint has passed human review and is considered reliable"
    characteristics:
      - "Complete kernel with all slots"
      - "Very strong evidence support"
      - "Validation passed"
      - "Human review completed"
      - "ADR documentation complete"
    typical_state:
      - "Complete kernel with all slots"
      - "Very strong confidence (>0.85)"
      - "All validation passed"
      - "Review completed"
      - "Maturity: reference"
    confidence: "Very high (>0.85)"
    evidence: "Very strong"
    action_needed: "Ready for use in datasets and libraries"
EML assessment example:
eml_assessment:
  blueprint_id: "payment-system-v1"

  level_assessment:
    slot_population:
      Goal: POPULATED
      Capabilities: POPULATED
      Components: POPULATED
      Interfaces: POPULATED
      Data: POPULATED
      Workflows: POPULATED

    evidence_assessment:
      evidence_ratio: 1.0
      evidence_strength: "Strong dominance"
      confidence_distribution:
        high_confidence: 14 (87.5%)
        medium_confidence: 2 (12.5%)
        low_confidence: 0 (0%)

    validation_assessment:
      dsl_validation: PASSED
      reference_integrity: PASSED
      kernel_completeness: PASSED
      anomaly_detection: FLAGGED (3 anomalies, all low severity)

    review_assessment:
      status: COMPLETED
      maturity: usable
      adr_count: 2
      unresolved_issues: 0

    confidence_assessment:
      average_confidence: 0.94
      minimum_confidence: 0.70
      confidence_variance: "Low (0.25)"

  eml_classification:
    level: EML_4
    name: "Reviewed and Validated Blueprint"
    confidence: 0.94

  justification:
    - "All kernel slots fully populated ✓"
    - "Strong evidence support (evidence_ratio = 1.0) ✓"
    - "Validation passed all checks ✓"
    - "Human review completed ✓"
    - "Confidence scores are high and consistent ✓"
    - "Only minor anomalies present (all low severity) ✓"

  next_steps:
    - "Promote to reference maturity"
    - "Include in pattern libraries"
    - "Use as canonical example"
EML progression example:
eml_progression:
  initial_state:
    level: EML_0
    description: "No structured extraction"
    action: "Implement extraction pipeline"

  after_signal_detection:
    level: EML_1
    description: "Signals detected"
    action: "Implement evidence aggregation and mapping"

  after_partial_kernel:
    level: EML_2
    description: "Kernel partially reconstructed"
    gaps:
      - "Goal not populated"
      - "Capabilities partially populated"
      - "Evidence incomplete"
    action: "Complete kernel extraction"

  after_extraction:
    level: EML_3
    description: "Evidence-backed blueprint draft"
    strengths:
      - "All slots populated"
      - "Evidence ratio: 1.0"
      - "Validation passed"
    weaknesses:
      - "Some low-confidence elements"
      - "Anomalies require review"
    action: "Conduct human review"

  after_review:
    level: EML_4
    description: "Reviewed and validated blueprint"
    strengths:
      - "All high-confidence elements"
      - "ADR documentation complete"
      - "Validation passed"
    action: "Blueprint ready for production use"
crumbs: Part V — Validation, Review, and Quality Gates

title: 22  Extraction Quality Metrics
text:
22.6 Metrics as Operational Feedback
Extraction quality metrics serve as feedback mechanisms for the blueprint mining process. Coverage highlights missing architectural reconstruction. Correctness audits identify mapping errors. Consistency checks reveal structural anomalies. Evidence ratios measure traceability. Extraction maturity levels summarize overall reliability.
Metrics as feedback mechanisms:
metrics_as_feedback:
  coverage_feedback:
    metric: "Kernel coverage"
    low_coverage: "< 0.7"
    interpretation: "Significant gaps in architectural reconstruction"
    actions:
      - "Review extraction patterns"
      - "Add missing signal detection rules"
      - "Improve evidence collection"
    target: "Increase coverage to > 0.7"

  correctness_feedback:
    metric: "Audit correctness"
    low_correctness: "< 0.7"
    interpretation: "Significant extraction errors present"
    actions:
      - "Review error patterns"
      - "Adjust confidence calculations"
      - "Refine mapping rules"
    target: "Increase correctness to > 0.8"

  consistency_feedback:
    metric: "Consistency score"
    low_consistency: "< 0.7"
    interpretation: "Internal inconsistencies present"
    actions:
      - "Review consistency checks"
      - "Fix relationship errors"
      - "Validate triangular consistency"
    target: "Increase consistency to > 0.85"

  evidence_feedback:
    metric: "Evidence ratio"
    low_evidence_ratio: "< 0.7"
    interpretation: "Low auditability"
    actions:
      - "Review evidence detection"
      - "Improve evidence extraction"
      - "Enhance evidence aggregation"
    target: "Increase evidence ratio to > 0.8"

  maturity_feedback:
    metric: "EML level"
    low_maturity: "< EML_3"
    interpretation: "Insufficient reliability for production use"
    actions:
      - "Address low-coverage slots"
      - "Increase evidence strength"
      - "Pass validation checks"
      - "Conduct review"
    target: "Achieve EML_3 or higher"
Quality improvement iteration:
quality_improvement:
  cycle:
    iteration_1:
      state: "Initial extraction"
      metrics:
        coverage: 0.6
        correctness: 0.7
        consistency: 0.8
        evidence_ratio: 0.75
        eml_level: EML_2
      improvements:
        - "Add interface detection rules"
        - "Fix boundary inference"
        - "Increase evidence aggregation"

    iteration_2:
      state: "Refined extraction"
      metrics:
        coverage: 0.9
        correctness: 0.85
        consistency: 0.9
        evidence_ratio: 0.92
        eml_level: EML_3
      improvements:
        - "Improved evidence collection"
        - "Fixed relationship errors"
        - "Completed remaining slots"

    iteration_3:
      state: "Production-ready"
      metrics:
        coverage: 1.0
        correctness: 0.95
        consistency: 0.95
        evidence_ratio: 1.0
        eml_level: EML_4
      improvements:
        - "Complete evidence support"
        - "High confidence maintained"
        - "Ready for production use"
crumbs: Part V — Validation, Review, and Quality Gates

title: 22  Extraction Quality Metrics
text:
22.7 Dataset-Level Quality Metrics
Confidence annotations also support dataset-level quality analysis. Metrics derived from confidence values provide insights into overall extraction quality and identify areas where extraction methods require improvement.
Dataset metrics example:
dataset_quality_metrics:
  dataset_size:
    total_blueprints: 150
    total_entities: 6240
    average_entities_per_blueprint: 41.6

  confidence_distribution:
    observed_elements:
      count: 3234
      percentage: 51.8%
      average_confidence: 0.87

    inferred_elements:
      count: 2120
      percentage: 34.0%
      average_confidence: 0.68

    unknown_elements:
      count: 886
      percentage: 14.2%
      average_confidence: 0.12

  quality_trends:
    monthly:
      - month: "2026-01"
        observed_percentage: 42.2%
        average_confidence: 0.62
        eml_average: 2.3

      - month: "2026-02"
        observed_percentage: 48.5%
        average_confidence: 0.65
        eml_average: 2.7

      - month: "2026-03"
        observed_percentage: 51.8%
        average_confidence: 0.70
        eml_average: 3.0

    interpretation: >
      Confidence has improved over the last three months.
      This suggests that extraction methodology improvements
      (better signal detection, improved evidence collection) are
      effectively increasing extraction accuracy and confidence.

  by_repository_type:
    api_services:
      observed: 55%
      inferred: 30%
      unknown: 15%
      avg_confidence: 0.82

    data_pipelines:
      observed: 48%
      inferred: 38%
      unknown: 14%
      avg_confidence: 0.78

    cli_tools:
      observed: 65%
      inferred: 20%
      unknown: 15%
      avg_confidence: 0.88

  recommendations:
    improve_low_confidence_extraction:
      areas:
        - "Goal inference (currently 0.45 avg confidence)"
        - "Complex boundary inference (currently 0.55 avg confidence)"
      actions:
        - "Improve pattern recognition for boundaries"
        - "Enhance documentation extraction"
        - "Collect more explicit goal statements"

    increase_observed_percentage:
      target: "Increase from 51.8% to 60%"
      methods:
        - "Better signal detection rules"
        - "Improve evidence aggregation"
        - "Refine confidence calculation"
Dataset quality targets:
dataset_quality_targets:
  production_dataset_target:
    observed_percentage: "> 60%"
    average_confidence: "> 0.75"
    evidence_ratio: "> 0.85"
    eml_distribution:
      - EML_3: 80%
      - EML_4: 20%

  high_quality_dataset_target:
    observed_percentage: "> 70%"
    average_confidence: "> 0.85"
    evidence_ratio: "> 0.95"
    eml_distribution:
      - EML_3: 40%
      - EML_4: 60%

  acceptable_dataset_target:
    observed_percentage: "> 50%"
    average_confidence: "> 0.65"
    evidence_ratio: "> 0.75"
    eml_distribution:
      - EML_2: 30%
      - EML_3: 60%
      - EML_4: 10%"
crumbs: Part V — Validation, Review, and Quality Gates

title: 22  Extraction Quality Metrics
text:
22.8 Chapter Summary

Quality metrics enable systematic evaluation of extraction results
Coverage measures how much of system architecture has been reconstructed, typically evaluated at kernel slot level
Correctness measures extraction accuracy through sampling-based audits; relies on human verification
Consistency measures internal coherence through triangular consistency checks and relationship validations
Evidence ratio measures the proportion of elements with traceable evidence; indicates auditability
Extraction Maturity Levels (EML 0-4) provide meaningful classification: No Structured Extraction, Partial Signals Detected, Kernel Partially Reconstructed, Evidence-Backed Blueprint Draft, Reviewed and Validated Blueprint
EML0: No structured extraction, EML1: Signals detected, EML2: Kernel partially reconstructed, EML3: Evidence-backed draft, EML4: Reviewed and validated
Metrics provide operational feedback for pipeline improvement and dataset quality monitoring
Coverage feedback highlights gaps; correctness feedback identifies mapping errors; consistency feedback reveals structural anomalies; evidence ratio feedback measures traceability
Dataset-level metrics provide insights into extraction quality trends, repository type differences, and overall dataset maturity
Quality targets define benchmarks for production, high-quality, and acceptable blueprint datasets
Together, these metrics transform extraction into a measurable engineering discipline enabling continuous improvement

Next Chapter: Chapter 21 begins Part VI by introducing Mining Pipeline Operations—the framework for operating blueprint extraction at scale. This chapter covers repository discovery and filtering, cheap pass and deep pass strategies for cost control, and incremental extraction for version-aware blueprint evolution.
crumbs: Part V — Validation, Review, and Quality Gates

title: 23  Mining Pipeline Operations
text:
23.1 Repository Discovery and Filtering
The preceding chapters describe how to extract a blueprint from a single repository. In practice, blueprint extraction often operates at scale—across hundreds or thousands of repositories. Scaling extraction requires operational infrastructure: repository discovery and filtering, cost control strategies, incremental extraction, and workflow orchestration.
This chapter defines the mining pipeline operations framework for extracting blueprints at scale. The framework balances thoroughness with efficiency, ensuring that extraction can be applied to large repository collections without excessive cost or time.
Blueprint extraction operates on repositories, but not all repositories are suitable targets. The first operational step is therefore repository discovery and filtering to identify which repositories should be processed.
Repository discovery sources:
Repository filtering criteria:
Filtering pipeline:
crumbs: Part VI — Operating Blueprint Mining at Scale

title: 23  Mining Pipeline Operations
text:
repository_discovery:
  github_search:
    source: "GitHub search API"
    queries:
      - "language:python stars:>100"
      - "topic:microservices"
      - "archived:false"
    metadata:
      - stars
      - forks
      - language
      - topics
      - updated_at
    filtering:
      min_stars: 50
      min_forks: 10
      languages: [python, go, java, javascript]
      last_updated: "< 1 year ago"

  organization_inventories:
    source: "Organization repository listings"
    access: "GitHub organization API, GitLab group API"
    metadata:
      - repository name
      - language
      - visibility (public/private)
      - last commit date
    filtering:
      exclude_templates: true
      exclude_experimental: true
      include_active_repos_only: true

  code_hosting_platforms:
    sources:
      - GitHub
      - GitLab
      - Bitbucket
      - Azure DevOps
      - Gitea
    unified_access: "Platform abstraction layer"

  package_registries:
    sources:
      - npm (JavaScript)
      - PyPI (Python)
      - crates.io (Rust)
      - Maven Central (Java)
      - Go Modules
    metadata:
      - package name
      - repository URL
      - download count
      - last published date
    filtering:
      min_downloads: 1000
      last_published: "< 2 years ago"

repository_filtering:
  quality_filters:
    min_activity:
      criteria: "Repository must be actively maintained"
      metric: "Last commit date"
      threshold: "< 18 months ago"
      rationale: "Stale repositories may have outdated architecture"

    min_adoption:
      criteria: "Repository must have some adoption"
      metric: "Stars or forks"
      threshold: "stars >= 50 OR forks >= 10"
      rationale: "No adoption may indicate experimental or abandoned projects"

    language_filters:
      supported_languages:
        - python
        - go
        - java
        - javascript
        - typescript
        - rust
        - csharp
      rationale: "Extraction patterns defined for these languages"
      unsupported_handling: "Skip repository"

  type_filters:
    exclude_templates:
      pattern: "repository name contains 'template' or 'starter'"
      rationale: "Templates are not real systems"

    exclude_examples:
      pattern: "repository name contains 'example' or 'demo'"
      rationale: "Examples are not production systems"

    exclude_tests:
      pattern: "repository name contains 'test' or 'testing'"
      rationale: "Test repositories are not representative"

    exclude_documentation:
      pattern: "repository with only documentation files"
      rationale: "No code to extract architecture from"

  size_filters:
    min_size:
      criteria: "Repository must have minimum code complexity"
      metric: "Number of non-vendor files"
      threshold: ">= 10 files"
      rationale: "Too small to have meaningful architecture"

    max_size:
      criteria: "Repository must not be too large"
      metric: "Number of files"
      threshold: "< 10,000 files"
      rationale: "Too large may indicate monorepo or vendor code"

  license_filters:
    allow_licenses:
      - MIT
      - Apache-2.0
      - BSD-3-Clause
      - GPL-3.0
      - LGPL-3.0
    block_licenses:
      - "Proprietary (no license)"
      - "Custom license requiring attribution"
    rationale: "Legal compliance for analysis"

  dependency_filters:
    require_build_files:
      required_files:
        - "requirements.txt"
        - "package.json"
        - "go.mod"
        - "pom.xml"
        - "build.gradle"
      rationale: "Build files contain dependency information"
      missing_handling: "May have weak dependency detection"

filtering_pipeline:
  step_1_discovery:
    action: "Query repository sources"
    input: "Discovery queries and criteria"
    output: "Initial repository list (unfiltered)"
    volume: "10,000+ repositories"

  step_2_language_filter:
    action: "Filter by supported languages"
    input: "Initial repository list"
    output: "Language-filtered list"
    retention_rate: "40-60% (depends on language distribution)"

  step_3_quality_filter:
    action: "Filter by activity and adoption"
    input: "Language-filtered list"
    output: "Quality-filtered list"
    retention_rate: "30-50%"

  step_4_type_filter:
    action: "Exclude templates, examples, tests"
    input: "Quality-filtered list"
    output: "Type-filtered list"
    retention_rate: "80-90%"

  step_5_size_filter:
    action: "Filter by repository size"
    input: "Type-filtered list"
    output: "Size-filtered list"
    retention_rate: "85-95%"

  step_6_license_filter:
    action: "Filter by license compatibility"
    input: "Size-filtered list"
    output: "License-filtered list"
    retention_rate: "95-99%"

  step_7_dependency_filter:
    action: "Require build files"
    input: "License-filtered list"
    output: "Filtered repository list"
    retention_rate: "70-80%"

  overall_retention:
    from_discovery_to_final: "5-20%"
    example:
      discovered: 10,000
      final: 500-2000
crumbs: Part VI — Operating Blueprint Mining at Scale

title: 23  Mining Pipeline Operations
text:
23.2 Cheap Pass and Deep Pass
Extraction cost varies dramatically based on repository characteristics. A small, well-structured API service may be extracted in seconds, while a complex monorepo may require hours. To control costs, the mining pipeline uses a two-pass strategy: cheap pass (lightweight analysis) followed by deep pass (full extraction).
Cheap pass definition:
cheap_pass:
  objective: "Lightweight, fast analysis to estimate extraction value"
  duration: "Seconds to minutes"
  resource_usage: "Low"

  analysis_scope:
    signal_detection:
      - directory_structure_analysis
      - entrypoint_identification
      - dependency_inference_from_build_files
      - basic_config_parsing

    quality_assessment:
      - architecture_complexity_estimate
      - extraction_feasibility_score
      - expected_coverage_estimate

    resource_estimation:
      - expected_extraction_time
      - expected_compute_resources
      - expected_storage_requirements

  cheap_pass_outputs:
    repository_profile:
      repo_id: "owner/repo"
      language: "python"
      size_estimate: "medium"
      complexity_estimate: "moderate"

    extraction_feasibility:
      feasibility_score: 0.8
      factors:
        - has_clear_structure: true
        - has_build_files: true
        - has_entrypoints: true
        - has_observability: false

    resource_estimate:
      expected_time: "5-10 minutes"
      expected_cost: "low"
      confidence: 0.7

  pass_or_fail:
    pass:
      condition: "feasibility_score >= 0.6"
      action: "Proceed to deep pass"
      criteria:
        - "Sufficient architectural structure"
        - "Reasonable extraction cost"
        - "Expected good coverage"

    fail:
      condition: "feasibility_score < 0.6"
      action: "Skip repository"
      criteria:
        - "Poor architectural structure"
        - "High extraction cost"
        - "Expected poor coverage"
        - "Template or test repository"

  cheap_pass_examples:
    high_feasibility:
      repo: "payment-service"
      structure:
        - clear separation: services/workers
        - entrypoints: API routes, CLI
        - dependencies: requirements.txt
      assessment:
        feasibility_score: 0.9
        action: "PASS - proceed to deep pass"

    medium_feasibility:
      repo: "data-pipeline"
      structure:
        - some separation: unclear
        - entrypoints: only one
        - dependencies: requirements.txt
      assessment:
        feasibility_score: 0.7
        action: "PASS - proceed to deep pass with caution"

    low_feasibility:
      repo: "example-project"
      structure:
        - flat structure
        - no clear entrypoints
        - no build files
      assessment:
        feasibility_score: 0.3
        action: "FAIL - skip repository"
Deep pass definition:
deep_pass:
  objective: "Full blueprint extraction with comprehensive analysis"
  duration: "Minutes to hours"
  resource_usage: "Moderate to high"

  analysis_scope:
    signal_detection:
      - complete_directory_structure_analysis
      - all_entrypoint_identification
      - comprehensive_dependency_extraction
      - detailed_config_parsing
      - documentation_extraction
      - test_structure_analysis
      - observability_signal_extraction
      - security_signal_extraction

    evidence_collection:
      - all_signal_types
      - comprehensive_evidence_aggregation
      - evidence_conflict_preservation

    ontology_mapping:
      - component_discovery
      - interface_extraction
      - data_model_analysis
      - workflow_identification
      - boundary_inference

    kernel_construction:
      - all_slot_filling
      - triangular_consistency_checks
      - confidence_calculation

    view_generation:
      - product_view_projection
      - architecture_view_projection
      - operations_view_projection
      - security_view_projection

    validation:
      - dsl_validation
      - reference_integrity_checks
      - kernel_completeness_validation
      - anomaly_detection

  deep_pass_outputs:
    blueprint_pack:
      blueprint_id: "payment-system-v1"
      kernel: "Complete"
      views: "Generated"
      evidence: "Comprehensive"
      report: "Detailed"

    quality_metrics:
      coverage: 0.95
      correctness: 0.92
      consistency: 0.94
      evidence_ratio: 0.98
      eml_level: "EML_3"

    extraction_report:
      extraction_time: "8 minutes"
      resource_usage: "moderate"
      warnings: 2
      anomalies: 3

  deep_pass_completion:
    success:
      condition: "All validation checks pass OR only minor issues"
      output: "Valid blueprint pack"
      action: "Proceed to review"

    failure:
      condition: "Critical validation failures"
      output: "Extraction report with failures"
      action: "Reject repository or retry with adjustments"
Two-pass pipeline:
two_pass_pipeline:
  phase_1_queue_processing:
    action: "Process repository queue"
    input: "Filtered repository list"
    parallel_processing: "Multiple cheap passes concurrently"
    throughput: "50-100 repositories/hour"

  phase_2_cheap_pass:
    action: "Run cheap pass on each repository"
    input: "Repository metadata"
    output:
      - pass_feasible: "Proceed to deep pass"
      - fail_unfeasible: "Skip repository"
    pass_rate: "40-60%"

  phase_3_deep_pass:
    action: "Run deep pass on feasible repositories"
    input: "Feasible repositories"
    output: "Blueprint packs or extraction failures"
    success_rate: "80-95%"

  phase_4_quality_assessment:
    action: "Evaluate extraction quality"
    input: "Blueprint packs"
    output: "Quality metrics and EML classification"
    quality_distribution:
      - EML_4: 20-30%
      - EML_3: 50-60%
      - EML_2: 10-20%
      - EML_1: 5-10%

  phase_5_dataset_integration:
    action: "Integrate blueprints into dataset"
    input: "Quality-validated blueprints"
    output: "Updated blueprint dataset"

  efficiency_gains:
    cost_reduction: "60-80% reduction in compute cost"
    time_savings: "50-70% reduction in processing time"
    quality_improvement: "Focus effort on high-value repositories"
crumbs: Part VI — Operating Blueprint Mining at Scale

title: 23  Mining Pipeline Operations
text:
23.3 Incremental Extraction
Repositories evolve over time through commits, merges, and refactoring. To keep blueprints current without repeatedly running full extraction, the mining pipeline supports incremental extraction—updating existing blueprints based on repository changes.
Incremental extraction strategy:
incremental_extraction:
  principle: "Update only what has changed"
  benefit: "Reduce extraction cost and time"
  challenge: "Determine which architectural elements are affected by code changes"

  change_detection:
    commit_analysis:
      action: "Analyze repository changes since last extraction"
      metadata:
        - commit_hash
        - commit_date
        - files_changed
        - lines_added
        - lines_deleted

    change_categorization:
      structural_changes:
        patterns:
          - "new service directory created"
          - "service directory deleted"
          - "component merged or split"
        impact: "Component boundaries may change"
        action: "Re-run component discovery"

      interface_changes:
        patterns:
          - "API route added or modified"
          - "event topic added or modified"
          - "CLI command added or modified"
        impact: "Interfaces may change"
        action: "Re-run interface extraction"

      data_changes:
        patterns:
          - "schema migration added"
          - "ORM model modified"
          - "data directory restructured"
        impact: "Data model may change"
        action: "Re-run data model extraction"

      workflow_changes:
        patterns:
          - "DAG definition modified"
          - "pipeline configuration changed"
          - "scheduler configuration updated"
        impact: "Workflows may change"
        action: "Re-run workflow extraction"

      minor_changes:
        patterns:
          - "business logic modified"
          - "test files added"
          - "documentation updated"
        impact: "Minimal architectural impact"
        action: "No re-extraction needed"

  change_impact_analysis:
    example_1:
      commit: "Add refund service"
      files_changed:
        - "services/refund_service/ (new directory)"
        - "services/refund_service/api.py (new file)"
        - "requirements.txt (modified)"
      impact_analysis:
        - structural_change: "New component added"
        - interface_change: "New API interface"
        - workflow_change: "May affect payment workflow"
      extraction_updates:
        - re_run: "component_discovery"
        - re_run: "interface_extraction"
        - re_run: "workflow_extraction"
        - re_run: "kernel_construction"

    example_2:
      commit: "Fix payment calculation bug"
      files_changed:
        - "services/payment_service/calc.py (modified)"
        - "tests/test_calc.py (modified)"
      impact_analysis:
        - structural_change: "None"
        - interface_change: "None"
        - data_change: "None"
        - workflow_change: "None"
      extraction_updates:
        - re_run: "none"
        - action: "Skip extraction, no architectural change"

    example_3:
      commit: "Refactor data access layer"
      files_changed:
        - "services/payment_service/db.py (modified)"
        - "migrations/002_refactor_payments.sql (new file)"
        - "models/payment.py (deleted)"
        - "models/refactored_payment.py (new file)"
      impact_analysis:
        - structural_change: "None"
        - interface_change: "None"
        - data_change: "Schema and model changed"
        - workflow_change: "None"
      extraction_updates:
        - re_run: "data_model_extraction"
        - re_run: "kernel_construction"
Incremental extraction workflow:
incremental_workflow:
  step_1_version_tracking:
    action: "Track extraction version"
    storage: "Blueprint pack metadata"
    metadata:
      - last_extraction_commit
      - extraction_date
      - extraction_version

  step_2_change_detection:
    action: "Detect repository changes"
    input: "Last extraction commit"
    output: "Changed files and patterns"
    comparison:
      - "git diff last_extraction_commit HEAD"
      - "Pattern matching on changed files"

  step_3_impact_analysis:
    action: "Analyze change impact"
    input: "Changed files and patterns"
    output: "Affected architectural elements"
    analysis:
      - "Map changes to architectural layers"
      - "Determine re-extraction scope"

  step_4_selective_extraction:
    action: "Re-run affected extraction stages"
    input: "Affected architectural elements"
    output: "Updated blueprint elements"
    strategy:
      - "Only re-run affected stages"
      - "Reuse unchanged elements"

  step_5_blueprint_update:
    action: "Update blueprint pack"
    input: "Updated blueprint elements"
    output: "Incrementally updated blueprint"
    update:
      - "Replace changed elements"
      - "Preserve unchanged elements"
      - "Update metadata"

  step_6_validation:
    action: "Validate updated blueprint"
    input: "Updated blueprint"
    output: "Validation results"
    checks:
      - "DSL validation"
      - "Reference integrity"
      - "Kernel completeness"

  efficiency_gains:
    time_reduction: "70-90% faster than full extraction"
    cost_reduction: "80-95% less compute"
    accuracy: "Maintains blueprint currency"
Incremental extraction challenges:
incremental_challenges:
  cross_slot_dependencies:
    problem: "Changes to one slot may affect other slots"
    example: "Component change may affect interfaces and workflows"
    solution:
      - "Analyze dependency graph"
      - "Cascade re-extraction to dependent slots"
      - "Run triangular consistency checks"

  boundary_inference:
    problem: "Structural changes may invalidate boundary inferences"
    example: "Component merge may affect multiple components"
    solution:
      - "Re-run boundary inference on structural changes"
      - "Verify ownership relationships"
      - "Check for orphaned entities"

  confidence_recalculation:
    problem: "Changes may affect confidence calculations"
    example: "New evidence may increase confidence"
    solution:
      - "Recalculate confidence for affected elements"
      - "Propagate confidence changes through dependencies"
      - "Update EML classification"

  version_conflicts:
    problem: "Concurrent changes may cause conflicts"
    example: "Multiple commits affecting same component"
    solution:
      - "Use latest repository state"
      - "Merge conflicting changes"
      - "Re-run full extraction if conflicts severe"
crumbs: Part VI — Operating Blueprint Mining at Scale

title: 23  Mining Pipeline Operations
text:
23.4 Pipeline Orchestration
Operating extraction at scale requires orchestration infrastructure to manage queue processing, resource allocation, failure handling, and output management. The pipeline must be robust, scalable, and observable.
Pipeline architecture:
pipeline_architecture:
  components:
    repository_queue:
      purpose: "Manage repository processing queue"
      implementation: "Message queue (RabbitMQ, Kafka, SQS)"
      features:
        - "Priority-based processing"
        - "Retry on failure"
        - "Dead-letter queue for failed repos"

    coordinator:
      purpose: "Orchestrate extraction workflow"
      implementation: "Workflow engine (Airflow, Argo, Prefect)"
      responsibilities:
        - "Schedule cheap passes"
        - "Trigger deep passes"
        - "Manage resource allocation"
        - "Handle failures and retries"

    worker_pool:
      purpose: "Execute extraction tasks"
      implementation: "Containerized workers (Docker, Kubernetes)"
      scaling:
        - "Auto-scaling based on queue size"
        - "Resource quotas per worker"
        - "Timeout handling"

    storage:
      purpose: "Store extraction outputs"
      implementation: "Object storage (S3, GCS, Azure Blob)"
      structure:
        - "Blueprint packs"
        - "Extraction reports"
        - "Quality metrics"
        - "Logs and traces"

    monitoring:
      purpose: "Monitor pipeline health"
      implementation: "Monitoring stack (Prometheus, Grafana)"
      metrics:
        - "Queue size and wait time"
        - "Processing throughput"
        - "Success/failure rates"
        - "Resource utilization"
        - "Extraction quality trends"
Pipeline workflow:
pipeline_workflow:
  stage_1_ingestion:
    action: "Ingest repositories into queue"
    input: "Filtered repository list"
    output: "Queued repositories"
    processing:
      - "Assign priority"
      - "Add to queue"
      - "Start coordinator workflow"

  stage_2_cheap_pass:
    action: "Execute cheap pass"
    input: "Queued repository"
    output: "Feasibility assessment"
    processing:
      - "Allocate worker"
      - "Run cheap pass analysis"
      - "Generate feasibility score"
      - "Update queue status"

  stage_3_decision:
    action: "Decide on deep pass"
    input: "Feasibility assessment"
    output: "Decision (pass/fail)"
    processing:
      - "If score >= 0.6: queue for deep pass"
      - "If score < 0.6: mark as skipped"
      - "Update queue status"

  stage_4_deep_pass:
    action: "Execute deep pass"
    input: "Feasible repository"
    output: "Blueprint pack or failure"
    processing:
      - "Allocate worker with higher resources"
      - "Run full extraction pipeline"
      - "Generate blueprint pack"
      - "Store outputs"

  stage_5_validation:
    action: "Validate blueprint"
    input: "Blueprint pack"
    output: "Validation results"
    processing:
      - "Run validation checks"
      - "Calculate quality metrics"
      - "Classify EML level"
      - "Generate extraction report"

  stage_6_integration:
    action: "Integrate into dataset"
    input: "Validated blueprint"
    output: "Updated dataset"
    processing:
      - "Store blueprint pack"
      - "Index for search"
      - "Update quality metrics"
      - "Trigger downstream processing"

  stage_7_failure_handling:
    action: "Handle failures"
    input: "Failure event"
    output: "Retry or skip"
    processing:
      - "Log failure details"
      - "Determine retry strategy"
      - "If retryable: queue for retry"
      - "If not retryable: send to dead-letter queue"
Resource management:
resource_management:
  worker_resources:
    cheap_pass_worker:
      cpu: "1 core"
      memory: "2 GB"
      storage: "10 GB"
      timeout: "5 minutes"

    deep_pass_worker:
      cpu: "4 cores"
      memory: "8 GB"
      storage: "50 GB"
      timeout: "60 minutes"

  scaling_policies:
    scale_up:
      trigger: "Queue size > 100"
      action: "Increase worker pool size"
      max_workers: 50

    scale_down:
      trigger: "Queue size < 10"
      action: "Decrease worker pool size"
      min_workers: 5

  cost_optimization:
    spot_instances:
      use_spot: true
      discount: "60-80%"
      handling: "Graceful termination and checkpointing"

    resource_quotas:
      daily_quota:
        cpu_hours: 1000
        storage_gb: 1000
      action: "Throttle when quota exceeded"

    batch_processing:
      strategy: "Process repositories in batches"
      batch_size: 100
      benefit: "Better resource utilization"
Monitoring and observability:
monitoring:
  pipeline_metrics:
    throughput:
      metric: "Repositories processed per hour"
      target: "50-100/hour"
      alert: "< 30/hour for > 1 hour"

    success_rate:
      metric: "Successful extractions / total attempts"
      target: "> 80%"
      alert: "< 70% for > 1 hour"

    queue_wait_time:
      metric: "Time from queue to processing"
      target: "< 1 hour"
      alert: "> 2 hours for > 100 repos"

    resource_utilization:
      metric: "CPU and memory utilization"
      target: "60-80%"
      alert: "> 90% for > 15 minutes"

  quality_metrics:
    average_coverage:
      metric: "Average kernel coverage"
      target: "> 0.8"
      trend: "Monitor over time"

    average_confidence:
      metric: "Average confidence score"
      target: "> 0.7"
      trend: "Monitor over time"

    eml_distribution:
      metric: "EML level distribution"
      target: "EML_3+ > 70%"
      trend: "Monitor over time"

  logging:
    extraction_logs:
      level: "INFO"
      contents:
        - "Repository URL"
        - "Extraction stages"
        - "Errors and warnings"
        - "Processing time"

    audit_logs:
      level: "DEBUG"
      contents:
        - "Signal detection details"
        - "Evidence collection details"
        - "Mapping decisions"
        - "Validation results"

  alerting:
    critical_alerts:
      - "Pipeline crash"
      - "Queue stall"
      - "Storage quota exceeded"
      - "Authentication failure"

    warning_alerts:
      - "Success rate drop"
      - "Quality degradation"
      - "Resource underutilization"
      - "Cost overrun"
crumbs: Part VI — Operating Blueprint Mining at Scale

title: 23  Mining Pipeline Operations
text:
23.5 Chapter Summary

Repository discovery identifies potential extraction targets from GitHub search, organization inventories, code hosting platforms, and package registries
Repository filtering excludes unsuitable targets based on quality (activity, adoption), type (templates, examples), size, language, and license criteria
Filtering pipeline processes repositories through multiple stages with 5-20% retention rate from discovery to final list
Two-pass strategy balances cost and quality: cheap pass (lightweight analysis) followed by deep pass (full extraction)
Cheap pass performs quick analysis to estimate extraction feasibility and value; takes seconds to minutes
Deep pass performs comprehensive extraction including all stages from signal detection to validation; takes minutes to hours
Two-pass pipeline reduces cost by 60-80% and time by 50-70% by focusing deep pass on feasible repositories
Incremental extraction updates existing blueprints based on repository changes, reducing cost by 70-90%
Change detection analyzes commits to identify structural, interface, data, workflow, or minor changes
Impact analysis determines which architectural elements are affected and which extraction stages need re-running
Incremental challenges include cross-slot dependencies, boundary inference, confidence recalculation, and version conflicts
Pipeline orchestration manages queue processing, worker pools, storage, and monitoring at scale
Pipeline architecture includes repository queue, coordinator, worker pool, storage, and monitoring components
Resource management uses different worker configurations for cheap and deep passes, with auto-scaling and cost optimization
Monitoring tracks throughput, success rate, queue wait time, resource utilization, and quality metrics
Observability includes extraction logs, audit logs, and alerting for critical and warning conditions

Next Chapter: Chapter 22 introduces Normalization and Deduplication—the framework for managing blueprint similarity and variation across repositories. This chapter covers blueprint family identification, variant normalization, duplicate detection, and canonical representation for pattern discovery.
crumbs: Part VI — Operating Blueprint Mining at Scale

title: 24  Normalization and Deduplication
text:
24.1 Blueprint Families
As the mining pipeline extracts blueprints from thousands of repositories, it inevitably encounters similar architectures. Different organizations may implement payment services, user authentication, or data pipelines in similar ways. These similarities provide valuable insights into architectural patterns, but they also create redundancy in blueprint datasets.
This chapter defines the normalization and deduplication framework for managing blueprint similarity. The framework identifies blueprint families, normalizes variations, detects duplicates, and creates canonical representations that support pattern discovery.
Architectural similarity emerges from common problems, constraints, and conventions. A blueprint family represents a group of blueprints that share fundamental architectural structure while differing in specific implementation details.
Family characteristics:
Family example: REST API services
Family identification process:
crumbs: Part VI — Operating Blueprint Mining at Scale

title: 24  Normalization and Deduplication
text:
blueprint_families:
  definition: "Group of blueprints with shared fundamental architectural structure"
  criteria:
    structural_similarity:
      - similar_kernel_slot_population
      - similar_component_types
      - similar_workflow_structure
      - similar_interface_types

    functional_similarity:
      - similar_capabilities
      - similar data models
      - similar integration patterns

    domain_similarity:
      - solve similar problems
      - operate in similar domains
      - address similar requirements

  family_identification:
    kernel_structure:
      components:
        - similar_component_count
        - similar_component_types
        - similar_component_boundaries

      interfaces:
        - similar_interface_count
        - similar_interface_types
        - similar_interface_patterns

      data:
        - similar_data_model_structure
        - similar_storage_types
        - similar_data_relationships

      workflows:
        - similar_workflow_types
        - similar_workflow_structure
        - similar_trigger_types

    capability_overlap:
      metric: "Jaccard similarity of capabilities"
      calculation: "intersection / union"
      threshold: "> 0.6"

    structural_similarity:
      metric: "Graph similarity of component relationships"
      calculation: "Graph edit distance"
      threshold: "Similar within 20% edit distance"

family_example_rest_api_services:
  family_name: "REST API Services"
  description: "Standard REST API service architecture"

  common_characteristics:
    kernel_structure:
      Goal: "Provide REST API for domain operations"
      Capabilities: "CRUD operations on domain entities"
      Components:
        - api_service (type: Service)
        - possibly: worker_service (type: Worker)
      Interfaces:
        - api_interface (type: API)
      Data:
        - domain_table (type: Table)
      Workflows:
        - api_request_processing (type: Pipeline)

    technology_patterns:
      - Web framework: FastAPI, Flask, Express.js
      - Database: PostgreSQL, MySQL, MongoDB
      - ORM: SQLAlchemy, TypeORM, Mongoose
      - Authentication: JWT, OAuth2

  variant_1_simple_crud:
    blueprint_id: "simple-todo-api"
    components:
      - comp.todo_service (Service)
    interfaces:
      - int.todo_api (API)
    data:
      - data.todos (Table)
    workflows:
      - wf.api_processing (Pipeline)
    capabilities:
      - cap.create_todo
      - cap.read_todo
      - cap.update_todo
      - cap.delete_todo
    technology:
      framework: FastAPI
      database: PostgreSQL
      orm: SQLAlchemy

  variant_2_crud_with_worker:
    blueprint_id: "task-processing-api"
    components:
      - comp.task_service (Service)
      - comp.task_worker (Worker)
    interfaces:
      - int.task_api (API)
      - int.task_events (Event)
    data:
      - data.tasks (Table)
      - data.task_queue (Queue)
    workflows:
      - wf.api_processing (Pipeline)
      - wf.task_processing (Pipeline)
    capabilities:
      - cap.create_task
      - cap.read_task
      - cap.update_task
      - cap.delete_task
      - cap.process_task_async
    technology:
      framework: Flask
      database: MySQL
      message_queue: RabbitMQ

  variant_3_crud_with_caching:
    blueprint_id: "product-catalog-api"
    components:
      - comp.product_service (Service)
    interfaces:
      - int.product_api (API)
    data:
      - data.products (Table)
      - data.product_cache (Key-Value)
    workflows:
      - wf.api_processing_with_cache (Pipeline)
    capabilities:
      - cap.create_product
      - cap.read_product
      - cap.update_product
      - cap.delete_product
      - cap.cache_product
    technology:
      framework: Express.js
      database: MongoDB
      cache: Redis

  family_metrics:
    component_types: "Service (always), Worker (sometimes)"
    interface_types: "API (always), Event (sometimes)"
    data_types: "Table (always), Queue (sometimes), Key-Value (sometimes)"
    workflow_types: "Pipeline (always)"
    technology_diversity: "Multiple frameworks, databases, caches"
    structural_similarity: "0.85"
    capability_overlap: "0.75"

family_identification:
  step_1_similarity_calculation:
    action: "Calculate pairwise similarity"
    metrics:
      - kernel_structure_similarity
      - capability_jaccard_similarity
      - component_type_similarity
      - interface_type_similarity
      - graph_edit_distance
    output: "Similarity matrix"

  step_2_clustering:
    action: "Cluster similar blueprints"
    algorithm: "Hierarchical clustering or DBSCAN"
    parameters:
      - similarity_threshold: "0.6"
      - minimum_cluster_size: "3"
    output: "Blueprint families"

  step_3_family_characterization:
    action: "Characterize each family"
    analysis:
      - "Identify common patterns"
      - "Identify variations"
      - "Document technology patterns"
    output: "Family profiles"

  step_4_family_labeling:
    action: "Assign meaningful family labels"
    criteria:
      - "Based on domain"
      - "Based on architecture"
      - "Based on capabilities"
    output: "Labeled families"

  example_clustering:
    blueprints:
      - blueprint_a: "simple-todo-api"
      - blueprint_b: "task-processing-api"
      - blueprint_c: "product-catalog-api"
      - blueprint_d: "microservices-system"
      - blueprint_e: "data-pipeline"

    similarity_matrix:
      a-b: 0.85
      a-c: 0.80
      a-d: 0.40
      a-e: 0.25
      b-c: 0.75
      b-d: 0.35
      b-e: 0.30
      c-d: 0.30
      c-e: 0.35
      d-e: 0.65

    clustering_result:
      family_1_rest_api_services:
        members: [a, b, c]
        avg_similarity: 0.80
        label: "REST API Services"

      family_2_microservices:
        members: [d]
        label: "Microservices System"

      family_3_data_pipelines:
        members: [e]
        label: "Data Pipeline"
crumbs: Part VI — Operating Blueprint Mining at Scale

title: 24  Normalization and Deduplication
text:
24.2 Variant Normalization
Blueprints within a family exhibit variation due to technology choices, team conventions, and specific requirements. Variant normalization reduces these variations to canonical forms that highlight the shared architectural structure while preserving meaningful differences.
Normalization strategies:
normalization_strategies:
  technology_abstraction:
    objective: "Abstract specific technologies to generic categories"
    transformations:
      web_frameworks:
        - FastAPI → web_framework
        - Flask → web_framework
        - Express.js → web_framework
        - Spring Boot → web_framework

      databases:
        - PostgreSQL → relational_database
        - MySQL → relational_database
        - MongoDB → document_database
        - Redis → key_value_store

      message_queues:
        - RabbitMQ → message_queue
        - Kafka → message_queue
        - SQS → message_queue

    rationale: "Technology choices are implementation details, not architectural differences"

  naming_normalization:
    objective: "Normalize entity names to canonical forms"
    transformations:
      component_names:
        - payment_service → api_service
        - order_service → api_service
        - user_service → api_service

      interface_names:
        - payment_api → api_interface
        - order_api → api_interface
        - user_api → api_interface

      data_names:
        - payments → domain_data
        - orders → domain_data
        - users → domain_data

    rationale: "Names are domain-specific, not architectural"

  capability_normalization:
    objective: "Map capabilities to canonical operations"
    transformations:
      crud_operations:
        - create_payment → create_entity
        - create_order → create_entity
        - create_user → create_entity
        - read_payment → read_entity
        - read_order → read_entity
        - read_user → read_entity

      async_operations:
        - process_payment → process_entity_async
        - process_order → process_entity_async
        - process_user → process_entity_async

    rationale: "Specific capabilities are instances of general patterns"

  structural_normalization:
    objective: "Normalize structural variations"
    transformations:
      optional_components:
        - "If optional component present, preserve as optional"
        - "If optional component absent, represent as optional with null"

      interface_variations:
        - "Normalize interface protocols to generic types"
        - "Preserve interface semantics"

      data_variations:
        - "Normalize schema variations to generic structure"
        - "Preserve data relationships"

    rationale: "Optional variations should be represented consistently"
Normalization example:
normalization_example:
  input_blueprint:
    blueprint_id: "payment-service-v1"
    kernel:
      Goal: "Process payment transactions"
      Capabilities:
        - create_payment
        - process_payment
        - refund_payment
      Components:
        - payment_service (Service, FastAPI)
        - payment_worker (Worker)
      Interfaces:
        - payment_api (API, HTTP/REST)
        - payment_events (Event, RabbitMQ)
      Data:
        - payments (Table, PostgreSQL)
        - transactions (Table, PostgreSQL)
      Workflows:
        - payment_processing (Pipeline)

  normalized_blueprint:
    blueprint_id: "rest-api-service-template"
    kernel:
      Goal: "Provide CRUD and async operations"
      Capabilities:
        - create_entity
        - read_entity
        - update_entity
        - delete_entity
        - process_entity_async
      Components:
        - api_service (Service, web_framework)
        - worker_service (Worker, optional: true)
      Interfaces:
        - api_interface (API, generic_protocol)
        - event_interface (Event, message_queue, optional: true)
      Data:
        - domain_data (Table, relational_database)
        - transaction_log (Table, relational_database)
      Workflows:
        - processing_pipeline (Pipeline)

  transformations_applied:
    - "Technology abstraction: FastAPI → web_framework, PostgreSQL → relational_database, RabbitMQ → message_queue"
    - "Naming normalization: payment_* → generic names"
    - "Capability normalization: specific CRUD → generic CRUD"
    - "Structural normalization: worker marked as optional"

  family_representation:
    family: "REST API Services"
    canonical_template: "normalized_blueprint"
    variation_count: 3
    variation_types:
      - "Worker present/absent"
      - "Caching layer present/absent"
      - "Async processing present/absent"
Normalization challenges:
normalization_challenges:
  over_normalization:
    problem: "Losing meaningful differences"
    example:
      - "Normalizing microservices to single service"
      - "Losing architectural complexity"
    mitigation:
      - "Preserve structural differences"
      - "Mark optional variations explicitly"
      - "Maintain family sub-clusters"

  context_loss:
    problem: "Losing domain-specific information"
    example:
      - "Payment service vs. Order service"
      - "Different business semantics"
    mitigation:
      - "Retain original blueprint"
      - "Keep family as abstraction layer"
      - "Link normalized to original"

  semantic_drift:
    problem: "Normalization may introduce semantic errors"
    example:
      - "CRUD operations don't capture domain logic"
      - "Generic names lose business meaning"
    mitigation:
      - "Careful capability mapping"
      - "Review normalization decisions"
      - "Human validation of templates"
crumbs: Part VI — Operating Blueprint Mining at Scale

title: 24  Normalization and Deduplication
text:
24.3 Duplicate Detection
Similar repositories or repeated extractions can produce duplicate blueprints within the dataset. Duplicate detection identifies these duplicates to maintain dataset quality and efficiency.
Duplicate sources:
duplicate_sources:
  repository_duplicates:
    cause: "Same repository extracted multiple times"
    scenarios:
      - "Extraction repeated after code changes"
      - "Different branches of same repository"
      - "Repository cloned under different names"
    detection:
      - "Compare repository URLs"
      - "Compare commit hashes"
      - "Compare file structures"

  architectural_duplicates:
    cause: "Different repositories with identical architecture"
    scenarios:
      - "Tutorial examples copied"
      - "Boilerplate projects cloned"
      - "Standard templates used"
    detection:
      - "Kernel structure comparison"
      - "Capability comparison"
      - "Component comparison"

  variant_duplicates:
    cause: "Minor variations within same family"
    scenarios:
      - "Configuration differences only"
      - "Technology stack changes only"
      - "Naming differences only"
    detection:
      - "Normalized form comparison"
      - "Semantic similarity analysis"
      - "Edit distance calculation"
Duplicate detection metrics:
duplicate_detection_metrics:
  exact_duplicates:
    criterion: "100% match on kernel structure"
    comparison:
      - component_identical: true
      - interface_identical: true
      - data_identical: true
      - workflow_identical: true
      - capabilities_identical: true
    action: "Remove duplicates, keep one instance"

  near_duplicates:
    criterion: "> 95% similarity on normalized forms"
    comparison:
      - kernel_similarity: > 0.95
      - capability_similarity: > 0.95
      - component_similarity: > 0.95
    action: "Merge or flag for review"

  architectural_clones:
    criterion: "> 90% similarity with same normalized template"
    comparison:
      - normalized_form_identical: true
      - capability_semantic_similarity: > 0.90
    action: "Keep as variants, link to template"

  detection_process:
    step_1_exact_match:
      - "Compare repository URLs and commit hashes"
      - "Identify exact repository duplicates"
      - "Remove duplicate extractions"

    step_2_kernel_comparison:
      - "Compare kernel structures"
      - "Identify near duplicates"
      - "Calculate similarity scores"

    step_3_normalized_comparison:
      - "Compare normalized forms"
      - "Identify architectural clones"
      - "Link to family templates"

    step_4_review:
      - "Review ambiguous cases"
      - "Human validation of duplicates"
      - "Finalize duplicate removal"
Duplicate detection example:
duplicate_detection_example:
  blueprints:
    - blueprint_a:
        id: "payment-service-main"
        repo: "https://github.com/org/payment-service"
        commit: "abc123"
        kernel:
          components: [payment_service, payment_worker]
          interfaces: [payment_api, payment_events]

    - blueprint_b:
        id: "payment-service-feature-branch"
        repo: "https://github.com/org/payment-service"
        commit: "def456"
        kernel:
          components: [payment_service, payment_worker, refund_worker]
          interfaces: [payment_api, payment_events, refund_api]

    - blueprint_c:
        id: "payment-service-tutorial"
        repo: "https://github.com/other/payment-tutorial"
        commit: "ghi789"
        kernel:
          components: [payment_service, payment_worker]
          interfaces: [payment_api, payment_events]

  detection_results:
    exact_duplicates: []
    rationale: "Different commit hashes and architectures"

    near_duplicates: []
    rationale: "Component differences (b has refund_worker)"

    architectural_clones:
      - pair: [blueprint_a, blueprint_c]
        similarity: 0.95
        rationale: "Identical kernel structure, different repositories"
        action: "Link as variants"

    repository_duplicates: []
    rationale: "Different repository URLs"

  actions:
    - "Keep blueprint_a as canonical"
    - "Keep blueprint_b as separate (architectural change)"
    - "Link blueprint_c as clone of blueprint_a"
    - "Mark clone relationship in metadata"
crumbs: Part VI — Operating Blueprint Mining at Scale

title: 24  Normalization and Deduplication
text:
24.4 Canonical Representation
For each blueprint family, the pipeline generates a canonical representation—a normalized template that captures the shared architectural structure. Canonical representations serve as the basis for pattern discovery and architectural analysis.
Canonical template structure:
canonical_template:
  family: "REST API Services"

  kernel_template:
    Goal:
      value: "Provide CRUD operations for domain entities with optional async processing"
      template: true

    Capabilities:
      - capability: create_entity
        template: true
        required: true

      - capability: read_entity
        template: true
        required: true

      - capability: update_entity
        template: true
        required: true

      - capability: delete_entity
        template: true
        required: true

      - capability: process_entity_async
        template: true
        required: false

    Components:
      - component: api_service
        type: Service
        template: true
        required: true
        interfaces: [api_interface]
        data_access: [domain_data]

      - component: worker_service
        type: Worker
        template: true
        required: false
        interfaces: [event_interface]
        data_access: [domain_data]

    Interfaces:
      - interface: api_interface
        type: API
        template: true
        required: true
        protocol: "generic_http"
        exposes: [create_entity, read_entity, update_entity, delete_entity]

      - interface: event_interface
        type: Event
        template: true
        required: false
        protocol: "generic_message_queue"
        exposes: [process_entity_async]

    Data:
      - data: domain_data
        type: Table
        template: true
        required: true
        storage: "relational_database"

      - data: transaction_log
        type: Table
        template: true
        required: false
        storage: "relational_database"

    Workflows:
      - workflow: processing_pipeline
        type: Pipeline
        template: true
        required: true
        stages:
          - "receive_api_request"
          - "validate_request"
          - "process_data"
          - "return_response"

  variation_patterns:
    pattern_1: "Worker presence"
      description: "Optional worker for async processing"
      frequency: "40%"

    pattern_2: "Caching layer"
      description: "Cache layer for read operations"
      frequency: "30%"

    pattern_3: "Message queue"
      description: "Message queue for async events"
      frequency: "35%"

  technology_patterns:
    web_frameworks:
      - FastAPI: 35%
      - Flask: 25%
      - Express.js: 20%
      - Spring Boot: 20%

    databases:
      - PostgreSQL: 40%
      - MySQL: 30%
      - MongoDB: 20%
      - SQLite: 10%

    message_queues:
      - RabbitMQ: 40%
      - Kafka: 30%
      - SQS: 20%
      - Redis: 10%

  statistics:
    total_blueprints: 150
    family_members: 45
    template_confidence: 0.92
    variation_coverage: 0.85
Canonical representation benefits:
canonical_benefits:
  pattern_discovery:
    benefit: "Canonical templates make patterns explicit"
    outcome: "Identify architectural patterns across families"
    impact: "Better architectural understanding"

  dataset_organization:
    benefit: "Organize blueprints by families"
    outcome: "Easier navigation and search"
    impact: "Better dataset usability"

  quality_metrics:
    benefit: "Compare blueprints to canonical templates"
    outcome: "Measure deviation from patterns"
    impact: "Identify outliers and innovations"

  learning:
    benefit: "Canonical templates capture best practices"
    outcome: "Learn from common patterns"
    impact: "Better architectural guidance"

  generation:
    benefit: "Canonical templates can guide new implementations"
    outcome: "Generate scaffold code"
    impact: "Faster development"

title: 24  Normalization and Deduplication
text:
24.5 Normalization and Deduplication Pipeline
The normalization and deduplication pipeline operates after extraction to organize blueprints into families, normalize variants, detect duplicates, and generate canonical representations.
Pipeline workflow:
normalization_deduplication_pipeline:
  stage_1_family_identification:
    action: "Identify blueprint families"
    input: "Extracted blueprints"
    process:
      - "Calculate pairwise similarity"
      - "Cluster blueprints"
      - "Label families"
    output: "Blueprint families"

  stage_2_normalization:
    action: "Normalize blueprints within families"
    input: "Blueprint families"
    process:
      - "Abstract technologies"
      - "Normalize names"
      - "Normalize capabilities"
      - "Normalize structure"
    output: "Normalized blueprints"

  stage_3_duplicate_detection:
    action: "Detect duplicates"
    input: "Normalized blueprints"
    process:
      - "Find exact duplicates"
      - "Find near duplicates"
      - "Find architectural clones"
    output: "Duplicate relationships"

  stage_4_canonical_generation:
    action: "Generate canonical templates"
    input: "Normalized blueprints"
    process:
      - "Identify common patterns"
      - "Create template structure"
      - "Document variations"
    output: "Canonical templates"

  stage_5_dataset_organization:
    action: "Organize dataset"
    input: "Canonical templates and blueprints"
    process:
      - "Link blueprints to families"
      - "Link blueprints to templates"
      - "Index for search"
    output: "Organized dataset"

  stage_6_quality_metrics:
    action: "Calculate quality metrics"
    input: "Organized dataset"
    process:
      - "Family coverage"
      - "Template confidence"
      - "Duplicate rate"
    output: "Quality metrics"
Quality metrics:
quality_metrics:
  family_coverage:
    metric: "Proportion of blueprints assigned to families"
    calculation: "blueprints_in_families / total_blueprints"
    target: "> 0.7"
    interpretation: "Higher is better - more structure in dataset"

  template_confidence:
    metric: "Confidence in canonical templates"
    calculation: "average_similarity_to_template"
    target: "> 0.85"
    interpretation: "Higher is better - templates are accurate"

  duplicate_rate:
    metric: "Proportion of duplicate blueprints"
    calculation: "duplicates / total_blueprints"
    target: "< 0.1"
    interpretation: "Lower is better - less redundancy"

  variation_coverage:
    metric: "Proportion of variations captured in templates"
    calculation: "variations_in_templates / total_variations"
    target: "> 0.8"
    interpretation: "Higher is better - templates are comprehensive"

  example_metrics:
    total_blueprints: 1000
    blueprints_in_families: 750
    family_coverage: 0.75
    template_confidence: 0.88
    duplicates: 80
    duplicate_rate: 0.08
    variations_in_templates: 240
    total_variations: 300
    variation_coverage: 0.8
crumbs: Part VI — Operating Blueprint Mining at Scale

title: 24  Normalization and Deduplication
text:
24.6 Chapter Summary

Blueprint families represent groups of blueprints with shared fundamental architectural structure
Family identification uses structural similarity (kernel structure), functional similarity (capability overlap), and domain similarity criteria
Similarity metrics include kernel structure similarity, Jaccard capability similarity, component/interface type similarity, and graph edit distance
Variant normalization reduces variations to canonical forms through technology abstraction, naming normalization, capability normalization, and structural normalization
Technology abstraction maps specific technologies to generic categories (FastAPI → web_framework, PostgreSQL → relational_database)
Naming normalization maps domain-specific names to canonical forms (payment_service → api_service)
Capability normalization maps specific operations to generic patterns (create_payment → create_entity)
Duplicate detection identifies exact duplicates (100% match), near duplicates (>95% similarity), and architectural clones (>90% similarity)
Duplicate sources include repository duplicates (same repo extracted multiple times), architectural duplicates (identical architecture), and variant duplicates (minor variations)
Canonical representation generates normalized templates capturing shared architectural structure for each family
Canonical templates include kernel templates with required/optional elements, variation patterns, technology patterns, and statistics
Normalization benefits include pattern discovery, dataset organization, quality metrics, learning, and code generation
Pipeline workflow: family identification → normalization → duplicate detection → canonical generation → dataset organization → quality metrics
Quality metrics include family coverage (>0.7), template confidence (>0.85), duplicate rate (<0.1), and variation coverage (>0.8)

Next Chapter: Chapter 23 introduces From Blueprints to Patterns—the framework for discovering architectural patterns from blueprint datasets. This chapter covers pattern extraction, pattern clustering, pattern signatures, and pattern libraries for reusable architectural knowledge.
crumbs: Part VI — Operating Blueprint Mining at Scale

title: 25  From Blueprints to Patterns
text:
25.1 What Are Architectural Patterns?
Once a large collection of blueprints has been extracted, normalized, and organized, a new question emerges: what can we learn from this collection of architectural knowledge? Individual blueprints describe specific systems, but collections of blueprints reveal architectural patterns that recur across systems.
This chapter defines the pattern discovery framework for extracting reusable architectural patterns from blueprint datasets. The framework identifies recurring structures, abstracts pattern signatures, and organizes patterns into libraries that serve as references for architecture design and analysis.
In this context, an architectural pattern is a reusable architectural structure that appears repeatedly across multiple systems. Patterns are not code snippets—they are high-level structural relationships that capture how components, interfaces, data, and workflows are organized.
Pattern characteristics:
Pattern examples:
crumbs: Part VI — Operating Blueprint Mining at Scale

title: 25  From Blueprints to Patterns
text:
pattern_characteristics:
  definition: "Reusable architectural structure appearing repeatedly across systems"

  core_attributes:
    structural_recurrence:
      description: "Pattern appears in multiple systems"
      criteria: "Found in >= 5 blueprints"
      examples:
        - REST API service pattern (45 blueprints)
        - Microservices pattern (32 blueprints)
        - Event-driven architecture pattern (28 blueprints)

    coherence:
      description: "Pattern has coherent structural relationships"
      criteria: "Elements within pattern have strong internal coupling"
      examples:
        - API service + data store + processing workflow
        - Publisher + message queue + subscriber

    abstraction_level:
      description: "Pattern operates at architectural level, not implementation"
      criteria: "Independent of specific technologies"
      examples:
        - "API service pattern" not "FastAPI service pattern"
        - "Message queue pattern" not "RabbitMQ pattern"

    reusability:
      description: "Pattern can be applied to new systems"
      criteria: "Pattern provides structural guidance"
      examples:
        - "Use REST API pattern for new service"
        - "Use event-driven pattern for async system"

  pattern_vs_blueprint:
    blueprint:
      describes: "Specific system architecture"
      scope: "Individual system"
      specificity: "Detailed, concrete"
      example: "Payment processing system with FastAPI, PostgreSQL, RabbitMQ"

    pattern:
      describes: "Reusable architectural structure"
      scope: "Multiple systems"
      specificity: "Abstract, generalized"
      example: "REST API service with async processing"

pattern_examples:
  pattern_1_rest_api_service:
    name: "REST API Service"
    description: "Standard REST API service architecture"

    structure:
      components:
        - api_service (Service)
        - optional: worker_service (Worker)

      interfaces:
        - api_interface (API)
        - optional: event_interface (Event)

      data:
        - domain_data (Table)

      workflows:
        - request_processing (Pipeline)

    recurrence: 45 blueprints
    domains:
      - E-commerce
      - SaaS
      - Internal tools

    variations:
      - with worker: 40%
      - with caching: 30%
      - with message queue: 35%

  pattern_2_event_driven:
    name: "Event-Driven Architecture"
    description: "Decoupled communication through events"

    structure:
      components:
        - event_producer (Service)
        - event_consumer (Service or Worker)
        - message_broker (ControlPlane)

      interfaces:
        - event_publisher (Event)
        - event_subscriber (Event)

      data:
        - event_store (Table)
        - optional: event_log (Table)

      workflows:
        - event_publishing (Pipeline)
        - event_processing (Pipeline)

    recurrence: 28 blueprints
    domains:
      - Real-time systems
      - Microservices
      - IoT platforms

    variations:
      - with event store: 60%
      - with dead-letter queue: 45%
      - with event replay: 30%

  pattern_3_data_pipeline:
    name: "Batch Data Pipeline"
    description: "Structured data processing pipeline"

    structure:
      components:
        - data_ingestion (Worker)
        - data_transformation (Worker)
        - data_loading (Worker)
        - scheduler (Scheduler)
        - orchestration_engine (ControlPlane)

      interfaces:
        - data_source (API or CLI)
        - notification_interface (Event)

      data:
        - raw_data (Table or Document)
        - transformed_data (Table or Document)
        - data_catalog (Table)

      workflows:
        - ingestion_workflow (Pipeline)
        - transformation_workflow (Pipeline)
        - loading_workflow (Pipeline)
        - scheduled_pipeline (Schedule)

    recurrence: 22 blueprints
    domains:
      - Data engineering
      - Analytics
      - ETL systems

    variations:
      - with real-time streaming: 35%
      - with data quality checks: 55%
      - with monitoring: 70%
