title: 25  From Blueprints to Patterns
text:
25.2 Pattern Extraction
Pattern extraction identifies recurring architectural structures from blueprint datasets. The process analyzes blueprint families, identifies common substructures, and abstracts these substructures into reusable patterns.
Extraction process:
pattern_extraction:
  step_1_family_analysis:
    action: "Analyze blueprint families"
    input: "Blueprint families with canonical templates"
    analysis:
      - "Identify common kernel structures"
      - "Identify common capability patterns"
      - "Identify common interface patterns"
      - "Identify common workflow patterns"
    output: "Family structural summaries"

  step_2_substructure_identification:
    action: "Identify recurring substructures"
    input: "Family structural summaries"
    analysis:
      - "Find common component combinations"
      - "Find common interface patterns"
      - "Find common data models"
      - "Find common workflow structures"
    output: "Candidate substructures"

  step_3_pattern_abstraction:
    action: "Abstract substructures into patterns"
    input: "Candidate substructures"
    analysis:
      - "Generalize specific names"
      - "Identify required vs optional elements"
      - "Document relationships"
      - "Specify constraints"
    output: "Pattern definitions"

  step_4_pattern_validation:
    action: "Validate patterns against blueprints"
    input: "Pattern definitions and blueprints"
    analysis:
      - "Check pattern coverage"
      - "Check pattern consistency"
      - "Check pattern utility"
    output: "Validated patterns"

  step_5_pattern_refinement:
    action: "Refine patterns based on feedback"
    input: "Validation results"
    analysis:
      - "Adjust pattern boundaries"
      - "Add missing elements"
      - "Remove over-specific elements"
    output: "Refined patterns"
Substructure identification:
substructure_identification:
  component_combinations:
    analysis: "Find common component type combinations"
    method: "Frequent itemset mining"
    example:
      frequent_combination:
        - api_service (Service)
        - data_store (Table)
      frequency: 45/150 (30%)
      pattern: "REST API service with database"

      frequent_combination:
        - api_service (Service)
        - worker_service (Worker)
        - message_queue (Queue)
      frequency: 28/150 (19%)
      pattern: "API service with async worker"

  interface_patterns:
    analysis: "Find common interface structures"
    method: "Pattern matching on interface definitions"
    example:
      frequent_pattern:
        - api_interface (API)
        - methods: [create, read, update, delete]
      frequency: 38/150 (25%)
      pattern: "CRUD API interface"

      frequent_pattern:
        - event_interface (Event)
        - events: [created, updated, deleted]
      frequency: 25/150 (17%)
      pattern: "Entity lifecycle events"

  workflow_patterns:
    analysis: "Find common workflow structures"
    method: "Graph pattern matching"
    example:
      frequent_pattern:
        - receive_request → validate → process → respond
      frequency: 42/150 (28%)
      pattern: "Standard API request processing"

      frequent_pattern:
        - ingest → transform → validate → load
      frequency: 22/150 (15%)
      pattern: "ETL pipeline structure"
Pattern abstraction example:
pattern_abstraction:
  input_substructure:
    components:
      - payment_service (Service, FastAPI)
      - payment_worker (Worker)
      - payment_queue (Queue, RabbitMQ)

    interfaces:
      - payment_api (API, HTTP/REST)
      - payment_events (Event, RabbitMQ)

    data:
      - payments (Table, PostgreSQL)
      - transactions (Table, PostgreSQL)

    workflows:
      - payment_processing (Pipeline)

  abstraction_steps:
    step_1_generalize_names:
      - payment_service → api_service
      - payment_worker → worker_service
      - payment_queue → message_queue
      - payment_api → api_interface
      - payment_events → event_interface
      - payments → domain_data
      - transactions → transaction_log

    step_2_abstract_technologies:
      - FastAPI → web_framework
      - RabbitMQ → message_queue
      - PostgreSQL → relational_database

    step_3_identify_required_elements:
      - api_service: required
      - api_interface: required
      - domain_data: required
      - payment_processing: required

    step_4_identify_optional_elements:
      - worker_service: optional
      - event_interface: optional
      - transaction_log: optional

    step_5_specify_relationships:
      - api_service exposes api_interface
      - api_service owns domain_data
      - worker_service subscribes to event_interface
      - payment_processing implemented by api_service

  output_pattern:
    name: "REST API Service with Async Processing"
    description: "API service with optional async worker for background processing"

    structure:
      required:
        - api_service (Service)
        - api_interface (API)
        - domain_data (Table)
        - request_processing (Pipeline)

      optional:
        - worker_service (Worker)
        - event_interface (Event)
        - transaction_log (Table)

    relationships:
      - api_service exposes api_interface
      - api_service owns domain_data
      - worker_service subscribes to event_interface
      - request_processing implemented by api_service

    recurrence: 28 blueprints
    confidence: 0.92
crumbs: Part VI — Operating Blueprint Mining at Scale

title: 25  From Blueprints to Patterns
text:
25.3 Pattern Clustering
Different patterns may share similarities. Pattern clustering groups similar patterns into pattern families that represent broader architectural categories.
Clustering criteria:
pattern_clustering:
  structural_similarity:
    criterion: "Patterns share similar kernel structures"
    metric: "Graph similarity of pattern graphs"
    threshold: "> 0.7"

    example:
      pattern_a: "REST API Service"
        structure: api_service + api_interface + domain_data

      pattern_b: "GraphQL API Service"
        structure: api_service + api_interface + domain_data

      similarity: 0.85
      cluster: "API Service Patterns"

  functional_similarity:
    criterion: "Patterns serve similar functional purposes"
    metric: "Capability overlap"
    threshold: "> 0.6"

    example:
      pattern_a: "REST API Service"
        capabilities: [create_entity, read_entity, update_entity, delete_entity]

      pattern_b: "GraphQL API Service"
        capabilities: [create_entity, read_entity, update_entity, delete_entity]

      similarity: 0.75
      cluster: "API Service Patterns"

  domain_similarity:
    criterion: "Patterns used in similar domains"
    metric: "Domain overlap"
    threshold: "> 0.5"

    example:
      pattern_a: "REST API Service"
        domains: [e-commerce, SaaS, internal tools]

      pattern_b: "GraphQL API Service"
        domains: [e-commerce, SaaS, mobile apps]

      similarity: 0.67
      cluster: "API Service Patterns"
Pattern clusters example:
pattern_clusters:
  cluster_1_api_services:
    name: "API Service Patterns"
    description: "Patterns for API-based service architectures"

    member_patterns:
      - pattern: "REST API Service"
        recurrence: 45
        description: "Standard REST API service"

      - pattern: "GraphQL API Service"
        recurrence: 18
        description: "GraphQL-based API service"

      - pattern: "gRPC Service"
        recurrence: 12
        description: "gRPC-based service"

    common_characteristics:
      - api_service component
      - api_interface (various protocols)
      - domain_data storage
      - request processing workflow

    variations:
      - protocol: HTTP/REST, GraphQL, gRPC
      - async processing: present/absent
      - caching: present/absent

  cluster_2_event_driven:
    name: "Event-Driven Patterns"
    description: "Patterns for event-based communication"

    member_patterns:
      - pattern: "Event-Driven Architecture"
        recurrence: 28
        description: "Decoupled event communication"

      - pattern: "Event Sourcing"
        recurrence: 8
        description: "Event-based state storage"

      - pattern: "CQRS"
        recurrence: 6
        description: "Command query separation"

    common_characteristics:
      - event interfaces
      - message broker
      - event consumers/producers
      - async processing

    variations:
      - event storage: present/absent
      - replay capability: present/absent
      - command/query separation: present/absent

  cluster_3_data_processing:
    name: "Data Processing Patterns"
    description: "Patterns for data processing and pipelines"

    member_patterns:
      - pattern: "Batch Data Pipeline"
        recurrence: 22
        description: "Structured batch processing"

      - pattern: "Stream Processing"
        recurrence: 15
        description: "Real-time stream processing"

      - pattern: "Lambda Architecture"
        recurrence: 5
        description: "Batch + stream hybrid"

    common_characteristics:
      - data ingestion
      - data transformation
      - data loading
      - orchestration

    variations:
      - processing: batch/stream/hybrid
      - real-time: present/absent
      - data quality checks: present/absent
crumbs: Part VI — Operating Blueprint Mining at Scale

title: 25  From Blueprints to Patterns
text:
25.4 Pattern Signatures
Each pattern is represented by a pattern signature—a concise, structured description that captures the essential characteristics of the pattern. Signatures enable pattern matching, pattern comparison, and pattern-based blueprint generation.
Signature structure:
pattern_signature:
  metadata:
    pattern_id: "pattern-rest-api-service"
    name: "REST API Service"
    cluster: "API Service Patterns"
    recurrence: 45
    confidence: 0.92

  kernel_signature:
    Goal:
      template: "Provide CRUD operations for domain entities"
      variability: "low"

    Components:
      required:
        - api_service (Service)
      optional:
        - worker_service (Worker)
        - cache_service (ControlPlane)

    Interfaces:
      required:
        - api_interface (API)
      optional:
        - event_interface (Event)

    Data:
      required:
        - domain_data (Table)
      optional:
        - cache_data (Key-Value)

    Workflows:
      required:
        - request_processing (Pipeline)
      optional:
        - async_processing (Pipeline)

  structural_signature:
    required_elements: 5
    optional_elements: 6
    total_elements: 11
    required_ratio: 5/11 = 0.45

    required_relationships:
      - api_service exposes api_interface
      - api_service owns domain_data
      - request_processing implemented by api_service

    optional_relationships:
      - worker_service subscribes to event_interface
      - cache_service manages cache_data

  variability_signature:
    high_variability_elements:
      - worker_service: present in 40%
      - cache_service: present in 30%
      - event_interface: present in 35%

    low_variability_elements:
      - api_service: present in 100%
      - api_interface: present in 100%
      - domain_data: present in 100%

  constraint_signature:
    constraints:
      - "api_service must expose at least one api_interface"
      - "api_service must own at least one domain_data"
      - "worker_service requires event_interface"

    invariants:
      - "API interface must have CRUD methods"
      - "Domain data must be relational"
      - "Request processing must be sequential"

  domain_signature:
    common_domains:
      - E-commerce: 35%
      - SaaS: 30%
      - Internal tools: 20%
      - Other: 15%

    common_use_cases:
      - CRUD operations
      - Business entity management
      - Data retrieval

  technology_signature:
    common_technologies:
      web_frameworks:
        - FastAPI: 35%
        - Flask: 25%
        - Express.js: 20%
        - Spring Boot: 20%

      databases:
        - PostgreSQL: 40%
        - MySQL: 30%
        - MongoDB: 20%
        - SQLite: 10%

  quality_signature:
    average_coverage: 0.95
    average_confidence: 0.88
    average_consistency: 0.92
    quality_grade: "high"
Signature matching:
signature_matching:
  purpose: "Match blueprints to patterns"

  matching_process:
    step_1_kernel_match:
      - "Compare blueprint kernel to pattern kernel"
      - "Calculate required element match rate"
      - "Calculate optional element match rate"

    step_2_relationship_match:
      - "Compare blueprint relationships to pattern relationships"
      - "Check invariants and constraints"

    step_3_variance_check:
      - "Check blueprint variations against pattern variability"
      - "Validate technology choices"

    step_4_score_calculation:
      - "Calculate overall match score"
      - "Determine pattern applicability"

  match_scoring:
    required_match_weight: 0.6
    optional_match_weight: 0.3
    relationship_weight: 0.1

    calculation:
      score = (
        (required_match_rate * 0.6) +
        (optional_match_rate * 0.3) +
        (relationship_match_rate * 0.1)
      )

    thresholds:
      high_match: "> 0.85"
      medium_match: "0.7 - 0.85"
      low_match: "< 0.7"

  example_matching:
    blueprint:
      components: [api_service, worker_service, cache_service]
      interfaces: [api_interface, event_interface]
      data: [domain_data, cache_data]
      workflows: [request_processing, async_processing]

    pattern: "REST API Service"
      required: [api_service, api_interface, domain_data, request_processing]
      optional: [worker_service, cache_service, event_interface, cache_data, async_processing]

    matching:
      required_match: 4/4 = 1.0
      optional_match: 4/5 = 0.8
      relationship_match: 0.9

    score:
      = (1.0 * 0.6) + (0.8 * 0.3) + (0.9 * 0.1)
      = 0.6 + 0.24 + 0.09
      = 0.93

    result: "HIGH MATCH"
    interpretation: "Blueprint strongly matches REST API Service pattern"
crumbs: Part VI — Operating Blueprint Mining at Scale

title: 25  From Blueprints to Patterns
text:
25.5 Pattern Libraries
Patterns are organized into pattern libraries that serve as comprehensive references for architectural design and analysis. Pattern libraries are structured, documented collections of patterns with search, browse, and comparison capabilities.
Library structure:
pattern_library:
  organization:
    by_cluster:
      - API Service Patterns
      - Event-Driven Patterns
      - Data Processing Patterns
      - Microservices Patterns
      - Batch Processing Patterns

    by_domain:
      - E-commerce Patterns
      - SaaS Patterns
      - Data Engineering Patterns
      - IoT Patterns
      - Internal Tools Patterns

    by_complexity:
      - Simple Patterns (1-3 components)
      - Moderate Patterns (4-7 components)
      - Complex Patterns (8+ components)

  documentation:
    for_each_pattern:
      metadata:
        - pattern_id
        - name
        - cluster
        - recurrence
        - confidence

      description:
        - "What the pattern does"
        - "When to use the pattern"
        - "When not to use the pattern"

      structure:
        - "Required components, interfaces, data, workflows"
        - "Optional elements"
        - "Relationships and constraints"

      examples:
        - "Example blueprints using the pattern"
        - "Example implementations"
        - "Technology stacks"

      variations:
        - "Common variations"
        - "Variation frequencies"
        - "Variation patterns"

      anti_patterns:
        - "Common mistakes"
        - "What to avoid"

      references:
        - "Related patterns"
        - "Evolution paths"
        - "Alternative patterns"

  search_capabilities:
    by_signature:
      - "Search by kernel structure"
      - "Search by capability set"
      - "Search by interface types"

    by_domain:
      - "Search by use case"
      - "Search by industry"
      - "Search by problem type"

    by_technology:
      - "Search by framework"
      - "Search by database"
      - "Search by protocol"

    by_quality:
      - "Search by coverage"
      - "Search by confidence"
      - "Search by recurrence"
Library usage:
library_usage:
  design_guidance:
    scenario: "Architect is designing a new payment service"
    process:
      step_1_browse_domain:
        - "Browse e-commerce patterns"
        - "Browse API service patterns"

      step_2_select_pattern:
        - "Select 'REST API Service with Async Processing'"
        - "Review pattern structure"

      step_3_adapt_pattern:
        - "Adapt pattern to payment domain"
        - "Choose technology stack"
        - "Add domain-specific elements"

      step_4_generate_blueprint:
        - "Generate initial blueprint from pattern"
        - "Refine blueprint"
        - "Implement system"

    benefits:
      - "Faster design process"
      - "Proven architecture"
      - "Best practices baked in"

  analysis_reference:
    scenario: "Architect is analyzing an existing system"
    process:
      step_1_match_pattern:
        - "Match system blueprint to patterns"
        - "Identify pattern matches"

      step_2_compare_to_library:
        - "Compare to canonical patterns"
        - "Identify deviations"

      step_3_assess_quality:
        - "Assess pattern adherence"
        - "Identify architectural debt"

      step_4_recommend_improvements:
        - "Suggest pattern-based improvements"
        - "Recommend refactorings"

    benefits:
      - "Objective assessment"
      - "Identify best practices"
      - "Guide improvements"

  learning_resource:
    scenario: "Developer is learning architectural patterns"
    process:
      step_1_explore_library:
        - "Browse pattern clusters"
        - "Read pattern descriptions"

      step_2_study_examples:
        - "Study example blueprints"
        - "Review implementations"

      step_3_understand_variations:
        - "Learn pattern variations"
        - "Understand trade-offs"

      step_4_apply_knowledge:
        - "Apply patterns to new projects"
        - "Build on best practices"

    benefits:
      - "Comprehensive resource"
      - "Real-world examples"
      - "Practical knowledge"
crumbs: Part VI — Operating Blueprint Mining at Scale

title: 25  From Blueprints to Patterns
text:
25.6 Chapter Summary

Architectural patterns are reusable architectural structures appearing repeatedly across systems
Pattern characteristics: structural recurrence (appears in multiple systems), coherence (strong internal coupling), abstraction level (architectural not implementation), reusability (applicable to new systems)
Patterns differ from blueprints: blueprints describe specific systems, patterns describe reusable structures
Common patterns: REST API Service (45 blueprints), Event-Driven Architecture (28 blueprints), Batch Data Pipeline (22 blueprints)
Pattern extraction: family analysis → substructure identification → pattern abstraction → pattern validation → pattern refinement
Substructure identification uses frequent itemset mining for components, pattern matching for interfaces, graph matching for workflows
Pattern abstraction generalizes names, abstracts technologies, identifies required/optional elements, specifies relationships
Pattern clustering groups similar patterns into families based on structural, functional, and domain similarity
Pattern clusters: API Service Patterns, Event-Driven Patterns, Data Processing Patterns
Pattern signatures capture essential characteristics: metadata, kernel signature, structural signature, variability signature, constraint signature, domain signature, technology signature, quality signature
Signature matching compares blueprints to patterns using required element match, optional element match, and relationship match
Pattern libraries organize patterns by cluster, domain, and complexity with comprehensive documentation
Library documentation includes metadata, description, structure, examples, variations, anti-patterns, and references
Library usage: design guidance (pattern-based design), analysis reference (pattern comparison), learning resource (pattern education)
Pattern discovery transforms blueprint collections into reusable architectural knowledge

Next Chapter: Chapter 24 introduces Blueprint Extraction Specification v0.1—the formal specification that defines the complete Blueprint Extraction framework. This chapter provides the authoritative reference for the extraction methodology, data structures, and operational procedures.
crumbs: Part VI — Operating Blueprint Mining at Scale

title: 26  Blueprint Extraction Specification v0.1
text:
26.1 Terminology
This chapter serves as the formal specification for the Blueprint Extraction framework. It consolidates the methodology, data structures, and operational procedures defined throughout this volume into a single authoritative reference. The specification provides the foundation for implementing extraction pipelines, building extraction tools, and ensuring consistency across extraction operations.
The specification is organized into sections covering: terminology, data structures, extraction methodology, quality standards, and operational procedures. Each section provides precise definitions and constraints to guide implementation.
Terms and definitions:

title: 26  Blueprint Extraction Specification v0.1
text:
terminology:
  blueprint:
    definition: "Structured representation of system architecture derived from repository artifacts"
    scope: "Includes kernel, views, evidence, and metadata"
    synonym: "Architectural reconstruction"

  kernel:
    definition: "Minimal architectural skeleton with six slots: Goal, Capabilities, Components, Interfaces, Data, Workflows"
    scope: "Represents essential architectural structure"
    synonym: "Blueprint Kernel"

  view:
    definition: "Projection of kernel tailored to specific stakeholders or concerns"
    scope: "Includes Product, Architecture, Operations, Security views"
    synonym: "Architectural view"

  signal:
    definition: "Repository artifact that may indicate architectural structure"
    scope: "Includes files, configurations, dependencies"
    synonym: "Architectural indicator"

  evidence:
    definition: "Structured observation linking signal to architectural claim"
    scope: "Includes signal reference, claim, confidence"
    synonym: "Extraction evidence"

  confidence:
    definition: "Three-state semantic indicating evidence strength: observed, inferred, unknown"
    scope: "Applies to architectural claims"
    synonym: "Confidence state"

  ontology_mapping:
    definition: "Translation of implementation artifacts to canonical entities"
    scope: "Components, interfaces, data, workflows"
    synonym: "Entity mapping"

  blueprint_pack:
    definition: "Standard output unit containing blueprint, views, evidence, and report"
    scope: "Version-controlled, reviewable representation"
    synonym: "Extraction output"

  extraction_maturity_level:
    definition: "Classification of extraction reliability (EML 0-4)"
    scope: "No extraction → reviewed and validated"
    synonym: "EML"

title: 26  Blueprint Extraction Specification v0.1
text:
26.2 Data Structures

26.2.1 Blueprint Kernel
Kernel schema:
blueprint_kernel:
  $schema: "https://spec.blueprintextraction.org/v0.1/kernel.schema.json"

  system:
    id: string (required)
    name: string (required)
    description: string (optional)
    repository_url: string (required)
    extraction_version: string (required)
    extraction_date: timestamp (required)

  kernel:
    Goal:
      value: string (required)
      confidence: number (0.0-1.0) (required)
      status: enum(observed, inferred, unknown) (required)
      evidence: array(evidence_reference) (optional)

    Capabilities:
      - id: string (required)
        name: string (required)
        description: string (optional)
        confidence: number (0.0-1.0) (required)
        status: enum(observed, inferred, unknown) (required)
        interfaces: array(interface_id) (optional)
        workflows: array(workflow_id) (optional)
        evidence: array(evidence_reference) (optional)

    Components:
      - id: string (required)
        name: string (required)
        type: enum(Service, Worker, Scheduler, Plugin, ControlPlane) (required)
        confidence: number (0.0-1.0) (required)
        status: enum(observed, inferred, unknown) (required)
        responsibilities: array(string) (optional)
        exposes: array(interface_id) (optional)
        owns: array(data_id) (optional)
        reads: array(data_id) (optional)
        participates_in: array(workflow_id) (optional)
        evidence: array(evidence_reference) (optional)

    Interfaces:
      - id: string (required)
        name: string (required)
        type: enum(API, Event, CLI, RPC) (required)
        protocol: string (optional)
        confidence: number (0.0-1.0) (required)
        status: enum(observed, inferred, unknown) (required)
        exposed_by: component_id (optional)
        exposes_capability: array(capability_id) (optional)
        contract: object (optional)
        evidence: array(evidence_reference) (optional)

    Data:
      - id: string (required)
        name: string (required)
        type: enum(Table, Collection, Document, Key-Value) (required)
        storage_type: enum(relational, document, key-value, object, analytical) (required)
        confidence: number (0.0-1.0) (required)
        status: enum(observed, inferred, unknown) (required)
        schema: object (optional)
        owned_by: component_id (optional)
        read_by: array(component_id) (optional)
        foreign_keys: array(object) (optional)
        evidence: array(evidence_reference) (optional)

    Workflows:
      - id: string (required)
        name: string (required)
        type: enum(DAG, Pipeline, Schedule, Event, StateMachine) (required)
        confidence: number (0.0-1.0) (required)
        status: enum(observed, inferred, unknown) (required)
        implemented_by: component_id (optional)
        participates: array(component_id) (optional)
        implements_capability: array(capability_id) (optional)
        triggers: array(object) (optional)
        tasks: array(object) (optional)
        evidence: array(evidence_reference) (optional)

    Constraint: (optional)
      non_functional_requirements: array(object) (optional)
      performance_requirements: array(object) (optional)
      security_requirements: array(object) (optional)


26.2.2 Evidence Object
Evidence schema:
evidence_object:
  $schema: "https://spec.blueprintextraction.org/v0.1/evidence.schema.json"

  evidence_reference:
    id: string (required)
    signal_id: string (required)
    signal_type: enum(structure, dependency, entrypoint, config, deploy, docs, test, observability, security, issue-pr) (required)
    signal_location: object (required)
      - path: string (required)
      - line: number (optional)
      - offset: number (optional)

    claim: string (required)
    confidence: number (0.0-1.0) (required)
    strength: enum(strong, moderate, weak) (required)

    extraction_method: string (required)
    extraction_date: timestamp (required)


26.2.3 Blueprint Pack
Pack structure:
blueprint_pack:
  structure:
    root/
      blueprint.yaml (required)
        - Kernel representation

      views/ (required)
        - product-view.yaml (required)
        - architecture-view.yaml (required)
        - operations-view.yaml (required)
        - security-view.yaml (required)

      evidence/ (required)
        - evidence-bundle.json (required)
        - signals/ (optional)
          - signal-001.json
          - signal-002.json

      report/ (required)
        - extraction-report.yaml (required)
        - validation-report.yaml (required)
        - quality-metrics.yaml (required)

      review/ (optional)
        - review-log.yaml
        - adr-001.yaml
        - adr-002.yaml

      metadata/ (required)
        - pack-metadata.yaml (required)
        - extraction-manifest.yaml (required)

  pack_metadata:
    pack_id: string (required)
    blueprint_id: string (required)
    version: string (required)
    created_at: timestamp (required)
    updated_at: timestamp (required)
    eml_level: enum(EML_0, EML_1, EML_2, EML_3, EML_4) (required)
    repository_url: string (required)
    commit_hash: string (required)

title: 26  Blueprint Extraction Specification v0.1
text:
26.3 Extraction Methodology

26.3.1 Extraction Pipeline
Pipeline stages:
extraction_pipeline:
  stage_1_signal_detection:
    objective: "Identify repository signals"
    input: "Repository files"
    output: "Signal inventory"
    process:
      - "Scan repository structure"
      - "Identify signal types"
      - "Extract signal metadata"
    quality_requirement: "Signal detection completeness > 0.8"

  stage_2_hypothesis_formation:
    objective: "Form architectural hypotheses"
    input: "Signal inventory"
    output: "Hypothesis set"
    process:
      - "Analyze signal patterns"
      - "Form component hypotheses"
      - "Form interface hypotheses"
      - "Form data hypotheses"
      - "Form workflow hypotheses"
    quality_requirement: "Hypothesis coverage > 0.7"

  stage_3_evidence_collection:
    objective: "Collect evidence for hypotheses"
    input: "Hypothesis set, signals"
    output: "Evidence bundle"
    process:
      - "Map hypotheses to signals"
      - "Extract evidence objects"
      - "Calculate confidence"
      - "Preserve conflicts"
    quality_requirement: "Evidence ratio > 0.8"

  stage_4_ontology_mapping:
    objective: "Map to canonical entities"
    input: "Evidence bundle"
    output: "Mapped entities"
    process:
      - "Normalize names"
      - "Canonicalize types"
      - "Establish relationships"
    quality_requirement: "Mapping consistency > 0.85"

  stage_5_kernel_construction:
    objective: "Construct blueprint kernel"
    input: "Mapped entities"
    output: "Blueprint kernel"
    process:
      - "Fill kernel slots"
      - "Apply triangular consistency"
      - "Calculate slot confidence"
    quality_requirement: "Kernel completeness > 0.8"

  stage_6_view_generation:
    objective: "Generate stakeholder views"
    input: "Blueprint kernel"
    output: "Architectural views"
    process:
      - "Project product view"
      - "Project architecture view"
      - "Project operations view"
      - "Project security view"
    quality_requirement: "View consistency = 1.0"

  stage_7_validation:
    objective: "Validate blueprint structure"
    input: "Blueprint kernel, views"
    output: "Validation report"
    process:
      - "DSL validation"
      - "Reference integrity check"
      - "Kernel completeness check"
      - "Anomaly detection"
    quality_requirement: "All critical checks must pass"

  stage_8_review:
    objective: "Human review and approval"
    input: "Validated blueprint"
    output: "Approved blueprint"
    process:
      - "Slot-oriented review"
      - "Priority-based review"
      - "Decision logging (ADR)"
      - "Maturity assessment"
    quality_requirement: "EML >= 3 for production use"


26.3.2 Quality Gates
Gate definitions:
quality_gates:
  gate_1_signal_detection:
    metric: "Signal detection completeness"
    threshold: "> 0.8"
    calculation: "detected_signals / expected_signals"
    action: "Retry or fail if below threshold"

  gate_2_evidence_collection:
    metric: "Evidence ratio"
    threshold: "> 0.8"
    calculation: "elements_with_evidence / total_elements"
    action: "Retry or fail if below threshold"

  gate_3_kernel_completeness:
    metric: "Kernel completeness"
    threshold: "> 0.8"
    calculation: "populated_slots / required_slots"
    action: "Retry or fail if below threshold"

  gate_4_validation:
    metric: "Validation status"
    threshold: "PASSED"
    checks:
      - DSL validation: must PASS
      - Reference integrity: must PASS
      - Kernel completeness: must PASS
    action: "Reject if any critical check fails"

  gate_5_review:
    metric: "EML level"
    threshold: ">= EML_3"
    levels:
      - EML_0: No extraction
      - EML_1: Partial signals
      - EML_2: Partial kernel
      - EML_3: Evidence-backed draft
      - EML_4: Reviewed and validated
    action: "EML < 3 requires additional review"


26.3.3 Confidence Calculation
Confidence derivation:
confidence_calculation:
  observed:
    definition: "Claim directly supported by strong signal"
    confidence_range: "0.8 - 1.0"
    derivation:
      - "Signal strength: strong"
      - "Signal count: >= 3"
      - "Consistency: no conflicts"
    example: "API route definition → interface exists"

  inferred:
    definition: "Claim derived from multiple weak signals or patterns"
    confidence_range: "0.4 - 0.8"
    derivation:
      - "Signal strength: moderate or weak"
      - "Signal count: 1-2"
      - "Consistency: some conflicts"
      - "Pattern inference: high probability"
    example: "Directory structure + queue usage → worker component"

  unknown:
    definition: "Claim cannot be supported by evidence"
    confidence_range: "0.0 - 0.4"
    derivation:
      - "Signal strength: none"
      - "Signal count: 0"
      - "Pattern inference: low probability"
    example: "Missing documentation → unknown goal"

  confidence_formula:
    confidence = (
      (signal_strength * 0.4) +
      (signal_count_normalized * 0.3) +
      (consistency_score * 0.2) +
      (pattern_probability * 0.1)
    )

  where:
    signal_strength:
      strong: 1.0
      moderate: 0.6
      weak: 0.3

    signal_count_normalized:
      count >= 3: 1.0
      count = 2: 0.7
      count = 1: 0.4
      count = 0: 0.0

    consistency_score:
      no_conflicts: 1.0
      minor_conflicts: 0.7
      major_conflicts: 0.3

    pattern_probability:
      high: 1.0
      medium: 0.6
      low: 0.3

title: 26  Blueprint Extraction Specification v0.1
text:
26.4 Quality Standards

26.4.1 Blueprint Quality Metrics
Metric definitions:
quality_metrics:
  coverage:
    definition: "Proportion of kernel slots populated"
    calculation: "populated_slots / required_slots"
    threshold: "> 0.8"
    weight: 0.25

  correctness:
    definition: "Proportion of correctly extracted elements"
    calculation: "correct_elements / audited_elements"
    threshold: "> 0.8"
    weight: 0.30
    method: "Sampling-based audit"

  consistency:
    definition: "Internal coherence of blueprint"
    calculation: "triangular_consistency_score"
    threshold: "> 0.85"
    weight: 0.20
    checks:
      - capability_interface_consistency
      - capability_workflow_consistency
      - component_interface_consistency

  evidence_ratio:
    definition: "Proportion of elements with traceable evidence"
    calculation: "elements_with_evidence / total_elements"
    threshold: "> 0.8"
    weight: 0.25

  overall_quality:
    calculation: "weighted_sum of metrics"
    threshold: "> 0.8"
    formula:
      quality_score = (
        (coverage * 0.25) +
        (correctness * 0.30) +
        (consistency * 0.20) +
        (evidence_ratio * 0.25)
      )


26.4.2 Extraction Maturity Levels (EML)
Level definitions:
extraction_maturity_levels:
  EML_0:
    name: "No Structured Extraction"
    description: "No blueprint representation exists"
    requirements: []
    usage: "None"
    confidence: N/A

  EML_1:
    name: "Partial Signals Detected"
    description: "Signals detected but not organized"
    requirements:
      - "Signal inventory exists"
      - "At least 3 signal types identified"
    usage: "Internal analysis only"
    confidence: "Variable (0.3-0.7)"

  EML_2:
    name: "Kernel Partially Reconstructed"
    description: "Parts of kernel populated"
    requirements:
      - "At least 4 kernel slots populated"
      - "Evidence ratio > 0.5"
      - "Partial validation passed"
    usage: "Draft analysis"
    confidence: "Medium (0.5-0.7)"

  EML_3:
    name: "Evidence-Backed Blueprint Draft"
    description: "Complete kernel with evidence support"
    requirements:
      - "All 6 kernel slots populated"
      - "Evidence ratio > 0.8"
      - "Validation passed"
      - "Confidence > 0.7"
    usage: "Analysis and comparison"
    confidence: "High (0.7-0.9)"

  EML_4:
    name: "Reviewed and Validated Blueprint"
    description: "Fully reviewed and verified"
    requirements:
      - "All 6 kernel slots populated"
      - "Evidence ratio > 0.9"
      - "Validation passed"
      - "Human review completed"
      - "ADR documentation complete"
      - "Confidence > 0.85"
    usage: "Pattern libraries, canonical examples"
    confidence: "Very high (> 0.85)"


26.4.3 Dataset Quality Targets
Target specifications:
dataset_quality_targets:
  production_dataset:
    observed_percentage: "> 60%"
    average_confidence: "> 0.75"
    evidence_ratio: "> 0.85"
    average_coverage: "> 0.8"
    average_correctness: "> 0.8"
    average_consistency: "> 0.85"
    eml_distribution:
      EML_3: ">= 70%"
      EML_4: ">= 20%"
      EML_0-2: "< 10%"

  high_quality_dataset:
    observed_percentage: "> 70%"
    average_confidence: "> 0.85"
    evidence_ratio: "> 0.95"
    average_coverage: "> 0.9"
    average_correctness: "> 0.9"
    average_consistency: "> 0.9"
    eml_distribution:
      EML_3: ">= 40%"
      EML_4: ">= 60%"
      EML_0-2: "< 0%"

  acceptable_dataset:
    observed_percentage: "> 50%"
    average_confidence: "> 0.65"
    evidence_ratio: "> 0.75"
    average_coverage: "> 0.7"
    average_correctness: "> 0.7"
    average_consistency: "> 0.75"
    eml_distribution:
      EML_2: ">= 30%"
      EML_3: ">= 60%"
      EML_4: ">= 10%"
      EML_0-1: "< 0%"

title: 26  Blueprint Extraction Specification v0.1
text:
26.5 Operational Procedures

26.5.1 Repository Filtering
Filter criteria:
repository_filtering:
  quality_filters:
    min_activity:
      criterion: "Last commit within 18 months"
      field: "last_commit_date"
      threshold: "< 18 months ago"

    min_adoption:
      criterion: "Minimum stars or forks"
      field: "stars, forks"
      threshold: "stars >= 50 OR forks >= 10"

  type_filters:
    exclude_templates:
      pattern: "repository name contains 'template' or 'starter'"

    exclude_examples:
      pattern: "repository name contains 'example' or 'demo'"

    exclude_tests:
      pattern: "repository name contains 'test' or 'testing'"

  size_filters:
    min_size:
      criterion: "Minimum code complexity"
      field: "non_vendor_files"
      threshold: ">= 10 files"

    max_size:
      criterion: "Maximum repository size"
      field: "total_files"
      threshold: "< 10,000 files"

  language_filters:
    supported_languages:
      - python
      - go
      - java
      - javascript
      - typescript
      - rust
      - csharp

  license_filters:
    allow_licenses:
      - MIT
      - Apache-2.0
      - BSD-3-Clause
      - GPL-3.0
      - LGPL-3.0

  dependency_filters:
    require_build_files:
      required_files:
        - "requirements.txt"
        - "package.json"
        - "go.mod"
        - "pom.xml"
        - "build.gradle"


26.5.2 Two-Pass Extraction
Pass specifications:
two_pass_extraction:
  cheap_pass:
    objective: "Lightweight feasibility assessment"
    duration: "Seconds to minutes"
    resources:
      cpu: "1 core"
      memory: "2 GB"
      timeout: "5 minutes"

    analysis:
      - directory_structure_analysis
      - entrypoint_identification
      - dependency_inference
      - basic_config_parsing

    output:
      - repository_profile
      - extraction_feasibility_score
      - resource_estimate

    pass_criteria:
      feasibility_score: ">= 0.6"
      expected_coverage: "> 0.5"
      expected_time: "< 60 minutes"

  deep_pass:
    objective: "Full blueprint extraction"
    duration: "Minutes to hours"
    resources:
      cpu: "4 cores"
      memory: "8 GB"
      timeout: "60 minutes"

    analysis:
      - complete_signal_detection
      - comprehensive_evidence_collection
      - full_ontology_mapping
      - complete_kernel_construction
      - view_generation
      - validation

    output:
      - blueprint_pack
      - extraction_report
      - quality_metrics

    success_criteria:
      validation: "PASSED"
      eml_level: ">= EML_3"
      quality_score: "> 0.8"


26.5.3 Incremental Extraction
Update process:
incremental_extraction:
  change_detection:
    action: "Analyze repository changes"
    input: "Last extraction commit"
    output: "Changed files and patterns"

  impact_analysis:
    action: "Determine affected architectural elements"
    input: "Changed files"
    output: "Affected slots and elements"

  selective_extraction:
    action: "Re-run affected extraction stages"
    input: "Affected elements"
    output: "Updated blueprint elements"

  stages_to_re_run:
    structural_changes:
      - component_discovery
      - kernel_construction

    interface_changes:
      - interface_extraction
      - kernel_construction

    data_changes:
      - data_model_extraction
      - kernel_construction

    workflow_changes:
      - workflow_extraction
      - kernel_construction

  validation:
    action: "Validate updated blueprint"
    input: "Updated blueprint"
    output: "Validation results"

  efficiency:
    time_reduction: "70-90%"
    cost_reduction: "80-95%"


26.5.4 Normalization and Deduplication
Processing steps:
normalization_deduplication:
  family_identification:
    similarity_threshold: "0.6"
    clustering_method: "Hierarchical clustering"
    min_cluster_size: "3"

  normalization:
    technology_abstraction: "Map specific to generic"
    naming_normalization: "Map domain to canonical"
    capability_normalization: "Map specific to generic"
    structural_normalization: "Mark optional variations"

  duplicate_detection:
    exact_duplicates: "100% match"
    near_duplicates: "> 95% similarity"
    architectural_clones: "> 90% normalized similarity"

  canonical_generation:
    template_confidence: "> 0.85"
    variation_coverage: "> 0.8"
    family_size: ">= 3"

  quality_metrics:
    family_coverage: "> 0.7"
    template_confidence: "> 0.85"
    duplicate_rate: "< 0.1"
    variation_coverage: "> 0.8"

title: 26  Blueprint Extraction Specification v0.1
text:
26.6 Specification Versioning
Version control:
specification_versioning:
  current_version: "v0.1"
  version_date: "2026-03-08"

  version_scheme: "Semantic versioning"
  - MAJOR: Breaking changes
  - MINOR: New features, backward compatible
  - PATCH: Bug fixes, backward compatible

  changelog:
    v0.1:
      - "Initial specification"
      - "Core methodology defined"
      - "Data structures specified"
      - "Quality standards established"
      - "Operational procedures defined"

  future_roadmap:
    v0.2:
      - "Enhanced pattern discovery"
      - "Multi-language support"
      - "Advanced visualization"

    v1.0:
      - "Production-ready specification"
      - "Complete tooling support"
      - "Industry validation"

  backward_compatibility:
    guarantee: "Backward compatible within MINOR versions"
    migration_path: "Migration guides provided for MAJOR versions"

title: 26  Blueprint Extraction Specification v0.1
text:
26.7 Chapter Summary

Blueprint Extraction Specification v0.1 provides formal definition for extraction framework
Terminology defines core concepts: blueprint, kernel, view, signal, evidence, confidence, ontology mapping, blueprint pack, EML
Data structures include Kernel schema (6 slots with specific fields), Evidence object schema, Blueprint Pack structure
Extraction methodology defines 8-stage pipeline: signal detection → hypothesis formation → evidence collection → ontology mapping → kernel construction → view generation → validation → review
Quality gates enforce thresholds: signal detection completeness >0.8, evidence ratio >0.8, kernel completeness >0.8, validation PASSED, EML >=3
Confidence calculation derives from signal strength (40%), signal count (30%), consistency (20%), pattern probability (10%)
Quality metrics include coverage (>0.8), correctness (>0.8), consistency (>0.85), evidence ratio (>0.8)
Extraction Maturity Levels (EML 0-4): No extraction → Partial signals → Partial kernel → Evidence-backed draft → Reviewed and validated
Dataset quality targets define production, high-quality, and acceptable dataset standards
Operational procedures include repository filtering (quality, type, size, language, license, dependency), two-pass extraction (cheap pass + deep pass), incremental extraction (change detection → impact analysis → selective extraction)
Normalization and deduplication include family identification, normalization, duplicate detection, canonical generation
Specification uses semantic versioning (v0.1 initial), with roadmap for v0.2 and v1.0
This specification serves as authoritative reference for implementing extraction pipelines and tools

End of Blueprint Extraction Volume 5
This specification concludes the comprehensive presentation of the Blueprint Extraction framework. The methodology defined in this volume provides a systematic, evidence-driven approach to reconstructing system architectures from repositories. From signal detection through pattern discovery, the framework enables scalable extraction, rigorous validation, and systematic analysis of architectural knowledge.

title: 27  Appendix A — Signal Taxonomy v0.1
text:
27.1 Signal Object Structure
The Signal Taxonomy defines the standardized categories of repository artifacts that may indicate the presence of architectural structure. Signals are the earliest observable layer in the blueprint extraction pipeline.
Signals do not directly define architectural entities. Instead, they represent structural leakage points where implementation artifacts reveal hints about system architecture.
Signal classification allows extraction pipelines to operate consistently across repositories and technology stacks. Each signal category represents a class of artifacts that frequently encode architectural meaning.
Signal Taxonomy v0.1 defines the baseline categories required for blueprint extraction.
Each detected signal should be represented using a minimal structured schema.
Signals are later transformed into evidence objects during the hypothesis and evidence aggregation stages.
crumbs: 27  Appendix A — Signal Taxonomy v0.1

title: 27  Appendix A — Signal Taxonomy v0.1
crumbs: 27  Appendix A — Signal Taxonomy v0.1

title: 27  Appendix A — Signal Taxonomy v0.1
text:
27.2 Repository Structure Signals
Repository structure often reveals implicit architectural segmentation.
Typical artifacts include:

directory hierarchies
module boundaries
service directories
plugin folders
pipeline folders

Examples of structural signals:
services/
workers/
pipelines/
plugins/
Architectural implications may include:

potential component boundaries
subsystem organization
modular structure

Structure signals are typically weak signals and require additional evidence before architectural interpretation.
crumbs: 27  Appendix A — Signal Taxonomy v0.1

title: 27  Appendix A — Signal Taxonomy v0.1
text:
27.3 Dependency Signals
Dependency manifests reveal the libraries and infrastructure systems used by a project.
Examples include:

package.json
requirements.txt
go.mod
Cargo.toml
pom.xml

Dependencies often reveal architectural capabilities.
Examples:

web frameworks → API interfaces
queue libraries → asynchronous workers
workflow frameworks → orchestration systems
database drivers → persistent storage

Dependency signals help infer system capabilities and technology roles.
crumbs: 27  Appendix A — Signal Taxonomy v0.1

title: 27  Appendix A — Signal Taxonomy v0.1
text:
27.4 Entrypoint Signals
Entrypoints reveal where system execution begins.
Typical entrypoint artifacts include:

application startup files
server initialization scripts
CLI bootstrap files
worker initialization commands

Examples:
main.py
server.ts
cmd/root.go
bin/start.sh
Entrypoints often indicate:

service components
CLI interfaces
worker processes

Entrypoint signals are often strong indicators of component roles.
crumbs: 27  Appendix A — Signal Taxonomy v0.1

title: 27  Appendix A — Signal Taxonomy v0.1
text:
27.5 Configuration Signals
Configuration files frequently encode structural relationships within the system.
Examples include:

environment configuration
service configuration
routing configuration
integration endpoints

Typical artifacts:
.env
config.yaml
application.yml
settings.py
Configuration signals often reveal:

service dependencies
external integrations
runtime parameters
resource bindings

These signals provide important clues about runtime architecture.
crumbs: 27  Appendix A — Signal Taxonomy v0.1

title: 27  Appendix A — Signal Taxonomy v0.1
text:
27.6 Deployment Signals
Deployment artifacts describe how the system is packaged and executed in production environments.
Examples include:

container configuration
infrastructure definitions
orchestration manifests
deployment pipelines

Typical files:
Dockerfile
docker-compose.yml
k8s/*.yaml
terraform/*.tf
Deployment signals reveal:

deployable units
runtime topology
service boundaries
infrastructure dependencies

These signals contribute strongly to the operations view of the blueprint.
crumbs: 27  Appendix A — Signal Taxonomy v0.1

title: 27  Appendix A — Signal Taxonomy v0.1
text:
27.7 Documentation Signals
Documentation artifacts may explicitly describe system behavior or architecture.
Examples include:

README files
architecture documents
API specifications
design notes

Documentation signals may provide:

system goals
capability descriptions
interface documentation
architecture diagrams

However, documentation must be treated cautiously because it may become outdated relative to the codebase.
crumbs: 27  Appendix A — Signal Taxonomy v0.1

title: 27  Appendix A — Signal Taxonomy v0.1
text:
27.8 Test Signals
Tests encode assumptions about system behavior.
Examples include:

integration tests
API tests
workflow execution tests
component interaction tests

Artifacts may include:
tests/
spec/
integration/
Test signals often reveal:

interface contracts
expected workflows
system boundaries

Tests can also help validate whether extracted interfaces correspond to real behavior.
crumbs: 27  Appendix A — Signal Taxonomy v0.1

title: 27  Appendix A — Signal Taxonomy v0.1
text:
27.9 Observability Signals
Observability instrumentation reveals operational monitoring mechanisms.
Typical signals include:

logging frameworks
metrics exporters
tracing libraries
alert configuration

Examples:
prometheus.yml
otel config
logging middleware
Observability signals indicate:

monitoring boundaries
operational health indicators
runtime instrumentation

These signals contribute to the operations view of the blueprint.
crumbs: 27  Appendix A — Signal Taxonomy v0.1

title: 27  Appendix A — Signal Taxonomy v0.1
text:
27.10 Security Signals
Security mechanisms often appear through middleware, libraries, or configuration.
Examples include:

authentication middleware
authorization policies
encryption libraries
secret management integration

Typical artifacts:
auth middleware
jwt validation
iam policies
vault integration
Security signals reveal:

trust boundaries
access control mechanisms
identity integration

These signals support the security/trust view of the blueprint.
crumbs: 27  Appendix A — Signal Taxonomy v0.1

title: 27  Appendix A — Signal Taxonomy v0.1
text:
27.11 Issue and Pull Request Signals (Optional)
Repository collaboration artifacts may contain architectural information.
Examples include:

issue discussions
pull request descriptions
commit messages

These artifacts may reference:

architecture refactors
new components
interface changes
infrastructure modifications

Because these signals are unstructured and inconsistent, they are treated as optional enrichment signals rather than primary sources.
crumbs: 27  Appendix A — Signal Taxonomy v0.1

title: 27  Appendix A — Signal Taxonomy v0.1
text:
27.12 Signal Categories Summary
Signal Taxonomy v0.1 defines the following categories:
structure
dependency
entrypoint
config
deploy
docs
test
observability
security
issue-pr (optional)
Each category captures a different way in which architectural structure may appear within repository artifacts.
Together, these signals form the foundation layer of blueprint extraction, providing the raw observations that initiate hypothesis formation and evidence collection.
crumbs: 27  Appendix A — Signal Taxonomy v0.1

title: 28  Appendix B — Evidence Object Schema v0.1
text:
28.1 Evidence Object Overview
The Evidence Object Schema defines the canonical structure used to represent traceable architectural evidence during blueprint extraction.
Evidence objects are the foundation of the extraction discipline.
Every architectural element included in a blueprint must be supported by one or more evidence objects that reference concrete repository artifacts.
The purpose of the evidence schema is to ensure that architectural interpretations remain:
Evidence objects form the bridge between raw repository artifacts and structured architectural entities.
An evidence object represents a specific fragment of a repository artifact that supports a hypothesis about system structure.
Evidence objects are attached to architectural entities such as:
Multiple evidence objects may support a single architectural element.
Evidence objects may also contradict one another, in which case both are preserved for review.
crumbs: 28  Appendix B — Evidence Object Schema v0.1

title: 28  Appendix B — Evidence Object Schema v0.1
text:
components
interfaces
workflows
data entities
capabilities
constraints
crumbs: 28  Appendix B — Evidence Object Schema v0.1

title: 28  Appendix B — Evidence Object Schema v0.1
crumbs: 28  Appendix B — Evidence Object Schema v0.1

title: 28  Appendix B — Evidence Object Schema v0.1
text:
28.3 Field Semantics

28.3.1 id
A unique identifier for the evidence object.
Evidence identifiers should remain stable within a blueprint pack to support traceability and cross-referencing.



28.3.2 source
The source field identifies the repository artifact from which the evidence originates.
It includes:

repository identifier
file path
revision reference

This information ensures that reviewers can locate the exact artifact used during extraction.



28.3.3 location
The location field specifies the exact position within the artifact.
Depending on the artifact type, this may include:

line ranges in source code
JSON paths in configuration files
object paths in structured documents
table or schema identifiers in migration files

Precise location references enable automated tools to retrieve the same evidence fragment during audits.



28.3.4 excerpt
The excerpt field contains the relevant fragment of the artifact.
Providing the excerpt improves review efficiency because reviewers can immediately inspect the relevant content without navigating the entire file.
Excerpts should remain minimal and focused on the architectural implication.



28.3.5 artifact_type
This field classifies the artifact that produced the evidence.
Typical artifact types include:
code
config
schema
deploy
doc
test
Artifact classification helps extraction systems evaluate the reliability of the evidence.
For example, schema definitions often provide stronger architectural signals than documentation.



28.3.6 signal_category
This field records the signal taxonomy category that produced the evidence.
Signal categories allow the extraction pipeline to trace architectural interpretations back to their originating signal class.
Examples include:
structure
dependency
entrypoint
config
deploy
docs
test
observability
security



28.3.7 strength
Evidence strength estimates how directly the artifact supports an architectural interpretation.
Strength levels are defined as:
strong
Artifacts that explicitly encode architectural structure.
Examples:

schema migrations
route definitions
workflow DAG specifications

moderate
Artifacts that strongly suggest architecture but do not explicitly define it.
Examples:

dependency declarations
scheduler configuration
deployment manifests

weak
Artifacts that provide indirect hints about system structure.
Examples:

directory names
naming conventions
documentation references

Strength classification contributes to confidence evaluation during extraction.



28.3.8 created_by
The created_by section records metadata about the extraction process that generated the evidence.
It typically includes:

the extraction tool identifier
extractor version
timestamp

This metadata ensures that evidence objects remain traceable to the pipeline that produced them.
crumbs: 28  Appendix B — Evidence Object Schema v0.1

title: 28  Appendix B — Evidence Object Schema v0.1
text:
28.4 Evidence Aggregation
Multiple evidence objects may support a single architectural entity.
For example, a worker component may be supported by:

a queue dependency declaration
a worker directory structure
a background task initialization script
a deployment configuration referencing worker processes

These evidence objects form an evidence set associated with the entity.
Evidence aggregation increases confidence and improves auditability.
crumbs: 28  Appendix B — Evidence Object Schema v0.1

title: 28  Appendix B — Evidence Object Schema v0.1
text:
28.5 Evidence Conflict Preservation
Extraction pipelines must preserve conflicting evidence.
Conflicts may arise from:

outdated documentation
legacy configuration files
partially removed components
inconsistent naming conventions

Rather than resolving conflicts automatically, the system records all evidence and defers resolution to later validation or review stages.
This preserves transparency and prevents silent misinterpretation.
crumbs: 28  Appendix B — Evidence Object Schema v0.1

title: 28  Appendix B — Evidence Object Schema v0.1
text:
28.6 Evidence Bundle
Evidence objects are packaged into an evidence bundle within the blueprint pack.
The bundle contains:

all evidence objects produced during extraction
indexes linking evidence to architectural entities
references to original repository artifacts

This bundle forms the audit trail for the blueprint.
Reviewers can trace every architectural element in the blueprint back to the evidence objects that justify it.
crumbs: 28  Appendix B — Evidence Object Schema v0.1

title: 28  Appendix B — Evidence Object Schema v0.1
text:
28.7 Schema Summary
The Evidence Object Schema v0.1 establishes a standardized representation for architectural evidence.
It ensures that:

every architectural claim remains traceable
extraction outputs remain auditable
evidence can be reviewed independently of the blueprint structure

By formalizing evidence representation, the schema transforms blueprint extraction from heuristic inference into a verifiable architecture reconstruction process.
crumbs: 28  Appendix B — Evidence Object Schema v0.1

title: 29  Appendix C — Confidence Semantics Spec v0.1
text:
29.1 Confidence Object Structure
Blueprint extraction reconstructs architectural structure from indirect repository artifacts. Because these artifacts rarely encode architecture explicitly, the extraction process inevitably involves interpretation under uncertainty.
The Confidence Semantics Specification defines how uncertainty is represented within extracted blueprints. Its purpose is to ensure that architectural interpretations remain transparent, traceable, and reviewable.
Rather than presenting extracted architecture as definitive truth, every extracted entity, relation, or field must carry an explicit confidence classification.
Confidence semantics therefore serve three functions:
Confidence annotations are attached to architectural entities and fields.
The minimal confidence representation is defined as follows:
This structure records both the qualitative classification of certainty and the evidence supporting that classification.
The score field is optional and may be used by extraction pipelines that compute quantitative confidence estimates.
crumbs: 29  Appendix C — Confidence Semantics Spec v0.1

title: 29  Appendix C — Confidence Semantics Spec v0.1
crumbs: 29  Appendix C — Confidence Semantics Spec v0.1

title: 29  Appendix C — Confidence Semantics Spec v0.1
text:
29.2 Confidence States
Confidence Semantics v0.1 defines three canonical states.
observed
inferred
unknown
These states represent increasing levels of uncertainty.


29.2.1 Observed
An element is classified as observed when its existence is directly supported by explicit repository artifacts.
Examples include:

HTTP route definitions defining API interfaces
schema migration files defining data entities
workflow DAG definitions specifying task dependencies
command declarations defining CLI interfaces

Observed elements rely on strong evidence and require minimal interpretation.
Such elements generally have the highest confidence level.



29.2.2 Inferred
An element is classified as inferred when it is derived from indirect signals rather than explicit artifacts.
Examples include:

a worker component inferred from queue consumers and scheduling configuration
a capability inferred from multiple related API endpoints
a workflow inferred from scheduling signals and task execution scripts

Inferred elements are plausible interpretations supported by evidence but not directly declared in repository artifacts.
They therefore carry moderate confidence and may require review.



29.2.3 Unknown
An element is classified as unknown when insufficient evidence exists to determine its presence or structure.
Unknown values appear when:

repository artifacts do not provide enough information
signals are too weak or ambiguous
architectural elements exist outside the repository (for example, operational infrastructure)

Instead of forcing speculative interpretations, the extraction framework preserves these gaps explicitly.
Unknown values maintain the integrity of the blueprint by preventing unsupported assumptions.
crumbs: 29  Appendix C — Confidence Semantics Spec v0.1

title: 29  Appendix C — Confidence Semantics Spec v0.1
text:
29.3 Confidence Derivation
Confidence classifications are determined by analyzing the evidence associated with an architectural element.
Several factors influence confidence.
Evidence strength
Artifacts that explicitly encode architecture (for example, schemas or route definitions) contribute stronger evidence than indirect indicators such as naming conventions.
Evidence quantity
Multiple independent evidence objects supporting the same interpretation increase confidence.
Evidence consistency
If evidence sources agree with each other, confidence increases. Conflicting evidence reduces confidence.
Pattern alignment
If extracted structures match well-known architectural patterns, the interpretation may gain additional confidence.
Extraction systems may compute confidence scores using these factors, but the final classification must always map to the defined confidence states.
crumbs: 29  Appendix C — Confidence Semantics Spec v0.1

title: 29  Appendix C — Confidence Semantics Spec v0.1
text:
29.4 Confidence Propagation
Confidence may propagate through relationships within the architecture graph.
For example:

if an interface is strongly observed, the capability it exposes may receive moderate inferred confidence
if a workflow is weakly inferred, related capabilities may also receive reduced confidence

Propagation ensures that uncertainty is reflected consistently across related architectural elements.
However, propagation must not overwrite strong evidence classifications.
Explicit observations always take precedence over propagated inference.
crumbs: 29  Appendix C — Confidence Semantics Spec v0.1

title: 29  Appendix C — Confidence Semantics Spec v0.1
text:
29.5 Confidence and Review Prioritization
Confidence semantics guide the human review process.
Fields classified as observed typically require minimal review because they are directly supported by artifacts.
Fields classified as inferred should receive moderate review attention, especially when they affect critical architectural slots such as components or interfaces.
Fields classified as unknown may require review when they correspond to important structural roles or when strong signals suggest missing information.
This prioritization allows large-scale extraction pipelines to allocate human attention efficiently.
crumbs: 29  Appendix C — Confidence Semantics Spec v0.1

title: 29  Appendix C — Confidence Semantics Spec v0.1
text:
29.6 Dataset-Level Confidence Metrics
Confidence annotations also support dataset-level quality analysis.
Metrics derived from confidence values include:

proportion of observed elements
proportion of inferred elements
proportion of unknown fields

These metrics help evaluate the maturity of blueprint datasets and identify areas where extraction methods require improvement.
crumbs: 29  Appendix C — Confidence Semantics Spec v0.1

title: 29  Appendix C — Confidence Semantics Spec v0.1
text:
29.7 Spec Summary
Confidence Semantics Spec v0.1 establishes a standardized framework for representing uncertainty in blueprint extraction.
It defines:

a structured confidence object
three canonical confidence states
rules for deriving confidence from evidence
propagation principles across architectural relationships
review prioritization based on confidence levels

By making uncertainty explicit, the confidence semantics framework ensures that extracted architectures remain transparent, auditable, and suitable for large-scale architectural analysis.
crumbs: 29  Appendix C — Confidence Semantics Spec v0.1

title: 30  Appendix D — Ontology Mapping Rules Template
text:
30.1 Mapping Rule Structure
Blueprint extraction reconstructs architecture by translating repository artifacts into system ontology entities and relationships. This translation is governed by mapping rules.
The Ontology Mapping Rules Template defines a structured format for specifying how signals and evidence are converted into architectural entities. The goal of the template is to ensure that mapping logic remains:
Mapping rules act as the interface between repository evidence and the system ontology.
Each mapping rule defines how a particular class of repository artifacts should be interpreted as architectural entities or relationships.
A canonical mapping rule is represented as:
This template allows extraction systems to apply explicit and reproducible transformations from repository artifacts to ontology entities.
crumbs: 30  Appendix D — Ontology Mapping Rules Template

title: 30  Appendix D — Ontology Mapping Rules Template
crumbs: 30  Appendix D — Ontology Mapping Rules Template

title: 30  Appendix D — Ontology Mapping Rules Template
text:
30.2 Rule Components

30.2.1 Rule Identifier
Each mapping rule must have a unique identifier.
id: map.interface.http_routes
Rule identifiers allow extraction pipelines and reviewers to reference the specific logic responsible for producing architectural elements.



30.2.2 Description
The description field provides a human-readable explanation of the rule’s purpose.
Example:
description: |
  Maps HTTP route definitions in web frameworks to Interface entities.
Clear descriptions improve auditability and maintainability of the extraction system.



30.2.3 Input Conditions
The input section defines the signals and artifact patterns that trigger the rule.
input:
  signal_category: entrypoint
  artifact_type: code
  pattern: http_route_definition
Inputs may include:

signal category
artifact type
file patterns
framework-specific constructs
structural patterns in configuration files

These conditions define when the rule should be applied.



30.2.4 Evidence Requirements
Mapping rules must define the minimum evidence required before creating an architectural entity.
evidence_requirements:
  min_evidence: 1
  acceptable_strength:
    - strong
    - moderate
These requirements ensure that architectural entities are not created without sufficient support.
Multiple evidence objects may be aggregated before the rule fires.



30.2.5 Output Entity Definition
The output section defines the ontology entity created by the mapping rule.
output:
  entity:
    type: Interface
    subtype: HTTP_API
The entity type must correspond to one of the ontology categories defined in the System Ontology.
Subtype definitions allow more specific classification without expanding the ontology core.



30.2.6 Relation Generation
Mapping rules may generate relationships between entities.
Example:
relation_generation:
  create_relations:
    - relation: exposed_by
      target: Component
This allows the extraction pipeline to automatically construct the relation graph alongside entity creation.
Common relations include:

exposes
consumes
reads
writes
triggers
depends_on

Relations ensure that extracted entities form a connected architectural graph rather than isolated elements.



30.2.7 Confidence Rules
Mapping rules also define how confidence should be assigned.
confidence_rule:
  observed_if:
    evidence_strength: strong
  inferred_if:
    evidence_strength: moderate
Confidence classification ensures that the resulting blueprint reflects the reliability of the underlying evidence.
Rules may also incorporate additional heuristics, such as evidence count or pattern alignment.
crumbs: 30  Appendix D — Ontology Mapping Rules Template

title: 30  Appendix D — Ontology Mapping Rules Template
text:
30.3 Example Mapping Rule
Below is a simplified example rule for extracting API interfaces.
mapping_rule:
  id: map.api.routes

  description: |
    Detects HTTP route definitions and maps them to API interface entities.

  input:
    signal_category: entrypoint
    artifact_type: code
    pattern: http_route

  evidence_requirements:
    min_evidence: 1
    acceptable_strength:
      - strong

  output:
    entity:
      type: Interface
      subtype: HTTP_API

  relation_generation:
    create_relations:
      - relation: exposed_by
        target: Component

  confidence_rule:
    observed_if:
      evidence_strength: strong
This rule allows the extraction pipeline to consistently convert route definitions into interface entities.
crumbs: 30  Appendix D — Ontology Mapping Rules Template

title: 30  Appendix D — Ontology Mapping Rules Template
text:
30.4 Rule Library Organization
Mapping rules are typically organized into a rule library.
The rule library may include rule groups such as:
component_rules/
interface_rules/
data_rules/
workflow_rules/
capability_rules/
constraint_rules/
Grouping rules by architectural entity type simplifies maintenance and enables incremental expansion of the extraction framework.
crumbs: 30  Appendix D — Ontology Mapping Rules Template

title: 30  Appendix D — Ontology Mapping Rules Template
text:
30.5 Deterministic Mapping Principle
Whenever possible, mapping rules should produce deterministic outputs.
Given the same repository artifacts and rule set, the extraction pipeline should produce the same architectural entities.
Deterministic mapping ensures that blueprint packs remain stable across repeated extraction runs and that architectural changes are visible through version control diffs.
crumbs: 30  Appendix D — Ontology Mapping Rules Template

title: 30  Appendix D — Ontology Mapping Rules Template
text:
30.6 Template Purpose
The Ontology Mapping Rules Template provides a formal interface between repository evidence and system architecture representation.
It ensures that:

extraction logic remains interpretable
architectural entities are created through explicit rules
evidence requirements are enforced
confidence classifications are consistently applied

By standardizing mapping rules, the template enables blueprint extraction pipelines to remain transparent, extensible, and consistent across repositories and ecosystems.
crumbs: 30  Appendix D — Ontology Mapping Rules Template

title: 31  Appendix E — Extraction Report Template
text:
31.1 Report Structure Overview
The Extraction Report is a required artifact within every Blueprint Pack.
Its purpose is to summarize the outcome of the extraction pipeline and provide a transparent diagnostic overview of the reconstructed architecture.
While the blueprint itself represents the architectural structure, the extraction report records how the blueprint was produced and where uncertainty remains.
The report serves three primary purposes:
Every extraction run must generate a corresponding extraction report.
An extraction report is represented as a structured document with the following sections:
Each section captures a different aspect of the extraction outcome.
crumbs: 31  Appendix E — Extraction Report Template

title: 31  Appendix E — Extraction Report Template
text:
extraction_report:
  metadata:
  summary:
  coverage:
  confidence_summary:
  gaps:
  conflicts:
  anomalies:
  extraction_metrics:
  recommendations:
crumbs: 31  Appendix E — Extraction Report Template

title: 31  Appendix E — Extraction Report Template
crumbs: 31  Appendix E — Extraction Report Template

title: 31  Appendix E — Extraction Report Template
crumbs: 31  Appendix E — Extraction Report Template

title: 31  Appendix E — Extraction Report Template
crumbs: 31  Appendix E — Extraction Report Template

title: 31  Appendix E — Extraction Report Template
crumbs: 31  Appendix E — Extraction Report Template

title: 31  Appendix E — Extraction Report Template
text:
31.6 Gaps
The gaps section lists architectural elements that could not be reconstructed.
gaps:
  - slot: Data
    description: "No persistent storage signals detected"
  - slot: Workflow
    description: "Task orchestration signals absent"
Gaps are expected in some repositories, particularly when architecture elements exist outside the codebase.
Recording gaps explicitly prevents extraction systems from silently inventing structures.
crumbs: 31  Appendix E — Extraction Report Template

title: 31  Appendix E — Extraction Report Template
text:
31.7 Conflicts
The conflicts section records cases where evidence sources provide contradictory information.
conflicts:
  - entity: component.worker
    description: "Worker referenced in documentation but not found in code"
  - entity: interface.api
    description: "Route definitions conflict with OpenAPI specification"
Conflicts indicate areas where architectural interpretation may require human judgment.
These entries often become high-priority review items.
crumbs: 31  Appendix E — Extraction Report Template

title: 31  Appendix E — Extraction Report Template
text:
31.8 Anomalies
Anomalies represent structural irregularities detected during validation.
Examples include:
anomalies:
  - type: orphan_component
    entity: component.scheduler
    description: "Component has no interfaces or workflows"

  - type: unowned_data
    entity: data.orders
    description: "No component identified as owner"
Anomalies help reviewers identify potential extraction errors or unusual architectural structures.
crumbs: 31  Appendix E — Extraction Report Template

title: 31  Appendix E — Extraction Report Template
crumbs: 31  Appendix E — Extraction Report Template

title: 31  Appendix E — Extraction Report Template
text:
31.10 Recommendations
The recommendations section suggests potential follow-up actions.
recommendations:
  - "Review inferred component boundaries"
  - "Verify API interface definitions"
  - "Investigate missing workflow signals"
Recommendations guide reviewers toward the most important issues discovered during extraction.
crumbs: 31  Appendix E — Extraction Report Template

title: 31  Appendix E — Extraction Report Template
text:
31.11 Role of the Extraction Report
The extraction report functions as the diagnostic layer of blueprint extraction.
While the blueprint captures architectural structure, the report captures:

the reliability of the extraction process
the completeness of the reconstructed architecture
the remaining uncertainties that require review

Together with the blueprint and evidence bundle, the report ensures that blueprint packs remain transparent, auditable artifacts suitable for large-scale architecture mining.
crumbs: 31  Appendix E — Extraction Report Template

title: 32  Appendix F — Five Complete Extraction Cases (with Evidence Trace)
text:
This appendix presents five representative blueprint extraction cases.
Each case demonstrates how repository signals, evidence objects, and mapping rules combine to produce a structured blueprint kernel.
The examples are intentionally simplified but preserve the full extraction logic:
signal → evidence → mapping → kernel slot → blueprint element
Each case includes an evidence trace, showing how architectural conclusions are justified.


33 Case 1 — CLI Tool System

33.0.1 Repository Signals
cmd/
  root.go
  run.go
internal/
  engine/
  config/
Dependency signals:
cobra CLI framework
Entrypoint:
cmd/root.go



33.0.2 Evidence Trace
evidence:
  id: ev_cli_001
  source:
    file: cmd/root.go
  location:
    type: line-range
    value: 12-55
  excerpt: |
    var rootCmd = &cobra.Command{
        Use: "vibe",
        Short: "CLI tool"
    }
  artifact_type: code
  signal_category: entrypoint
  strength: strong



33.0.3 Mapping
Mapping rule applied:
map.cli.command → Interface(type=CLI)
Generated entity:
entity:
  id: interface.cli.vibe
  type: Interface
  subtype: CLI
  confidence: observed



33.0.4 Kernel Slot Contribution
Interfaces → CLI command
Components → CLI runtime
Capabilities → tool execution



33.0.5 Resulting Kernel Snapshot
Goal:
  command-line automation

Capabilities:
  run_command
  config_management

Components:
  cli_runtime

Interfaces:
  CLI(vibe)

Data:
  config files

Workflows:
  command execution flow




34 Case 2 — REST API Service

34.0.1 Repository Signals
app/
  controllers/
routes/
models/
Dependency:
express
