title: 33  Appendix G — Extraction Maturity Level (EML) Score Sheet
text:
33.1 EML Overview
The Extraction Maturity Level (EML) score sheet provides a standardized method for evaluating the quality and reliability of blueprint extraction results.
While earlier sections defined EML conceptually, the score sheet operationalizes those levels using measurable criteria. It allows extraction pipelines, reviewers, and dataset maintainers to assess the maturity of individual blueprint packs and monitor the overall quality of blueprint repositories.
The scoring framework evaluates several dimensions of extraction quality, including structural coverage, evidence traceability, confidence distribution, and review status.
Extraction Maturity Levels represent progressively higher levels of blueprint reconstruction quality.
Each level corresponds to specific operational criteria that must be satisfied before the blueprint can be classified at that level.
crumbs: 33  Appendix G — Extraction Maturity Level (EML) Score Sheet

title: 33  Appendix G — Extraction Maturity Level (EML) Score Sheet
text:
EML 0 — No structured extraction
EML 1 — Signal detection only
EML 2 — Partial kernel reconstruction
EML 3 — Evidence-backed blueprint draft
EML 4 — Reviewed reference blueprint
crumbs: 33  Appendix G — Extraction Maturity Level (EML) Score Sheet

title: 33  Appendix G — Extraction Maturity Level (EML) Score Sheet
text:
33.2 Scoring Dimensions
The EML score sheet evaluates blueprint extraction across four primary dimensions:

Kernel coverage
Evidence traceability
Structural consistency
Review status

Each dimension contributes to determining the final maturity level.
crumbs: 33  Appendix G — Extraction Maturity Level (EML) Score Sheet

title: 33  Appendix G — Extraction Maturity Level (EML) Score Sheet
text:
33.3 Kernel Coverage
Kernel coverage evaluates whether the six blueprint kernel slots have been populated with meaningful architectural elements.
Goal
Capabilities
Components
Interfaces
Data
Workflows
Coverage is evaluated as follows:



Coverage Level
Description




None
No kernel elements reconstructed


Partial
Some kernel slots populated


Substantial
Most slots populated with valid entities


Complete
All slots populated or explicitly marked unknown



Coverage indicates how completely the system architecture has been reconstructed.
crumbs: 33  Appendix G — Extraction Maturity Level (EML) Score Sheet

title: 33  Appendix G — Extraction Maturity Level (EML) Score Sheet
text:
33.4 Evidence Traceability
Evidence traceability measures whether architectural elements are supported by traceable evidence objects.



Evidence Ratio
Interpretation




< 30%
Mostly speculative extraction


30–60%
Mixed evidence and inference


60–85%
Strong evidence support


> 85%
Highly traceable architecture



Higher evidence ratios indicate stronger auditability.
Evidence traceability is essential for determining whether the blueprint can be trusted for architectural analysis.
crumbs: 33  Appendix G — Extraction Maturity Level (EML) Score Sheet

title: 33  Appendix G — Extraction Maturity Level (EML) Score Sheet
text:
33.5 Structural Consistency
Structural consistency evaluates whether the extracted blueprint forms a coherent architecture graph.
Checks include:

valid references between entities
capabilities connected to interfaces or workflows
components participating in interactions
data entities owned by components
workflows referencing valid execution units

Consistency levels may be classified as:



Level
Description




Low
Frequent anomalies or broken relationships


Moderate
Minor inconsistencies present


High
Architecture graph internally coherent



Consistency indicates the structural reliability of the blueprint.
crumbs: 33  Appendix G — Extraction Maturity Level (EML) Score Sheet

title: 33  Appendix G — Extraction Maturity Level (EML) Score Sheet
text:
33.6 Review Status
Review status reflects whether the blueprint has undergone human validation.



Status
Meaning




None
No human review performed


Partial
Limited review of critical elements


Complete
Full review according to protocol



Human review significantly increases blueprint reliability, particularly for inferred elements.
crumbs: 33  Appendix G — Extraction Maturity Level (EML) Score Sheet

crumbs: 33  Appendix G — Extraction Maturity Level (EML) Score Sheet

title: 33  Appendix G — Extraction Maturity Level (EML) Score Sheet
text:
33.8 Scoring Template
A blueprint pack may include an EML score entry similar to the following:
eml_score:
  level: EML3
  kernel_coverage: substantial
  evidence_ratio: 0.72
  structural_consistency: high
  review_status: partial
This structured representation allows automated systems to track extraction maturity across blueprint datasets.
crumbs: 33  Appendix G — Extraction Maturity Level (EML) Score Sheet

title: 33  Appendix G — Extraction Maturity Level (EML) Score Sheet
text:
33.9 Dataset-Level Usage
At scale, EML scores provide insight into the overall quality of blueprint mining pipelines.
Examples of dataset-level metrics include:

distribution of blueprints across EML levels
average evidence ratio across repositories
proportion of reviewed blueprints
extraction maturity growth over time

These metrics help pipeline operators identify areas where extraction methods require improvement.
crumbs: 33  Appendix G — Extraction Maturity Level (EML) Score Sheet

title: 33  Appendix G — Extraction Maturity Level (EML) Score Sheet
text:
33.10 Purpose of the EML Framework
The EML score sheet transforms blueprint extraction from a qualitative process into a measurable engineering discipline.
It enables consistent evaluation of blueprint quality, supports large-scale architecture mining pipelines, and ensures that blueprint repositories maintain clear distinctions between preliminary extraction results and verified architectural knowledge.
By standardizing extraction maturity assessment, the EML framework supports the long-term goal of building reliable architecture datasets and architecture intelligence systems.
crumbs: 33  Appendix G — Extraction Maturity Level (EML) Score Sheet

title: 34  Summary
text:
34.1 Key Takeaways
Blueprint Extraction is fundamentally an evidence-driven engineering discipline. This volume has presented a complete methodology for reverse-engineering system blueprints from repositories, with the following core principles:

title: 34  Summary
text:
34.1.1 Evidence Traceability

Every architectural claim must be backed by observable evidence
Evidence objects capture source, location, excerpt, type, and strength
Multi-evidence aggregation and conflict resolution support confidence modeling



34.1.2 Confidence Semantics

Observed: Directly evidenced in repository artifacts
Inferred: Derived through rules, patterns, or heuristics
Unknown: Insufficient evidence to make reliable claims
Confidence drives review prioritization and risk assessment



34.1.3 Human-in-the-Loop Pipeline

Automated extraction handles systematic, pattern-based inferences
Human reviewers focus on high-impact, low-confidence claims
Review gates transform drafts into usable and reference-quality blueprints



34.1.4 Scalability Design

Cheap-pass/deep-pass strategies enable cost control
Incremental extraction supports repository evolution
Normalization and deduplication manage blueprint families at scale

title: 34  Summary
text:
34.2 The Extraction Journey
From repository to blueprint, the pipeline progresses through several stages:

Signal Detection: Identify structural leaks (repo structure, dependencies, entrypoints, configs)
Evidence Collection: Capture traceable evidence with metadata
Hypothesis Generation: Formulate architectural claims from evidence patterns
Ontology Mapping: Map repository artifacts to system entities
Blueprint Draft: Fill kernel slots (Goal, Capabilities, Components, Interfaces, Data, Workflows)
Validation: Check schema compliance, reference integrity, and consistency
Review Protocol: Human review focused on high-risk inferences
Quality Gates: Progress through maturity levels (EML 0-4)

title: 34  Summary
text:
34.3 Blueprint Kernel Structure
The Blueprint Kernel provides a standardized representation with six mandatory slots:

Goal: System purpose and objectives
Capabilities: Functional capabilities the system provides
Components: Architectural components and their boundaries
Interfaces: API, events, and CLI interfaces
Data: Data entities, ownership, and storage
Workflows: Control planes, pipelines, and execution flows

Multiple views (Product, Architecture, Ops, Security) project from this kernel.

title: 34  Summary
text:
34.4 From Blueprints to Patterns
At scale, blueprint mining enables pattern discovery:

Clustering: Group similar blueprints into families
Signature Extraction: Identify stable architectural signatures
Pattern Candidates: Bridge blueprints to reviewed patterns
Catalog Building: Maintain curated pattern libraries
Feedback Loop: Pattern insights refine extraction accuracy

title: 34  Summary
text:
34.5 Maturity Model
The Extraction Maturity Levels (EML) provide a framework for assessing extraction quality:

EML 0: Unstructured documentation or extraction
EML 1: Evidence extraction without aggregation
EML 2: Evidence aggregation with basic mapping
EML 3: Complete extraction with validation
EML 4: Reference-quality blueprints with review gates

title: 34  Summary
text:
34.6 Quality Dimensions
Extraction quality is assessed across multiple dimensions:

Coverage: What fraction of system structure is captured?
Correctness: How accurate are the architectural claims?
Consistency: Are there internal contradictions in the blueprint?
Evidence Ratio: What proportion of fields have traceable evidence?
Auditability: Can reviewers validate extraction decisions?

title: 34  Summary
text:
34.7 Operational Considerations
Large-scale blueprint mining requires operational infrastructure:

Repo Discovery: Identify valuable and diverse repositories
Filtering: Apply criteria for inclusion/exclusion
Cost Control: Balance extraction depth against resource constraints
Incremental Updates: Handle repository evolution efficiently
Deduplication: Identify and manage blueprint families
Pattern Mining: Extract stable architectural patterns

title: 34  Summary
text:
34.8 The Specification
Chapter 21 provides the formal specification (v0.1) covering:

Signal taxonomy definitions
Evidence object schema
Confidence semantics specification
Mapping rules interface
Output requirements (pack, report, evidence bundle)
Review gate requirements

title: 34  Summary
text:
34.9 Appendices
The appendices provide practical artifacts:

Appendix A: Signal Taxonomy v0.1
Appendix B: Evidence Object Schema v0.1
Appendix C: Confidence Semantics Spec v0.1
Appendix D: Ontology Mapping Rules Template
Appendix E: Extraction Report Template
Appendix F: Five complete extraction cases with evidence traces
Appendix G: EML Scorecard

title: 34  Summary
text:
34.10 Looking Forward
This v0.1 specification establishes the foundation for evidence-driven blueprint extraction. Future directions include:

Enhanced signal taxonomy: Expanding coverage for emerging architectural patterns
Improved confidence modeling: Better handling of uncertainty and conflicts
Machine learning integration: Hybrid symbolic/neural approaches
Pattern catalog growth: Building comprehensive architectural pattern libraries
Tool ecosystem: Implementations, integrations, and developer tooling

title: 34  Summary
text:
34.11 The Evidence Mindset in Practice
The most important takeaway from this volume is the evidence mindset:

Don’t ask “What is the architecture?” — ask “What evidence supports this architectural understanding?”

This shift enables:

Testable and refutable architectural claims
Systematic improvement of extraction accuracy
Confidence-based risk assessment
Effective human-AI collaboration

title: 34  Summary
text:
34.12 Closing Thoughts
Blueprint extraction is both art and science. The methodology presented here provides the scientific foundation — systematic, auditable, and scalable processes. The art lies in applying these principles to diverse systems, making sound judgments under uncertainty, and continuously improving through feedback.
We hope this volume serves as a comprehensive guide for practitioners, researchers, and technical leaders working to understand and manage software architecture at scale.

Blueprint Extraction v0.1 — Making architectural understanding explicit, traceable, and auditable.
